Allena finished her pitch with a dramatic flourish."With Aethel's Al and my clinical oversight, we will revolutionize diagnostics."

She looked at Alastair, her eyes wide,waiting for the applause and the contract.

Alastair placed his hands flat on the table. He looked at her with an expression of absolute, freezing boredom.

"Dr. Mills," Alastair said, his voice echoing in the large room. "I have reviewed your proposal."

He paused, letting the silence stretch until it became uncomfortable.
"Frankly," Alastair continued, his tone brutal, "it is a catastrophic embarrassment."

Allena's jaw actually dropped. The color drained completely from her face. She looked around the room,convinced she had misheard him.

Alastair didn't give her time to recover. He pointed at the screen.

"Slide four contains fabricated clinical trial data,"Alastair stated coldly. "Slide nine demonstrates a fundamental, almost comedic misunderstanding of neural network architecture."

Behind the glass, Elisa pressed a button on her internal comms unit. She spoke a single, devastating sentence directly into Alastair's ear.

Alastair listened, his eyes locking onto Allena. He repeated Elisa's words verbatim.

"Your model," Alastair said, his voice dripping with disdain, "is like trying to build a nuclear reactor out of wooden blocks. It is not just stupid. It is dangerous."

The words hit Allena like physical blows. Her face flushed a dark, ugly red. Her hands gripped the
edges of the podium so hard her knuckles turned white.

"Mr. Cromwell," Allena stammered, her voice shrill with panic and anger. "This research is fully backed by the Chambers Group. August Chambers himself-"

"Aethel does not need August Chambers' money,"Alastair cut her off, his voice cracking like a whip."We need competent partners. Not parasites trying to build a career on nepotism and stolen credit."

The word parasite hung in the air. It shattered Allena's carefully constructed facade of professional dignity.

Before Allena could scream a response, the sound of sharp, rhythmic footsteps echoed from the back of the room.

Elisa stepped out from behind the one-way mirror.

She walked slowly into the light, her black suit absorbing the glare of the projector. Her posture was absolute dominance.

Allena turned her head. When she saw Elisa, her eyes bulged.Her brain completely short-circuited.
"You!" Allena shrieked, pointing a shaking finger at Elisa. She looked wildly at Alastair. "What is she doing here?! Security! Get this crazy bitch out of here!"

Nobody moved. The room was dead silent.

Elisa walked past Allena without looking at her. She pulled out the heavy leather chair directly to Alastair's right and sat down. She crossed her legs and folded her hands on the table.

She looked down the length of the table at Allena. A slow, chilling smile spread across Elisa's face.

"Let's introduce ourselves properly, Dr. Mills," Elisa said softly.

Alastair leaned forward. "Dr. Mills, allow me to introduce Aethel's Chief Technology Architect. The sole creator of Project Chiron."

Elisa tilted her head, her black eyes locking onto Allena's terrified face.

"People in the industry," Elisa whispered, the words slicing through the room like a scalpel, "call me Faye."
The name dropped like a bomb.

Allena froze. Her mouth opened and closed, but no sound came out. The absolute, world-ending realization crashed into her. The genius she had been begging for access, the titan she had been trying to impress, was the woman she had mocked in the lobby.

The sheer humiliation was so intense it made Allena physically sway on her feet.

The macchine roared to life, grinding the thick paper into confetti.

"Take your garbage," Elisa commanded, her voice absolute ice. "And get out of my building."

Allena let out a strangled sob. She turned and practically ran out of the boardroom, her cane clattering against the doorframe, leaving Elisa sitting in the quiet hum of the shredder, savoring the absolute destruction.

###Chapter 46

For the record, can you account for these anomalies?"

had never allowed her to get pregnant. He had meticulously controlled her contraception for seven years. If she had given birth, it meant a betrayal so profound it shattered his entire reality.

stress-induced medical anomaly she had studied years ago.

Dr. Roth flipped the page, his eyes scanning the data.

speaking. He looked like a caged animal.

Dr. Roth stared at the cortisol charts. The numbers were astronomically high, consistent with severe trauma.

Pseudocyesis was rare, but medically valid. It perfectly explained the physical markers without a real child.

Dr. Roth slowly nodded, his expression softening into professional sympathy.

she had to interlock her fingers to stop them.

"You really shouldn't have embarrassed yourself,Elisa," Allena cooed. "Some things are just meant for people of a certain... status. You can't force your way into royalty."
Jewel's face turned a violent shade of red. She couldn't take it anymore.

Jewel lunged forward, pointing a shaking finger directly at Allena's face. "You disgusting, parasitic thief! You steal her husband, you steal her life, and you stand here bragging about a necklace? You are trash!"

Devon's eyes narrowed. He stepped in front of Allena, his massive hand shooting out. He shoved Jewel hard in the center of her chest.

"Watch your mouth, you stupid bitch," Devon snarled.

Jewel, wearing high heels, lost her balance. She stumbled backward, her arms flailing, about to crash onto the hard marble floor.

Elisa's arm shot out with blinding speed. She caught Jewel by the waist, stabilizing her instantly.

Elisa slowly turned her head.

The air in the atrium seemed to instantly freeze. The ambient chatter died down.

Elisa stepped past Jewel. She walked directly up to

Devon. She didn't yell. She didn't raise her hands.She simply unleashed the full, terrifying aura of a trauma surgeon who held life and death in her hands every single day.

Her black eyes locked onto Devon's face. The sheer,physiological pressure radiating from her made Devon subconsciously take a half-step backward.

"Mr. Browning," Elisa said. Her voice was a low,vibrating hum that carried perfectly in the sudden silence. Alastair's deep-web intelligence network had never failed her, laying Devon's most fatal, buried vulnerabilities right in the palm of her hand. "Do you really think the SEC won't notice the offshore shell companies you used to short those medical tech stocks last quarter?"

Devon's face went completely, chalk-white. The cognac sloshed in his glass.

His illegal short-selling was buried under layers of encrypted corporate structures. It was a federal crime that would put him in prison for twenty years.There was absolutely no way a nurse should know about that.
Elisa didn't give him a second to breathe. She turned her dead eyes to Allena.

Allena shrank back, her hand clutching the sapphire amulet so hard her knuckles turned white.

"And you," Elisa whispered, her voice slicing through Allena's fragile ego like a scalpel. "That necklace is heavy, isn't it? Enjoy wearing it. Because just like the life you stole, the weight of it is eventually going to snap your pathetic little neck."

Allena gasped, her eyes wide with genuine terror.She looked at Elisa and saw nothing but a black void.

The surrounding crowd was completely paralyzed.The mocking laughter was gone, replaced by a suffocating, terrified silence. They realized they weren't watching a victim; they were looking at a predator.

Elisa turned away from them in disgust.

"Let's go, Jewel," Elisa said calmly. "The smell of rotting garbage is giving me a headache."

Elisa walked through the crowd. The wealthy socialites literally parted like the Red Sea, stepping
back to avoid making eye contact with her.

They walked out of the atrium and onto the outdoor terrace. The freezing night wind hit Elisa's face,clearing the stench of the room from her lungs.

Jewel was rubbing her chest where Devon had shoved her, her eyes still blazing with adrenaline."Elisa, we cant just let them get away with this. We have to destroy them."

Elisa stopped walking. She looked up at the dark,starless sky.

She reached into the inner pocket of her grey suit jacket. Her fingers brushed against the folded copy of the seven-year marriage contract.

Thirty-four hours.

A slow, terrifying smile curved Elisa's lips.

"Don't engage them on their level, Jewel," Elisa said,her voice calm and low. "It's pointless. Go back to your room. Trust me, the game isn't over yet."

Elisa watched Jewel walk toward the guest wing.Then, she turned and walked toward the private VIP elevators, heading back up to the top floor.
She didn't know that a monster was waiting for her in the dark.
###Chapter 57


The private elevator chimed softly as it reached the top floor. The brushed steel doors slid open.

Elisa stepped out into the dimly lit, heavily carpeted hallway of the penthouse level.

Standing exactly three feet from the heavy oak door of her suite was August.

He was leaning against the flocked wallpaper, one hand tucked into his tuxedo pocket, the other holding a lit cigarette. The tip glowed bright orange in the gloom. When he saw her, he dropped the cigarette onto the expensive carpet and crushed it out with the heel of his Italian leather shoe.

He looked nervous. The arrogant swagger from the auction floor was completely gone, replaced by a tense,jittery energy.

Elisa stopped walking. She stood ten feet away from him. She didn't reach for her keycard. She just stared at him, her eyes completely blank, analyzing him like
a malfunctioning piece of hardware.

August cleared his throat. The silence was suffocating him.

He pulled his hand out of his tuxedo pocket. He was holding a square, deep blue velvet box.

He took a step toward her and held the box out. His chin tilted up, trying to project a facade of generous authority.

"Open it," August commanded, though his voice lacked its usual thunder.

Elisa didn't move her hands. She dropped her gaze to the box. She didn't need to open it. She knew exactly what was inside. The fifteen-million-dollar sapphire amulet.

She looked back up at his face.

"What's the matter?" Elisa asked, her voice a flat,mechanical drone. "Did your mistress complain that it was too heavy for her delicate neck? Are you bringing me her discarded trash?"

August's jaw clenched. A flash of guilt mixed with irritation crossed his eyes. She had hit the nail
exactly on the head. Allena had complained the antique metal scratched her skin and demanded a modern diamond instead.

"I bought it to compensate you," August lied, his voice tight. "Take it. It's worth fifteen million."

Elisa let out a short, breathy scoff. She crossed her arms over her chest.

"Cut the bullshit, August," Elisa said coldly. "You don't do anything without a price. What do you need?"

August swallowed hard. He looked up and down the empty hallway, paranoid.

He took another step closer, lowering his voice to a harsh whisper. "My mother has security personnel stationed at the elevator banks on this floor. She is monitoring my movements. She thinks Allena and I are becoming a liability. She warned me that if another scandal breaks at this event, she'll rally the board to kill my acquisition deal. Being seen leaving Allena's suite at dawn is exactly the ammunition she's looking for."

He paused, his eyes darting away from her face.
"I need to come into your room for ten minutes,"August said, rushing the words out. "Just long enough to bypass the hallway cameras. Then I will go out to the connecting balcony and cross over into Allena's suite next door."

The words hung in the quiet hallway.

For a moment, Elisa's brain simply refused to process the sheer, astronomical audacity of the request.

Her legal husband. The man who had sworn to protect her. He was offering her a necklace he had maliciously stolen from her at an auction, using it as a bribe so she would provide physical cover for him to go fuck his mistress in the room next door.②

It was a level of moral bankruptcy so profound it defied human language.

Elisa didn't feel anger.Anger required a baseline of respect to be violated. She felt a cold, physiological nausea, like she was standing next to a rotting corpse.

August saw her staring at him. He panicked,thrusting the velvet box forcefully against her chest.

"Take the damn box, Elisa!" August hissed. "It's fifteen million dollars! That's more than enough to buy ten minutes of your silence!"

Elisa looked down at the velvet box pressed against her sternum.

She thought of Kayden. She thought of the little girl who deserved the protection this amnulet symbolized.She was not going to let this sociopath keep it.

Elisa raised her hand and snatched the box from his grip.

She reached into her pocket, pulled out the gold keycard, and swiped it against the lock. Click.

She pushed the door open and stepped inside,leaving the door wide open for him. As she did, her thumb discreetly slid across the screen of her phone,activating the voice memo app. It was a reflex Faye had drilled into her: when a predator cornered you,you documented the encounter.

"Ten minutes," Elisa said, her voice dropping to a terrifying, absolute zero. "If you are in my room on the eleventh minute, I will call security and tell them an intruder is trying to assault me."

August exhaled a massive breath of relief. He quickly stepped into the penthouse and shut the door behind him.

Elisa didn't turn on the main lights. She left the room bathed in the dim glow of the entryway sconces. She walked over to the floor-to-ceiling windows and stared out into the pitch-black night, her back completely turned to him.

August stood awkwardly in the center of the living room.

He looked at her rigid spine. He expected her to yell.He expected her to throw the box at his head. He expected some form of passionate hatred.

But there was nothing. The silence in the room was absolute. It was the silence of a tomb. A weird,hollow ache suddenly bloomed in the center of August's chest, a terrifying realization that he had completely,permanently lost his grip on her soul.

He checked his heavy Rolex. Ten minutes exactly.

"I'm leaving," August muttered.

Elisa didn't turn around. She simply raised her right
hand and pointed a single finger toward the heavy glass doors leading to the balcony.

August gritted his teeth. He walked over to the glass doors, slid them open, and stepped out into the freezing wind. He hiked up his expensive tuxedo pants and awkwardly, pathetically, climbed over the wrought-iron divider separating the two balconies.

He disappeared into the dark.

Elisa walked over to the glass doors. She slammed them shut, engaged the heavy metal lock, and yanked the thick blackout curtains closed,completely sealing off the outside world.

She reached a hand up, her fingers subconsciously brushing against her phone in her suit pocket. A wave of absolute, lethal satisfaction washed over her.Every single pathetic, bribing word he had just spoken was now a digital file, another nail securely driven into his coffin.

She stood in the quiet room. She opened the blue velvet box.

The sapphire amulet glowed in te dim light.③
Elisa smiled. It was a soft, genuine smile meant only for the daughter hiding miles away.

"I got it back for you, Kayden," Elisa whispered.

She snapped the box shut. August Chambers was officially dead to her. Tomorrow, the real blood would flow.
###Chapter 58


The morning sun broke over the Belmont Resort,casting long, sharp shadows across the manicured lawns.

Down on the main grounds, the estate was hosting a massive charity half-marathon. The air was filled with the sound of cheering crowds, upbeat music, and the sharp blasts of starting horns.

Elisa stepped out of the lobby doors. She was dressed in a sleek, black athletic zip-up and dark leggings, a black baseball cap pulled low over her eyes. She needed the cold morning air to clear the lingering stench of August from her lungs.

She walked along the stone path that curved beneath the towering balconies of the penthouse suites.

She didn't mean to look up, but a flash of movement caught her eye.

On the balcony directly above her-Allena's balcony-stood August and Allena. They were both wearing
plush white hotel robes. August had his arms wrapped tightly around Allena's waist from behind,burying his face in her neck, kissing her in the morning sun.

Elisa's heart rate didn't even spike.

She looked at them with the detached, clinical disgust one might reserve for two flies mating on a piece of garbage. She lowered her head and kept walking toward the marathon route.

Near the finish line, a cluster of large, white medical tents had been erected, marked with massive red crosses.

Elisa walked toward the hydration station near the tents to grab a bottle of water.

Suddenly, a horrific, blood-curdling scream ripped through the ambient noise of the crowd.

It came from the VIP viewing bleachers fifty yards away. The crowd instantly fractured, people backing away in sheer panic.

Elisa's brain instantly shifted gears. The cold,calculating Al architect vanished,replaced entirely by
the adrenaline-fueled trauma surgeon.

She dropped her water bottle and sprinted. Her sneakers tore across the grass.

She pushed violently through the wall of panicked spectators.

Lying flat on his back on the turf was a powerfully built, elderly man in a bespoke tweed suit. It was Arthur Vance, the notoriously ruthless founder of a multi-billion dollar private equity firm and a major player in global finance.

Vance's hands were clawing desperately at his chest.His face was rapidly turning a horrifying shade of bruised purple. His eyes were rolling back into his skull.

Three massive bodyguards in black suits were hovering over him, completely paralyzed by panic,screaming into their radios for the estate's medical team, who were stuck on the other side of the marathon route.

Elisa's eyes scanned the man. Massive myocardial infarction. Cardiac arrest.
Golden window: Four minutes until irreversible brain death.

She didn't hesitate. She ducked under the velvet VIP rope.

To avoid the bodyguards tackling her as an attacker,she grabbed a bright red volunteer medical vest draped over a folding chair. She shoved her arms through the armholes as she ran, instantly establishing visual authority.

Elisa dropped to her knees beside the dying billionaire.

She didn't check for a pulse; the cyanosis in his face told her everything. She grabbed the lapels of his expensive tweed shirt and violently ripped them apart, popping the buttons into the air.

She locked her hands together, placed the heel of her palm dead center on his sternum, and locked her elbows.

She began chest compressions. The force was brutal, precise, and rhythmic. One, two, three, four.She pushed down two full inches, forcing the blood manually through his failing heart.
"You!" Elisa roared, not looking up, her voice cracking like a whip at the largest bodyguard. "Run to that medical tent! Bring me the AED and a crash kit with epinephrine! Move your ass!"

The sheer, terrifying authority in her voice broke the bodyguard's paralysis. He turned and sprinted toward the tents.

Two minutes passed. Elisa's shoulders burned with lactic acid. Sweat beaded on her forehead under the black cap, dripping down her nose. Vance's face remained purple.

The bodyguard crashed to his knees beside her,shoving the yellow AED box and a red medical bag onto the grass.

Elisa didn't stop compressions. "Open the AED! Turn it on! Peel the pads!"

The machine beeped to life. Elisa snatched the sticky pads and slapped them onto Vance's bare, sweaty chest.

Analyzing rhythm, the machine's robotic voice announced. Shock advised. Charging.
"Clear!" Elisa yelled, throwing her hands up.
She hit the flashing orange button.

Bang. Vance's torso violently arched off the ground as the electricity slammed into his heart.

He dropped back down. Elisa instantly checked his carotid artery. Nothing. The portable EKG monitor on the AED showed a flatline.

Elisa's eyes went dark. Standard protocols were failing. She needed to go extreme.

She ripped open the red crash kit. She bypassed the IV needles and grabbed a massive, terrifyingly long syringe of epinephrine.

The bodyguards gasped in horror as Elisa uncapped the needle. She wasn't looking for a vein.

She placed two fingers on the left side of Vance's sternum, finding the exact intercostal space. Without a millimeter of hesitation, she drove the long needle straight down, plunging it directlythrough the chest wall and into the muscle of his heart.

She slammed the plunger down, injecting the adrenaline directly into the core.
She pulled the needle out and threw it aside.

"Clear!" Elisa screamed, hitting the shock button a second time.

Bang.

The body convulsed.

For three agonizing seconds, there was silence.

Then, the AED monitor beeped. A jagged,beautiful spike appeared on the screen. Then another.

Arthur Vance's chest heaved. He sucked in a massive, ragged breath of air. The purple hue in his face slowly began to recede, replaced by a faint,sickly pink.

He was alive. She had literally dragged him back from the gates of hell.

In the distance, the deafening chop of helicopter blades filled the air. The Medevac chopper was landing on the lawn.

Paramedics rushed toward them with a stretcher.

Elisa stood up, her chest heaving, her hands shaking slightly from the adrenaline dump.
She looked at the approaching medical team. She looked at the bodyguards. If they realized who she was, her identity as Faye and her medical background would be exposed to August's inner circle before the summit.

She couldn't let that happen.

Elisa quickly stripped off the sweaty red volunteer vest. She tossed it casually onto the empty folding chair.She pulled the brim of her black baseball cap down lower over her face.

As the first paramedic reached the scene, Elisa grabbed his arm, her voice a low, urgent whisper."V-fib, shocked twice. One of intracardiac epi. He's back, but unstable." The paramedic nodded, his professional focus instantly shifting to the patient.That split second of information transfer was all the cover Elisa needed. While the bodyguards and paramedics swarmed Vance, forming a massive,impenetrable physical wall of broad shoulders and medical equipment that completely blocked the spectators' line of sight, Elisa took a step back. She didn't hesitate for a fraction of a second. Utilizing the brief, chaotic window created by a passing catering cart being violently shoved aside by another medic,
she turned around, and slipped away dlown the secluded service pathway toward the rear of the estate.
###Chapter 59


The Medevac helicopter lifted off the grass, the massive downdraft whipping the trees into a frenzy as it rushed Arthur Vance to Mount Sinai Hospital in Manhattan.

Devon Browning sprinted across the lawn, his face pale and slick with panicked sweat. He had been at the clubhouse when he got the call. Vance's firm was a cornerstone investor in several of Devon's riskiest ventures; currying favor with the family was a top priority.

He crashed through the VIP ropes, grabbing the lead bodyguard by the lapels of his suit.

"Who did it?!" Devon screamed, his voice cracking with hysteria. "The paramedics said someone stabilized him before they got here! Who saved Arthur Vance?!"

The bodyguard, still in shock, pointed a shaking finger toward the empty folding chair.
"It was a woman," the bodyguard stammered."Young. She was wearing one of those red volunteer vests. She wore a black baseball cap, pulled down low. I couldn't get a good look at her face, she was so focused on his chest. But the red vest was unmistakable. Sir, I've never seen anything like it. She didn't even use an IV. She didn't even look for a vein.She took a huge, terrifyingly long needle and stabbed it directly down through his chest wall,straight into his actual heart! I've never seen anything so brutal... or so effective. She was a genius."

Devon's eyes widened. His mind instantly translated the bodyguard's frantic, graphic description. An intracardiac injection was an extreme, highly specialized trauma maneuver. Whoever did that wasn't just a volunteer; she was a top-tier medical professional.

"Find her!" Devon roared at the security team. "Lock down the estate! Find the woman in the red vest!"

Fifty yards away, near the edge of the medical tents,Allena was walking gingerly in her medical boot.

She had dragged a private photographer down from
the penthouse. She needed some "candid" shots of herself looking concerned near the charity event to post on Instagram and repair her damaged public image.

She scowled at the messy grass. "Make sure you get my good side," she snapped at the photographer.

As she posed near the tents, she noticed a bright red volunteer vest draped over afolding chair.

Allena's eyes lit up. A prop.

She picked up the vest. It was slightly damp with sweat, which disgusted her, but she slipped it on over her expensive silk dress anyway. She put her hands on her hips, putting on a face of deep, tragic concern for the camera.

"Take the shot now," Allena commanded.

Suddenly, the sound of heavy boots pounding against the grass made her turn.

Devon Browning, flanked by three massive security guards, was sprinting directly toward her. His eyes were locked onto the bright red vest she was wearing.
Devon's brain, clouded by panic and desperation,instantly connected the dots.

A young woman. A red vest. And Allena was a dloctor.

Devon crashed to a halt in front of her. He reached out and grabbed Allena by the shoulders. His eyes were red, filling with tears of absolute gratitude.

"Allena!" Devon gasped, his voice trembling. "It was you.You saved him."

Allena was completely caught off guard. She blinked,looking at Devon's manic face.

"Devon, you're hurting me," Allena said, trying to pull away. "I just got down here to-"

"The guards told me everything!" Devon gasped,his voice trembling. "They told me what you did! The chest compressions, the AED... the direct injection into his heart! You brought Arthur Vance back from the dead!"

The description of a direct injection into the heart hit Allena's brain.

She knew exactly what an intracardiac injection was from medical school, even though she had never
successfully performed one. She realized instantly that some actual doctor had saved the billionaire,and Devon was completely mistaking her for the savior.

Allena opened her mouth to correct him. She was about to say she just put the vest on for a photo.

But then she looked at Devon. She saw the absolute worship in his eyes. The Vance family controiled a financial empire. If she was the savior of their patriarch,she would be untouchable. She would have infinite power, infinite funding, and August would look at her like a god.

The sheer, intoxicating greed swallowed her morality whole.

Allena closed her mouth. She lowered her eyes,forcing her face into a mask of humble exhaustion.

"Devon," Allena whispered, her voice trembling with fake emotion. "I... I just did what any doctor would do. I couldn't let him die."

Devon let out a choked sob. He dropped to one knee right there on the grass, taking Allena's hand and pressing it to his forehead.
"The Browning family owes you, the Vance family owes you, we all owe you everything," Devon swore loudly. "From this day forward, you ask for anything,and it is yours. You are a hero, Allena."

Allena looked down at him. A slow, sickeningly triumphant smile spread across her face. She slipped the sweaty red vest off her shoulders and handed it to the bodyguard, treating it like a sacred relic.

She had just stolen the greatest medical victory of the decade.

An hour later, August burst into the VIP lounge.Devon had called him.

August looked at Allena sitting on the leather sofa,surrounded by fawning staff. His eyes were wide with shock and a deep, obsessive pride. He walked over,pulled her up, and kissed her fiercely. He felt completely vindicated. His mistress wasn't a liability;she was a genius savior.

Miles away, on the highway heading back to Manhattan, Elisa was driving the Porsche.⑤

Her phone buzzed on the passenger seat. It was a text from Jewel.
Elisa. You are not going to believe this. Devon just posted on Instagram. Allena is claiming she saved Arthur Vance. She stole your credit.

Elisa read the text at a stoplight.

She didn't grip the steering wheel in anger. She didn't scream.

Elisa threw her head back against the carbon-fiber headrest and laughed. It was a bright, genuinely amused sound.

Allena was a medical illiterate. By claiming she performed an advanced intracardiac injection, she had just painted a massive, glowing target on her own back for the entire medical community to scrutinize.

Elisa picked up the phone and typed back to Jewel.

Don't say a word. Let her wear the crown. Let her climb as high as she wants. When the pedestal breaks, I want her to fall far enough to shatter every bone in her body.
###Chapter 60


Twenty-four hours later. The countdown clock on the marriage contract was ticking down to its final agonizing digits. The air at the Belmont Resort's private marina was thick with the smell of lake water and burning money.

Docked at the pier was a massive, three-story superyacht. Devon Browning had rented the hundred-million-dollar vessel under the guise of a birthday party, but everyone in the New York elite knew the truth: it was a coronation ceremony for Allena Mills, the "savior" of Arthur Vance.

Elisa walked up the wooden gangway.②

She wore a razor-sharp, black Le Smoking tuxedo suit. Her hair was slicked back, her lips painted a dark, bruised crimson. She looked like a corporate assassin.

She was only here because August's lawyers had informed her that the final supplementary asset transfer documents for the divorce required his
physical, wet-ink signature, and he had arrogantly demanded she bring them to the yacht.

Elisa stepped onto the teak deck. The top level was a sea of glittering gowns and bespoke suits.Champagne flowed like water.

In the absolute center of the crowd stood Allena.

She was wearing a blindingly silver sequined gown.Resting heavily against her collarbone was the fifteen-million-dollar blue sapphire amulet. She was laughing, holding a glass of champagne, soaking in the absolute worship of the Wall Street elite.

Elisa bypassed the crowd, stepping into the shadows near the starboard railing. She leaned against the cool metal, watching the circus.

Devon Browning climbed up onto the raised DJ platform. He tapped the microphone, the feedback whining sharply over the speakers. The crowd went silent.

"Listen up!" Devon yelled, his face flushed with alcohol and excitement. "Tonight, we are here to celebrate a true hero. A woman with the hands of a god and the heart of an angel. Allena Mills!"
The crowd erupted into deafening applause.

Devon raised his glass high. "To show our gratitude,the Browning Venture Capital Fund is officially announcing a fifty-million-dollar full sponsorship to build Allena her own independent, state-of-the-art medical research laboratory!"

The cheers grew louder. Allena covered her mouth,faking tears of shock, though she had clearly known about the money.

She walked up onto the platform and took the microphone from Devon.

The power had completely rotted her brain. She looked out at the crowd of billionares and investors,her ego inflating to a catastrophic size.

"Thank you," Allena said, her voice dripping with fake humility. "Saving lives is my passion. But clinical work isn't my only frontier. With this new funding,I am excited to announce that I will be independently spearheading the architecture of a new, revolutionary medical Al system."

Standing in the shadows, Elisa actually choked on her sip of sparkling water.

She coughed, pressing a hand to her mouth to stifie a laugh.

It was the most spectacular display of suicidal stupidity she had ever witnessed. Stealing a CPR credit was one thing. Standing in front of tech investors and claiming she could architect an Al-claiming she was operating on Faye's level-was signing her own professional death warrant in blood.

Devon grabbed the mic back, grinning wildly. He pointed directly at August, who was standing near the front of the crowd, beaming with pride.

"August!" Devon yelled into the mic. "You have the most brilliant, perfect woman in the world right there!What are you waiting for? Show her how much you appreciate her!"

The crowd of sycophants instantly caught on. They started chanting, clapping their hands in rhythm."Kiss her! Kiss her! Kiss her!"

They knew Elisa, his legal wife, was on the boat.They didn't care. It was a collective, brutal bullying tactic designed to elevate Allena and erase Elisa from existence.
August's ego swelled. He loved the chanting. He loved being the center of the universe.

He walked up to the platform, grabbed Allena by the waist, dipped her dramatically, and kissed her deeply in front of the flashing cameras.

The crowd went wild.

Elisa watched them. Her heart beat with a slow,steady,icy rhythm.

A few minutes later, the music resumed. August walked down from the platform. He grabbed a fresh drink and swaggered over to the starboard railing,spotting Elisa in the shadows.

He walked up to her, his chest puffed out,radiating the arrogant energy of a conquering king.

"You saw that?" August sneered, looking down at her black suit. "That is what real brilliance looks like.Allena is a genius. She's building an empire. You could live a thousand lifetimes and never achieve a fraction of what she has."

Elisa didn't blink. She reached into her jacket pocket,pulled out the thick stack of legal documents, and
slapped them flat against August's chest.

August instinctively grabbed the papers before they fell.

Elisa looked up at him. Her black eyes were so cold,so entirely devoid of human warmth, that a sudden,involuntary shiver ran down August's spine.

"You're right, August," Elisa whispered. Her voice was smooth, deadly, and dripping with absolute mockery. "She is so brilliant. She is shining so brightly, I can barely wait to watch her burn on the pyre she just built for herself."

She pulled a silver pen from her pocket and clicked it. "Sign the paper. I have a funeral to prepare for."

August glared at her, unnerved by her total lack of jealousy. He angrily scribbled his signature on the bottom line and shoved the papers back at her.

Elisa took them, turned on her heel, and walked away without another word.

She walked down the stairs to the lower deck,stepping onto the small, motorized tender boat waiting to take her back to the mainland.
As the tender pulled away from the massive yacht,the bass from the party thumping over the water,Elisa's phone vibrated in her pocket.

She pulled it out. It was a secure text from Alastair Cromwell.

The Global Al Summit invitations have all been confirmed. The execution stage is fully built. We are just waiting for the Queen to arrive.

Elisa looked back at the glowing yacht shrinking in the distance. She typed her reply.

Game on.
###Chapter 61


The morning sun beat down on the Belmont Resort's sprawling stone terrace, baking the leftover smell of spilled champagne and expensive citrus perfume into the air. The post-gala brunch was winding down,the wealthy guests slowly dispersing toward the valet stands.

Elisa stepped onto the sun-drenched marble. She had already packed her bags. She was onlyreturning to this specific terrace for one reason, a final, critical piece of her exit strategy. Tucked inside the lining of a custom dark grey shawl she'd intentionally left draped over a chair the night before was a micro-SD card, no bigger than her pinky nail. It contained the only untraceable, offline backup of the payload she'd embedded in the Aethel servers. It was her insurance policy, her doomsday switch, and she would not leave without it.

She spotted the fabric resting on a wrought-iron seat near the edge of the patio. She walked toward it,her face a mask of absolute, exhausted indifference.
Before her fingers could brush the wool, a figure stepped directly into her path.

It was Allena.

She was holding a half-empty mimosa glass. She wore a pristine white sundress that contrasted sharply with the heavy medical boot still strapped to her right foot. She stood intentionally blocking the narrow walkway leading to the exit, her eyes gleaming with a toxic, provocative light.

Allena leaned in, lowering her voice so only Elisa could hear.

"You looked pathetic last night," Allena whispered,her tone dripping with vicious mockery. "Standing in the dark like a ghost while everyone celebrated me.August didn't even look at you. You are completely invisible."

Elisa stopped. She didn't clench her fists. She didn't frown.

She looked at Allena's face. She looked at the smug,triumphant smile of a woman who had built her entire existence on stolen achievements and stolen men.Elisa looked at her exactly the way a scientist looks at
a defective, slightly disgusting insect.

Elisa didn't say a single word. She simply stepped to the side and walked straight past her.

That absolute, silent dismissal hit Allena harder than a physical slap. It instantly punctured her inflated,fragile ego.

"Don't you walk away from me!" Allena hissed.

She reached out and grabbed the sleeve of Elisa's black athletic jacket. Her manicured nails dug sharply into the fabric.

Elisa's survival instincts flared. She didn't hesitate.She coldly and sharply yanked her arm forward,shaking off Allena's grip.

The physical force Elisa used was incredibly precise.It was just enough to break the hold, absolutely not enough to cause a grown woman to lose her balance.

But Allena didn't try to keep her balance.

Allena let out a sudden, blood-curdling, theatrical scream. She threw her arms up, her body going entirely limp, and pitched herself backward like a broken kite caught in a downdraft.
She tumbled backward down the three shallow marble steps leading to the lower garden.

It was a spectacularly exaggerated fall. Allena rolled over the hard stone. The delicate crystal mimosa glass in her hand shattered violently against the marble.

Sharp shards of glass exploded outward. One jagged piece sliced directly into Allena's bare left calf.

Bright red blood instantly welled up, spilling down her pale skin and staining the pristine white marble.The visual was shocking.

"Help!" Allena shrieked, clutching her bleeding leg,tears instantly flooding her eyes. "Oh my god,it hurts!"

The piercing screams acted like a siren. The ambient chatter on the terrace died instantly. Dozens of wealthy socialites and investors turned their heads,their eyes widening in horror. The crowd surged forward like a tidal wave.

Devon Browning was the first to break through the line of people.
He saw Allena-the woman he worshipped as the savior of his billionaire investor-lying in a pool of her own blood at the bottom of the stairs.

Devon's eyes went completely, violently red.

He let out a roar of pure, unhinged rage. He charged up the three marble steps like a wild animal.He didn't ask questions. He didn't pause. He lunged at Elisa and shoved her backward with both of his massive hands.

The sheer, brutal force of a man twice her size lifting her off her feet was unstoppable.

Elisa flew backward. Her lower back slammed violently into the hard, unyielding edge of a Roman stone pillar.

A sickening crack echoed in her own ears. A massive,blinding wave of agony exploded from her spine,shooting down her legs and up into her skull. Her vision went entirely black for a fraction of a second.The air was violently punched out of her lungs. The impact was so jarring that her athletic jacket rode up her back for a split second, revealing a flash of pale skin to a resort security guard standing only a few

feet away before the fabric fell back into place.

She siumped forward, a harsh, involuntary grunt tearing from her throat as she caught herself on her hands and knees.

"You vicious, psychotic bitch!" Devon screamed,pointing a shaking finger directly at Elisa's face. "You pushed her! You actually pushed her down the stairs!"

The surrounding socialites gasped. Women covered their mouths in exaggerated horror. Their eyes darted to Elisa, filled with absolute, piercing condemnation. They looked at her like she was a monster.③

"August! Let me through!" a deep, panicked voice roared from the back of the crowd.

August shoved two men aside, his face dark and frantic. He broke into the clearing and saw the blood on the marble.

"August!" Allena sobbed, her voice trembling perfectly. She reached her bloody hand out toward him."It hurts so much. I just wanted to say good morning to her... I didn't mean to upset her..."
August's heart violently contracted. He dropped to one knee on the hard stone, completely ignoring the blood soaking into his expensive trousers. He gathered Allena into his arms with a terrifying, fragile care, pressing her face into his chest.

He slowly lifted his head.

His dark eyes locked onto Elisa. She was just managing to stand up, her face pale, one hand pressing desperately against her throbbing lower back.

August's gaze was a pair of freezing, lethal swords.

"I didn't push her," Elisa said. Her voice was strained from the pain, but the tone was absolute ice."She threw herself down the stairs."

August let out a harsh, incredibly cruel laugh.

"She threw herself down?" August sneered, his voice booming over the silent crowd. "Look at her leg,Elisa! Your pathetic, desperate jealousy has finally made you lose your mind. You are sick."①

The words hit the air like a gavel slamming down in a courtroom.
August Chambers, the billionaire patriarch, had just publicly and definitively cemented his legal wife's guilt.

The crowd immediately erupted into vicious whispers.

"I knew she was crazy."

"She's just a jealous homewrecker trying to kill the real girlfriend."

"Someone call the police."

Elisa stood against the stone pillar. She looked at August. She looked at the man she had married, the man who was currently cradling a pathological liar while his own wife stood battered and bruised against a wall.

A thick, heavy wave of physiological nausea rolled through Elisa's stomach. The urge to vomit was so strong she had to swallow hard against the acid rising in her throat.

She didn't shed a single tear. Tears were for people who still had hope.

Elisa slowly lifted her right hand. She ignored the
crowd. She ignored Devon. She pointed a single,steady finger toward the upper corner of the resort's stucco wall, completely hidden behind a hanging fern.

"There is a security camera right there," Elisa said.Her voice wasn't loud, but it cut through the murmurs with razor-sharp clarity. "Since you all want to play judge and jury, let the footage speak."

The word 'camera' acted like a physical electric shock.

In August's arms, Allena's body went completely,rigidly stiff. A flash of pure, unadulterated panic ripped through her tear-filled eyes. She tried to bury her face deeper into his shirt to hide it.

Devon immediately jumped forward, his chest puffed out. "Good! The camera will show exactly what you did! We are pulling that footage right now. You are not leaving this estate, Elisa!"

August stood up, lifting Allena effortlessly into his arms. He looked down at Elisa with the ultimate,arrogant posture of a dictator passing a death sentence.
"Bring the estate manager to the security room,"August ordered coldly, not looking away from Elisa."Pull the footage immediately. I am going to make you watch it, Elisa. I want you to lose with absolutely zero excuses."
###Chapter 62


The estate's security control room was buried in the windowless basement of the main building. The air down here was stale, smelling faintly of ozone and hot electronics.

Elisa stood in the narrow, fluorescent-lit corridor outside the closed door of the control room. Two massive resort security guards stood on either side of her, their hands resting near their radios, treating her exactly like a violent criminal in custody.

A deep,rhythmic throbbing pulsed in Elisa's lower back. The impact against the stone pillar had left a massive, hot knot of damaged muscle right against her spine. She leaned her shoulder against the cold concrete wall, trying to take the weight off her legs.

At the other end of the narrow corridor stood Devon Browning.

He had brought a small entourage of wealthy socialites down with him. They stood in a tight cluster, arms crossed, staring down the hallway at
Elisa with eyes full of morbid, predatory excitement.

Inside the control room, the estate manager was sweating profusely as he typed on the keyboards.Upstairs, in the VIP medical suite, August was personally overseeing the doctor stitching Allena's calf.

The heavy silence in the corridor stretched on for ten agonizing minutes.

Devon couldn't handle the quiet. He swaggered a few steps forward, a cruel, mocking sneer twisting his face.

"What's the matter, Elisa?" Devon taunted, his voice echoing loudly off the concrete walls. "You look a little pale. Are you finally scared? Trying to think up a pathetic excuse for when that video shows you shoving her?"

Elisa slowly turned her head.

She looked at Devon. She didn't see a billionaire venture capitalist. She saw a loud, incredibly stupid piece of unrecyclable garbage.

"Mr. Browning," Elisa said. Her voice was a flat, dead
monotone. "Your verbal output is inversely proportional to your cognitive processing. A classic sign of systemic failure. I suggest a neurological evaluation before you short-circuit entirely."

The words hit Devon like a physical slap across the face.

His face instantly drained of color, then flushed a violent, mottled purple. The precision of her insult,the way she casually weaponized his own glaring incompetence, completely shattered his arrogant facade.

"You bitch," Devon snarled. He raised his hand, his body lunging forward, fully intending to strike her across the face.

Elisa didn't flinch. She didn't step back. Her black eyes instantly sharpened into two lethal points of obsidian.

"There is a camera in this hallway, too," Elisawarned,her voice dropping to a terrifying, freezing whisper."You lay one finger on me, Devon, and I guarantee this footage will be in every media inbox before the sun goes down, with the headline 'Billionaire's friend
physically assaults woman in public.' Guess how your family's investors will react to that?"

The sheer, dominant authority radiating from her small frame hit Devon like a brick wall.

He froze. Hisraised hand trembled in the air. He looked at her eyes and realized she wasn't bluffing.She would destroy him in the court of public opinion before he even took another breath. He slowly,furiously lowered his arm and took a step back, his jaw grinding so hard his teeth squeaked.

The socialites behind him shifted uncomfortably.They began to whisper, their voices dripping with venom.

"She has no shame," a woman in a Chanel suit muttered loudly. "She pushes a brilliant doctor down the stairs and acts like she owns the place."

"I heard she was just a nobody ER nurse before she trapped Mr. Chambers. She's just a desperate gold digger holding onto a marriage that's clearly over."

The toxic words washed over Elisa like dirty water.
She didn't even blink.
Her heart rate remained perfectly steady. In her mind, these people were already dead. Their intellect had been entirely rotted by inherited wealth and blind prejudice. They were not worth a single drop of her emotional energy.

Click.

The heavy metal door of the control room finally opened.

The estate manager walked out. He was holding a black tablet in his trembling hands. His face was completely ashen. Sweat beaded on his forehead.

Devon instantly pushed past the socialites and rushed the manager.

"Did you get it?" Devon demanded, his voice frantic with anticipation. "Play it! Show everyone exactly how she pushed Allena!"

The manager swallowed hard. His eyes darted nervously to Elisa, then back to Devon. He opened his mouth, but no words came out. He looked absolutely terrified.

Elisa pushed herself off the concrete wall. She stood
perfectly straight, ignoring the sharp spike of pain in her spine.

"Turn the tablet around," Elisa commanded,her voice steady and absolute. "Show them."

The manager wiped his sweating forehead with the back of his sleeve. He slowly rotated the tablet so the screen faced Devon and the crowd. He pressed play.

The high-definition security footage was crystal clear.

It showed Allena stepping into Elisa's path. It showed Allena aggressively grabbing Elisa's sleeve. It showed Elisa calmly,firmly shaking her arm free.

And then, it showed the truth.

It showed Allena standing perfectly still for a fraction of a second after the physical contact ended.Then,without a single ounce of external force touching her,Allena threw her arms into the air and violently launched her own body backward down the stairs.

It was a pathetic, incredibly obvious theatrical dive.

The video looped. And looped again.
The corridor went dead silent. It was the kind of heavy, suffocating silence that follows a bomb detonating. The socialites who had just been screaming for Elisa's blood suddenly looked like they had been collectively choked.

Devon's pupils dilated violently. He stared at the glowing screen. His mouth opened slightly, his brain completely short-circuiting as his entire reality regarding his "hero" shattered into pieces.

Elisa let out a short, breathy scoff. It was the coldest sound in the world.

"Did you all get a good look?" Elisa asked, her eyes sweeping over the paralyzed crowd. "Your holy,untouchable victim is nothing but a cheap, D-list actress."

Elisa reached her hand out to take the tablet from the manager. She needed that video file. It was the absolute proof she needed to clear her name.

Before her fingers could touch the glass screen, the sound of heavy, deliberate footsteps echoed from the far end of the corridor.

August Chambers walked out of the shadows.
He was wearing a fresh, perfectly tailored navy suit.Two massive private bodyguards walked a step behind him. His face was a mask of dark,impenetrable stone.

He didn't look at Devon. He didn't look at the crowd.He walked straight up to the estate manager.

August extended his large hand. His voice carried the heavy, absolute arrogance of a dictator who owned the very air in the room.

"Give me the tablet," August ordered.

The estate manager physically flinched. He didn't hesitate for a microsecond. He practically shoved the tablet into August's waiting hand, bowing his head in total submission.

Elisa's stomach violently dropped. The cold air in the hallway suddenly felt suffocating.

She looked at August's face. She saw the dark,calculating look in his eyes. She realized instantly that August hadn't come down here to find the truth.He had come down here to bury it.

###Chapter 63


August held the black tablet in his large hand. The high-definition video of Allena throwing herself backward down the stairs was still looping silently on the screen.

He stared at the footage for exactly one second. For a fraction of that second, a flicker of cold,hard shock registered in his eyes. He saw the blatant, undeniable deception. He saw the lie. But then, another, more powerful realization crashed over him: the public humiliation of admitting he'd been duped, of having chosen a liar over his wife in front of his entire social circle. His ego, vast and fragile, violently rejected the truth. His facial expression didn't change. There was no realization of his own horrific mistake.

His thumb moved. He pressed the power button on the side of the device.

The screen instantly went black, swallowing the truth whole.

Devon finally snapped out of his paralysis. He took a
step toward August, his hands shaking, his voice a confused, stuttering mess.

"August... the video," Devon stammered, pointing at the blank tablet. "Allena... she wasn't pushed. I think she might have just slipped... or maybe she lost her balance..."

August slowly raised his free hand, holding it flat in the air.

It was a silent, absolute command to shut up.Devon's mouth clicked shut instantly.

August was not going to let Devon finish that sentence. He was absolutely unwilling to let the image of Allena as a perfect, innocent victim be shattered in front of these people. His ego was too deeply tied to her purity.

August turned his head slowly. He locked his dark,terrifying gaze onto the trembling estate manager.

"Tell me," August said, his voice a low, vibrating threat that chilled the air in the corridor. "What does the Belmont Resort's VIP privacy policy state regarding internal security footage?"
The manager's knees practically buckled. He swallowed hard, his voice shaking as he recited the corporate mandate.

"Sir... the policy states that no internal security footage may be retained, copied, or viewed without the unanimous, written consent of all VIP guests involved in the incident."

August gave a slow, satisfied nod. He casually tossed the black tablet back into the manager's chest. The manager scrambled to catch it before it hit the floor.

"Since Ms. Mills is currently receiving medical treatment for severe trauma," August said, his tone dripping with fake legal authority, "she is in no condition to provide consent. Therefore, this footage is a violation of her privacy. Delete it. Completely wipe the server.Now."

Elisa stared at him.

She knew this man was arrogant. She knew he was selfish. But watching him stand in a hallway full of witnesses and blatantly, shamelessly order the destruction of evidence to protect a liar was a level of sociopathic entitlement that defied logic.
She took a step forward. Her hands clenched into tight fists at her sides. Her voice trembled, not from fear,but from a rage so pure it burned her throat.

"August," Elisa said, her voice cracking like a whip."That video is the only proof of my innocence. You are destroyingevidence to protect a woman who faked an injury to frame your wife. Have you completely lost your mind?"

August slowly turned to face her.

He looked down at her. His eyes were completely devoid of empathy. He looked at her with the supreme, condescending arrogance of a god looking at a complaining mortal.

He took one step closer, invading her personal space. He leaned down, lowering his voice so only she could hear the absolute poison in his words.

"Proof?" August whispered harshly. "You want to talk about proof? Even if she faked the fall, why did she do it? Because you provoked her. Because you stood there and insulted her. If you hadn't been so hostile,she wouldn't have felt the need to defend herself.Ultimately, Elisa, this is still your fault."

The words hit Elisa's brain, but they didn't process as language. They processed as a physical sickness.

It was the ultimate, twisted logic of an abuser. The victim-blaming was so absolute, so deeply ingrained in his psyche, that he actually believed his own lies.

Elisa felt something inside her chest finally,permanently snap. The last microscopic thread of human connection she had to this man turned to ash and blew away. Her heart became a solid block of ice.④

She didn't argue. She didn't scream. She just stared at him with dead, empty eyes.

The socialites in the hallway, taking their cue from August's dominant posture, instantly shifted their allegiance back to power.

"He's right," a man muttered. "She probably said something horrific to trigger the poor girl."

"She's toxic. She just wants to ruin the event."

August turned his back on Elisa. He faced the crowd,adjusting his suit jacket, instantly slipping back into his role as the benevolent billionaire.
"This ridiculous spectacle is over," August announced loudly. "To ensure the safety and comfort of the remaining guests, I am asking security to escort the instigator off the premises immediately."

He didn't even use her name. He called her 'the instigator'.

The two massive bodyguards immediately stepped forward. They flanked Elisa, their large hands hovering inches from her arms, projecting a clear threat of physical force if she resisted.

Elisa didn't fight them.

Fighting physical giants in a concrete basement would only make her look desperate and unhinged.She had too much dignity to give them that satisfaction.

She straightened her spine. She lifted her chin. She looked at the crowd of wealthy parasites, her eyes sweeping over them with a look of such profound,aristocratic disgust that several of them actually looked away in shame.

Finally, her gaze locked onto Augus's back.

A slow, terrifying, completely unnatural smile curved Elisa's lips.

"Remember exactly what you did today, August,"Elisa said. Her voice was soft, but it carried a chilling,metallic echo down the hallway. "Because I promise you, a day is coming very soon where you will be on your knees, begging me to delete this memory."

August's spine stiffened. The sheer, absolute certainty in her voice sent a sudden, involuntary spike of ice-cold panic straight into his heart. He forced himself not to turn around. He just let out a dismissive scoff.

Elisa turned around.

She walked down the corridor, the two bodyguards trailing her like prison wardens. She walked with perfect, unhurried grace, her footsteps echoing loudly against the concrete.

Ten minutes later, the bright afternoon sun blinded her as she stepped out of the resort lobby.

She walked across the gravel driveway to the valet stand. She handed the attendant her ticket. A minute later, the dark grey Porsche 911 GT3 pulled up, its

engine rumbling like a caged beast.

Elisa opened the heavy door and slid into the low carbon-fiber bucket seat.

She slammed the door shut. The thick acoustic glass instantly cut off the outside world. The silence of the cabin wrapped around her.

She reached behind her, her fingers gently touching her lower back. She winced as a sharp spike of pain shot through her muscles. A massive, dark bruise was already forming under her skin.

She pulled her phone from her pocket. She didn't call a doctor. She didn't call Jewel. Before she could open her secure app, a message from Jewel flashed on the screen: Trouble. A nasty rumor about you and Allena just hit a trashy gossip blog. Looks like Devon's people are already starting something. The seed of the attack had already been planted.

She opened her encrypted dialing app and pressed a single button.

The line connected instantly.

"Alastair," Elisa said. Her voice was entirely stripped

of emotion. It was the voice of a machine executing a kill code. "Accelerate the timeline. They've declared war. I want the Chambers family to wake up and see hell on the financial news tonight."

A low, dark chuckle came through the speaker.

"As you wish, Faye," Alastair replied.

Elisa dropped the phone onto the passenger seat.She gripped the Alcantara steering wheel, her knuckles turning white. She slammed her foot down on the accelerator.

The Porsche's engine screamed, the tires violently tearing at the gravel as the car shot forward, leaving the Belmont Resort behind in a cloud of dust.④
###Chapter 64


The digital clock on the microwave glowed a harsh green: 7:00 PM.

Elisa stood in the master bathroom of her temporary Manhattan apartment. She had just finished pressing a heavy, freezing ice pack against the massive,purple-black bruise blooming across her lower spine.The cold numbed the sharpest edges of the pain.④

She pulled on a set of loose, black silk loungewear.The soft fabric felt like armor against her battered skin.

She walked into the quiet living room,pouring herself a cup of hot black tea. She needed the caffeine. She needed her brain operating at maximum capacity for the digital war Alastair was currently launching.

Suddenly, the sharp, jarring buzz of the apartment intercom shattered the silence.

Elisa frowned. She walked over to the wall-mounted security console and tapped the screen.
The high-definition camera feed from the lobby downstairs flickered on. Standing in front of the glass doors, looking incredibly impatient and furious, was August.

Elisa stared at his face on the screen. A wave of pure,exhausted annoyance washed over her. She considered leaving him down there to rot. But the final, supplementary asset transfer documents for the divorce were still being processed by his lawyers.She couldn't afford to give him an excuse to stall the legal machinery.

She pressed the unlock button.

Three minutes later, a heavy, aggressive knock hammered against her front door.

Elisa unlocked the deadbolt and pulled the door open.

August pushed his way inside without waiting for an invitation. He was wearing the same navy suit from the resort, but his tie was ripped off and his collar was unbuttoned. He radiated a frantic, chaotic energy. He looked around the modest apartment with blatant disgust, as if the very air was beneath him.
He turned to face her. There was no apology in his eyes for the violence of the morning. There was only a demanding, arrogant expectation.

"We have a problem," August ordered, his voice sharp and absolute. "A problem you created. Get dressed. You're coming with me to fix it."

Elisa stood by the kitchen island. She picked up her teacup, bringing the hot porcelain to her lips. She didn't even blink at his command.

"Did one of the estate guard dogs die?" Elisa asked,her voice a flat, deadpan drawl. "Isthat why you need me to attend a mourning service?"

August's jaw clenched violently. The muscles in his neck jumped.

"Don't be a smart-ass," August snapped. "Some pathetic paparazzi managed to sneak a long-lens camera onto the Belmont grounds this morning. They sold the photos of the terrace incident to Page Six.The pictures are completely out of context."

Elisa raised an eyebrow. "Out of context?"

"They are framing Allena as a homewrecker," August
hissed, his eyes wide with outrage. "The headline is calling her a violent mistress who attacked the legal wife. The internet is tearing her apart. She hasn't stopped crying for three hours."

Elisa stared at himn. She looked at the genuine distress on his face. He was actually suffering because his mistress was facing the consequences of her own actions.

"Well," Elisa said softly, taking a slow sip of her tea."It sounds like the internet finally got something right."

"Shut up!" August roared, slamming his open palm down on the marble kitchen island. The teacup rattled violently.

He leaned over the counter, his face inches from hers.

"You are going to fix this," August commanded,pronouncing every word with slow, threatening emphasis. "You are going to come to the manor. And when we leave, you are going to hold my hand in front of the paparazzi waiting at the gates. You are going to smile, and you are going to act like we are

perfectly happy."

He stood up straight, crossing his arms, presenting his demand as if it were a generous business offer.

"If you act as a PR shield and prove to the media that our marriage is fine," August said, "the rumors about Allena being a homewrecker will die instantly. Do this, and I will tell my lawyers to add an extra million to your divorce settlement."

Elisa's fingers slowly tightened around the handle of her teacup.

She looked at the man standing in her kitchen. He was asking his legally wedded wife-the woman whose back was currently bruised black and blue because of his friends-to go out in public and pretend to be in love with him, solely to protect the reputation of the woman he was sleeping with.

It was a level of psychological cruelty so profound it bordered on the surreal.

Elisa carefully set the teacup down on the marble.She walked around the island. She walked straight to the front door, grabbed the heavy brass handle, and yanked the door wide open.
She stood in the doorway and pointed a single, rigid finger toward the hallway.

"August," Elisa said. Her voice was completely devoid of anger. It was the freezing, absolute zero of deep space. "You are a medical miracle. I have never seen a human being survive without a brain or a spine for this long."

August's face turned a violent shade of red. "Elisa-"

"Take your pathetic bribes, take your fake tears,and get out of my house," Elisa cut him off, her voice slicing through the air like a scalpel. "I would rather swallow broken glass than spend one second protecting that parasite. Get out."

August stared at her. His chest heaved. The absolute rejection, the total lack of fear in her eyes, shattered his fragile ego into a million pieces.

He stepped into the doorway. He leaned down, his eyes burning with a dark, sociopathic hatred.

"You are going to regret this," August whispered,his voice shaking with rage. "You think you can embarrass me? You think you can hurt her and get away with it? I will unleash a storm on you that will
leave you with absolutely nothing. You won't be able to walk down a street in New York without people spitting on you."

Elisa didn't flinch. She looked right back into his eyes, her lips curving into a terrifying, predatory smile.

She shoved the heavy wooden door forward. August had to step back quickly to avoid getting hit in the face.

Bang.

She leaned her back against the wood, closing her eyes, letting out a long, slow breath. Her heart was beating a fast, steady rhythm against her ribs.

Suddenly, her cell phone on the kitchen counter began to vibrate violently. It buzzed, stopped, and immediately buzzed again.

Elisa walked over and picked it up. It was Jewel.
She swiped to answer. "Jewel?"

"Elisa, are you looking at your phone right now?"Jewel's voice was high-pitched, frantic, and breathless. "You need to open Twitter. You need to look at Page Six. Right now."

"August was just here complaining about Page Six,"Elisa said, her brow furrowing. "He said they were attacking Allena."

"What? No!" Jewel yelled into the phone. "Elisa, they aren't attacking Allena. They flipped the narrative.They are destroying you."

Elisa's blood ran cold. She pulled the phone away from her ear and tapped the Twitter icon.

The screen loaded. A massive, coordinated digital execution was already underway.
###Chapter 65


Elisa stared at the glowing screen of her phone. The bright blue light illuminated the absolute horror unfolding in real-time.

She tapped the link Jewel had sent. It opened the Page Six homepage. The banner headline was printed in massive, bold black letters, designed to inflict maximum psychological damage:

THE CRAZED HOMEWRECKER: Chambers Heir Stalked by Deranged Nurse, Real Girlfriend Narrowly Escapes Vicious Attack.

Elisa felt a cold, heavy stone drop into the pit of her stomach.

Directly beneath the headline was a video player.Elisa tapped it.

It was the security footage from the Belmont terrace.But it had been maliciously, professionally butchered.The video started exactly at the moment Elisa's hand was on Allena's sleeve. It completely cut out Allena
blocking her path and grabbing her first.

The footage cut to black the exact millisecond before Allena threw herself backward,leaving the viewer with the undeniable impression that Elisa had launched her down the stairs.

Below the video were high-definition photos taken by the paparazzi. They showed August kneeling on the marble, cradling a bleeding, weeping Allena. In the background, Elisa was standing against the pillar, her face pale and expressionless. The caption read: The attacker shows zero remorse, staring coldly at her victim.

Elisa swiped out of the browser and opened Twitter.

The hashtag JusticeForAllena was dominating the trending list with over two hundred thousand tweets.
Elisa clicked on the tag. A tsunami of pure, unfiltered human malice flooded her screen.

"Look at that nurse's face. She looks like a complete psychopath. Lock her up!"

"I heard she's been obsessed with August Chambers for years. She's a desperate gold digger trying to ruin his real relationship."

"Allena Mills is a literal hero who saves lives, and this trash tries to kill her out of jealousy? Disgusting."

Elisa's breathing grew shallow. The sheer volume of the hatred was suffocating.

But the worst was yet to come. She scrolled down and saw a post from a massive gossip account with millions of followers.

"Internet do your thing. Who is this crazy nurse?"

Beneath that tweet, the replies were a digital firing squad. Elisa's full name. The address of the municipal hospital where she used to work. And then,a photo of the exterior of her current temporary apartment building in Manhattan-clearly taken by a paparazzi Devon had hired to tail her Porsche
directly from the resort gates.

Her personal information had been completely doxxed.

Suddenly, her phone vibrated against her palm. An incoming call from an unknown number.

Elisa answered it on instinct.

"You ugly, jealous bitch," a distorted, angry male voice screamed through the speaker. "You better watch your back. We know where you live. You're dead!"

Elisa didn't gasp. She didn't reply. She pulled the phone away from her ear and hung up.

Three seconds later, another unknown number called. Then a text message popped up: Go kill yourself. Then another call.

The phone was vibrating so fast it felt like a live wire in her hand.

Elisa walked over to the kitchen drawer. She pulled out a small metal pin, popped the SIM card tray out of her phone, and physically removed the tiny chip.She snapped the SIM card in half and threw it in the

trash.
The phone went dead silent. The apartment was suddenly,terrifyingly quiet.

Elisa leaned her hands flat against the cool marble of the kitchen island. She closed her eyes. Her brain,trained to analyze complex Al algorithms, rapidly processed the data points.

This wasn't a spontaneous internet mob. This was a highly coordinated, incredibly expensive smear campaign. The video editing, the instant trending hashtags, the rapid doxxing-it required massive capital and a dedicated bot network.

She walked over to her laptop, opened a secure,encrypted terminal, and immediately sent a data extraction request to Alastair. Five minutes later,Alastair's top-tier cybersecurity team fired back a detailed report. The trace on the initial IP addresses that seeded the video pointed to a routing node belonging to a shell PR firm registered in Delaware.A firm wholly owned by the Browning Venture Capital Fund.

Devon.

Devon was executing the hit, and August was absolutely allowing it to happen to punish her for refusing to be his PR shield.

The screen of her laptop suddenly flashed green. An incoming encrypted video call request from Alastair Cromwell.

Elisa clicked accept.

Alastair's face appeared on the screen. He was sitting in his glass-walled office at Aethel headquarters. The usual calm amusement in his eyes was gone, replaced by a dark, lethal seriousness.

"Faye," Alastair said, his voice tight. "The situation is critical. I have my cyber team trying to scrub your address from the forums, but it's spreading too fast."

He typed something on his end, and a secondary window opened on Elisa's screen. It was a live feed from the traffic camera on her street corner.

"Look at the feed," Alastair commanded.

Elisa looked. Parked directly across the street from her apartment building were two unmarked, black Ford transit vans. The windows were heavily tinted.
"They aren't paparazzi," Alastair said grimly. "Those are hired fixers. Devon is trying to physically intimidate you, or worse, provoke an altercation so they can film you acting 'crazy' for the police. I am dispatching Aethel's Tier-One security team right now. Pack a bag. We are moving you to a secure black site."

Elisa walked over to the living room window. She peeked through the narrow slit of the blinds. She could see the black vans idling in the dark. They looked like vultures waiting for a corpse.

A heavy, suffocating pressure pressed against her chest. If she ran now, if she hid in a bunker, the internet would take it as an admission of guilt. The narrative that she was a deranged stalker would become historical fact.

Elisa turned away from the window. She walked back to the laptop.

"No, Alastair," Elisa said. Her voice was terrifyingly calm. It was the voice of a woman who had nothing left to lose."If I run,they win."

Alastair frowned deeply. "Faye, you cannot fight a
multi-million dollar PR machine with logic. They control the narrative."

"They control the lies," Elisa corrected him, her black eyes locking onto the camera lens. "There is only one weapon that can instantly decapitate this entire campaign. Absolute, legally binding truth."

She needed to prove she wasn't a stalker. She needed to prove she was the legal wife.

"I am going to release my original Marriage Certificate to the New York Times," Elisa stated."With the state embossed seal. The second that document goes live, Allena's 'real girlfriend' narrative becomes the biggest joke in the country, and the defamation lawsuits will bankrupt Devon's firm."

"Do you have the original?" Alastair asked.

Elisa's jaw tightened. "No. It's locked inside a biometric safe in the master bedroom of the Chambers Manor in Long Island. I left in such a rush the night I moved out, prioritizing my core Al hard drives, that this critical document was left behind."②

She reached into the closet and pulled out a heavy,black tactical windbreaker. She grabbed a black
baseball cap and a medical face mask.

"Call off your security team, Alastair," Elisasaid,pulling the zipper up to her chin. "I'm going back into the snake pit."
###Chapter 66


The clock on the dashboard of the Porsche read 11:45 PM.

Elisa killed the headlights a quarter-mile down the road. She let the dark grey car roll silently on its momentum, turning off the main paved road and onto a hidden, unpaved service path that cut through the dense woods behind the Chambers Manor.

She parked the car deep in the shadows of the ancient oak trees. She stepped out into the freezing night air, pulling the black baseball cap low over her eyes and snapping the dark medical mask over her face.

She knew the security grid of this estate flawlessly.She had spent seven years navigating its suffocating rules.

Elisa moved through the trees like a ghost. She approached the towering stone wall at the rear of the property. She bypassed the main gate cameras,slipping through a narrow wrought-iron side gate
used exclusively by the landscaping staff. A chill went down her spine as the gate swung open without a sound. It wasn't just unlocked; it had been oiled.This wasn't an oversight. It was an invitation. A trap.

The massive, Gothic architecture of the Chambers Manor loomed in the dark, casting long, jagged shadows across the manicured lawns. The house was dead silent. Only a few security lights illuminated the perimeter.

She crept along the stone facade until she reached the servants' entrance near the kitchens. She pressed her thumb against the biometric scanner hidden beneath a fake electrical box.

The scanner flashed green. The heavy door clicked open. Germaine had deliberately left her access intact, banking on Elisa's desperation.

Elisa slipped inside. The air in the manor was cold and smelled faintly of lemon polish and old money.

She bypassed the grand staircase, opting for the narrow, spiraling wooden stairs used by the maids.Her rubber-soled shoes made absolutely no sound.She climbed to the third floor, her heart beating a
slow, steady, highly controlled rhythm.

She stepped out into the main hallway. The walls were lined with oil paintings of dead Chambers patriarchs, their painted eyes seeming to follow her in the dark.

She reached the heavy oak double doors of the master suite. She pushed them open and stepped inside.

The room was suffocatingly quiet. It smelled of camphor and dust. It was clear August hadn't slept in this room since she left; he had likely moved his things to a guest suite to avoid the memory of her.

Elisa didn't turn on the lights. The pale moonlight filtering through the massive windows was enough.

She walked straight past the king-sized bed and into the cavernous walk-in closet. She moved to the far back wall, stopping in front of a large, heavy mirror.

She pressed her hands against the edges of the glass and pushed upward. The mirror slid smoothly on hidden tracks, revealing a high-grade steel wall safe embedded in the concrete.
Elisa quickly punched in the six-digit code. It was a sequence only she and August knew.

A soft, mechanical click echoed in the quiet closet.The heavy steel door popped open.

She had personally installed this biometric safe,knowing it could be physically brute-forced, but the speed of this silent intrusion suggested something far more sophisticated. This wasn't a simple breach.This required resources far beyond the estate's security team. Germaine must have brought in external specialists.

Elisa reached her hand inside, feeling for the false bottom panel. She pressed the hidden latch. The panel lifted. She reached her fingers into the secret compartment, expecting to feel the thick, textured parchment of her Marriage Certificate.

Her fingertips scraped against cold, bare steel.

Elisa froze. Her breath caught in her throat.

She pulled her phone from her pocket and turned on the flashlight, shining the harsh white beam directly into the safe.
The secret compartment was completely, utterly empty.

A massive, terrifying wave of ice-cold dread crashed over her.

The documents hadn't just been moved. They had been surgically extracted.

Elisa's brain spun. August didn't do this. August was a sledgehammer; he didn't have the patience or the intellect to quietly rifle through a secret compartment without leaving a mess.

There was only one person in this entire estate who possessed the absolute authority, the cunning, and the access to execute a silent theft like this.

Suddenly, a blindingly bright light flooded the master bedroom behind her.

Elisa whipped around, her body instantly dropping into a low, defensve crouch, her muscles coiled like a spring ready to strike.
