Tonight was the official celebration gala for the JHU and Aethel Intelligence joint venture. It was a massive media event, designed to showcase the future of medical technology to the world's wealthiest investors.①

Elisa stepped out of the private elevator and into the ballroom foyer.

She was not wearing a lab coat tonight. She wore a custom-tailored, deep charcoal-grey evening gown.The fabric was heavy and matte, devoid of any cheap sequins or flashy cutouts. It draped perfectly over her frame, radiating a cold, untouchable,architectural elegance. She looked like a weapon forged from dark steel.

Alastair walked beside her, looking effortlessly
powerful in a classic black tuxedo.

As they entered the main hall, the ambient noise of the crowd washed over them. The room was packed with New York's elite.

Directly in the center of the room, standing under the largest chandelier, were August and Allena.

They were the absolute center of gravity.Photographers were snapping pictures rapidly.August was wearing a midnight-blue tuxedo that perfectly accentuated his broad shoulders. Allena was clinging to his arm, wearing a violently bright,crimson-red silk dress with a plunging neckline. It was a dress designed to scream for attention.

Their fingers were tightly interlaced. August was leaning down, whispering something into Allena's ear, making her throw her head back in a loud,theatrical laugh.

They were putting on a flawless, aggressive display of public romance.

Elisa stopped walking. She stood near a massive marble pillar, her black eyes locked onto her legal husband and his mistress.
A heavy, cold knot formed in her stomach. It wasn't jealousy. It was the visceral, sickening realization of her own public erasure. Legally, she was still August Chambers' wife. But in this room, under these lights,she was a ghost. August's eyes swept over the crowd, briefly landing on Elisa.

He didn't flinch. He didn't look away in shame. He simply let his gaze slide right past her, as if she were a piece of ugly furniture he had decided to ignore.

Allena, however, saw her. Allena immediately tightened her grip on August's bicep. She lifted her chin, her eyes locking onto Elisa with a look of pure,venomous triumph. She leaned closer to August,pressing her chest against his arm, making sure Elisa saw every millimeter of their intimacy.

"Don't look at them, Faye," Alastair murmured,stepping slightly in front of her to break the line of sight. He handed her a flute of champagne. "They are performing for the cameras. It's pathetic."

Elisa took the cold crystal glass. Her fingers were completely numb.

"I'm not looking at them," Elisa said, her voice a flat,
dead whisper. "I'm looking at a corpse."

The official presentations began. The host, a famous television anchor, took the stage to introduce the key players of the project.

"And representing the brilliant minds at Aethel Intelligence, the Chief Technical Advisor, Ms. Elisa Wilder!" the host boomed over the speakers.

A smattering of polite, tepid applause echoed through the room. A few people looked at her, but most quickly looked away, their eyes filled with the lingering judgment of the Page Six scandal.

"And joining the JHU team, made possible by the incredible generosity of the Chambers Group," the host continued, his voice rising in excitement."The brilliant Clinical Data Consultant, and the lovely partner of Mr. August Chambers... Dr. Allena Mills!"

The ballroom erupted. The applause was thunderous. People cheered. August raised his glass in a toast to Allena, kissing herpublicly on the cheek.

Elisa stood in the shadows, taking a slow sip of her champagne. The bubbles burned the back of her throat.
...	-.."Hear that?" Alastair asked quietly, his jaw tignt witñanger. "That applause isn't for her brain. It's for his bank account."

"I know," Elisa replied, her eyes completely devoid of warmth. "My applause doesn't come from sycophants.It comes from the data."

An hour later, the formal dinner concluded, and the guests began mingling freely. Elisa stood near a quiet corner of the bar, discussing the latency protocols with Professor Finch.

Suddenly, a heavy, arrogant presence invaded her personal space.

Cyprian Thatcher, August's closest friend and loyal attack dog, swaggered up to the bar. He was holding a glass of scotch, his face flushed with alcohol and malice.

"Well, well, well," Cyprian drawled, his voice loud enough to turn several heads. "If it isn't the 'Chief Technical Advisor'. Tell me, Ms. Wilder, how does it feel to be the least important person in the room?"

Elisa didn't turn her head. She kept her eyes locked on Professor Finch,completely ignoring Cyprian's
existence.

Cyprian's face darkened. He stepped closer, invading her physical space.

"You think that fancy title means anything?" Cyprian sneered,gesturing with his scotch glass toward the center of the room where August and Allena were holding court. "Look at them. That is power. That is the core of this project. You are just a diversity hire Cromwell brought in to make himself look good."

Elisa slowly turned around.

She looked at Cyprian. She didn't look angry. She looked at him with the terrifying, clinical detachment of a mortician examining a body.

"Mr. Thatcher," Elisa said, her voice dropping to a freezing, razor-sharp pitch. "The core of a project is built on functional algorithms, not public displays of biological fluid exchange."

Cyprian choked on his scotch. He coughed violently,his face turning bright red.

"You arrogant bitch," Cyprian hissed, wiping his mouth. "You better watch your mouth. You might
have a job today, but you will never be anything more than a discarded mistake. The position of 'Mrs.Chambers' belongs to Allena now. Everyone knows it."

Elisa let out a soft, chilling laugh. The sound made the hair on the back of Cyprian's neck stand up.

"Your definition of that title is incredibly narrow,Cyprian," Elisa whispered, stepping one inch closer to him. "If you think being 'Mrs. Chambers' is a prize,you are welcome to wear the collar. I prefer to own the building."

Before Cyprian could formulate a response, a bright flash of light blinded them.

A reporter from a major medical journal had approached, a cameraman trailing behind him.

"Ms. Wilder! Mr. Thatcher!" the reporter said eagerly."Can I get a quick quote? Ms. Wilder, as the technical lead, what are your expectations for this collaboration? Specifically, how do you feel about Dr.Mills joining the team?"

Cyprian smirked, stepping back to watch Elisa squirm.
Elisa turned to the camera. Her face instantly smoothed into a mask of perfect, terrifying professional grace.

"Technology is objective. Expectations are subjective," Elisa stated, her voice clear and ringing with authority. "My sole focus is ensuring the absolute viability and security of the Chiron architecture."

"And Dr. Mills?" the reporter pressed, sensing the tension.

Elisa's blackeyes flicked across the room, landing directly on Allena, who was watching the interview from afar, a smug smile on her lips.

Elisa looked back at the camera. The corners of her mouth curved up into a smile that was entirely made of ice.

"Aethel Intelligence," Elisa said slowly, emphasizing every lethal syllable, "always welcomes anyone who can actually contribute something real to the project."

Across the room, Allena's smug smile instantly shattered. The implication that she was fake,that
she contribouted nothing, hit her like a physical slap.August's face turned black with rage. He grabbed Allena's arm and violently pulled her away from the crowd,marching toward the exit.

Elisa watched them flee. She set her empty champagne glass down on the bar. The glass made a sharp,final clink against the marble.

The marriage was dead. The rot was complete. And she was finally, gloriously free to bury it.
###Chapter 78


The main ballroom began to thin out as the clock struck midnight. The heavy, suffocating energy of the gala shifted into the more relaxed, venomous atmosphere of the post-party.

Elisa retreated from the main floor, seeking the quiet of a dimly lit, velvet-lined lounge area near the hotel's massive arched windows. She sat alone in a high-backed armchair, the heavy charcoal fabric of her gown pooling around her legs. She held her phone in her hand, her thumb scrolling rapidly through a dense block of encrypted server logs. She was hunting for anomalies in the data ingestion ports.

She needed the distraction. The physical exhaustion of maintaining her armor all night was beginning to make her muscles ache.

The soft, rhythmic clicking of high heels approaching broke her concentration.

Elisa didn't look up immediately. She waited until the
scent of heavy, cloying floral perfumes assaulted her nose.

Three women stood in a tight semicircle around her chair. They were the apex predators of the Upper East Side socialite food chain. Wives of hedge fund managers and real estate tycoons. Their faces were pulled tight with expensive surgeries, and their eyes glittered with malicious, predatory curiosity.

"Ms. Wilder," the tallest one, a woman wearing a diamond necklace heavy enough to choke a horse,purred. "We didn't want to interrupt your... reading.But we simply had to come over and say hello."

Elisa slowly locked her phone screen.She placed the device face down on the small glass table next to her.She looked up, her face a mask of absolute, freezing polite indifference.

"Hello," Elisa said. A single, flat word.

The second woman, clutching a crystal martini glass,leaned in slightly.Her smile was sharp and cruel.

"The gala was simply stunning tonight," the woman said, her voice dripping with fake sweetness. "And didn't August and Allena look absolutely radiant?
They are just the most perfect, devoted couple. It's so rare to see that kind of genuine passion in our circles."

The third woman let out a soft, mocking sigh. "Oh,absolutely. You can just tell they belong together.It makes you wonder about... other arrangements.Doesn't it?"

The trap was set. They were circling her, waiting for her to bleed. They wanted her to scream, to cry,to act like the jealous, deranged homewrecker the tabloids claimed she was.

The tallest woman went for the throat.

"Ms. Wilder," she asked, her voice loud enough for the neighboring tables to hear. "Forgive my bluntness, but the rumors are so confusing. What exactly is your relationship with Mr. Chambers? Some people are actually whispering that you claim to be his wife. Is that some kind of... delusion?"

The insult wvas a physical strike. It was designed to humiliate her into the ground.

Elisa didn't flinch. Her heart rate didn't spike. She looked at the three women, taking in their eager,
VICIOus faces.
A slow, dark, incredibly cold smile spread across Elisa's lips. It wasn't a defensive smile. It was the smile of a predator watching three blind mice walk into a trap.

She reached out and picked up an empty crystal water glass from the table. She held it up, turning it slowly in the dim light, watching the facets catch the chandelier's glow.

"My relationship with Mr. Chambers?" Elisa repeated,her voice a soft, melodic hum that carried a lethal undercurrent.

She paused, letting the silence stretch until the three women leaned in closer, desperate for the gossip.

"Tell me, ladies," Elisa asked, her tone light and conversational. "Are any of you familiar with the concept of a 'Taxidermy romance'?"

The three socialites frowned, exchanging confused,blank glances.

"Taxidermy?" the woman with the martini glass asked, her nose wrinkling in disgust. "Like...stuffing
dead animals?"
"Precisely," Elisa whispered.

She set the glass down on the table with a sharp,definitive click. She leaned forward, her black eyes locking onto the women with terrifying intensity.

"A taxidermy romance," Elisa explained, her voice dropping to a freezing, surgical precision, "is an arrangement that looks exactly like love from a distance. The posture is perfect. The glass eyes are polished. The fur is brushed. It is posed in a beautiful, expensive glass cabinet for everyone to admire."

She turned her head slowly, looking across the vast expanse of the lounge.

"But if you get close enough," Elisa continued, her voice echoing softly in the quiet corner. "lf you actually touch it... y/ou realize the soul has been dead for years. The organs have been scooped out and replaced with cheap stuffing. It is nothing but a rigid,rotting corpse, desperately pretending to be alive just to impress the audience."

The three women physically recoiled. The color
drained from their perfectly contoured faces. The metaphor was so brutal, so vividly grotesque,that it made their stomachs turn.

Elisa looked back at them, her smile widening into something truly terrifying.

"Some people," Elisa said, her eyes flashing with dark amusement, "believe that if you hold hands tight enough for the cameras, it makes the corpse breathe. But in my professional opinion... it's just a very sad, very expensive museum exhibit."

The tallest woman's face flushed a violent,angry red.She realized she had just been intellectually slaughtered.

"Are you... are you implying that August and Allena are fake?" the woman hissed, her voice trembling with outrage. "You are just a bitter, jealous woman!"

Elisa shook her head slowly. The amusement vanished from her eyes, replaced by a look of profound, crushing pity.

"Jealous?" Elisa whispered. "No. I am just deeply,profoundly sorry for anyone who looks at a stuffed corpse and thinks they are seeing a heartbeat."
She stood up. The movement was so fluid, so commanding, that the three women instinctively took a step back to give her space. Elisa picked up her small clutch purse.

"Enjoy the rest of the exhibit, ladies," Elisa said.

She turned and walked away, leaving them standing in stunned, humiliated silence.

Elisa navigated through the tables, heading toward the grand staircase that led to the lobby. The adrenaline of the verbal execution was fading,leaving a cold,hollow ache in her chest.

As she passed the edge of the lounge, she stopped dead in her tracks.

Standing half-hidden in the heavy shadows of a marble archway, not twenty feet from where she had been sitting, was August. He had clearly moved there moments before, positioning himself just out of sight when he saw the women approach her, his curiosity a magnetic pull he couldn't resist.

He was holding a glass of bourbon. He was looking directly at her.
Elisa's breath caught in her throat. Her stomach violently contracted. He had been standing there the whole time. He had heard every single word of the "Taxidermy romance" speech. He had heard her call his grand, public love affair a rotting corpse.

She braced herseIf for the explosion. She waited for the rage, the shouting, the physical intimidation.

But August did absolutely nothing outwardly.

His face was completely blank, but deep within those dark eyes, a violent, freezing storm of humiliation and exposed truth was violently churning. He looked at her not as a stranger, but as a mirror reflecting his own rotting choices. The muscles in his jaw ticked as he ground his teeth together, suppressing the explosive urge to shatter the glass in his hand.

He didn't defend Allena. He didn't attack Elisa. He simply took a slow, rigid sip of his bourbon, burying that suffocating shame deep into his chest,turned his back on her, and walked away into the darkness of the hallway.

The stifling, aggressive silence hit Elisa harder than a physical punch to the face.
The air was punched out of her lungs. Her fingertips went completely numb.

If he had screamed at her, it would have meant an easy, predictable confrontation. It would have meant her words had merely scratched the surface. But his terrifyingly suppressed, white-knuckled reaction proved a much darker reality.

He knew she was right. And his refusal to engage meant the war between them had just escalated into something entirely unspoken and deadly.

She burst through the revolving doors of the Waldorf into the freezing New York night air. She took a massive, shuddering breath, filling her lungs with the cold wind.

Taxidermy romance, Elisa thought, her eyes hardening into two chips of black ice. You're right,August. It's dead. And tomorrow, I start digging the grave.
###Chapter 79


The freezing November wind whipped down Park Avenue, biting into Elisa's exposed skin. She stood on the edge of the curb outside the Waldorf Astoria,her dark shawl pulled tight across her chest. Her body was trembling slightly, a physical reaction to the adrenaline crash and the absolute, crushing apathy she had just witnessed from August.

She raised her hand, signaling the valet to call her a taxi. She just needed to get back to the safe house.She needed to see Kayden. She needed to touch something real and alive.

Before the valet could even move, a massive, sleek black Maybach glided silently out of the flow of traffic and stopped directly in front of her.

The heavy,tinted rear window rolled down with a soft mechanical hum.

Alastair Cromwell sat in the back seat. The harsh streetlights illuminated the sharp angles of his jaw and the warm, steady concern in his eyes. He had
shed his tuxedo jacket, his white dress shirt unbuttoned at the collar.

"Faye," Alastair said, his voice a low, calming anchor in the chaotic night. "Get in. It's freezing out there.I'm taking you home."

Elisa blinked, momentarily disoriented. "Alastair? I thought you had a late-night strategy meeting with the JHU board members."

"I canceled it," Alastair replied smoothly, pushing the heavy leather door open from the inside. "They were boring me. And you look like you're about to shatter.Get in the car."

The blast of warm, cedar-scented air escaping from the Maybach's cabin hit Elisa's face. It felt like a physical embrace. The exhaustion in her bones suddenly felt ten times heavier. She didn't argue. She stepped off the curb and slid into the plush leather seat next to him.

The heavy door pulled shut with a solid, isolating thud, instantly cutting off the howling wind and the noise of the city.

Elisa sank back into the leather. She closed her eyes,
letting out a long, shaky breath.

"Drink this," Alastair said softly.

Elisa opened her eyes. Alastair was holding out a small, insulated thermos cup. She took it. Her fingers brushed against his. His skin was warm and solid. A strange, unexpected flutter of comfort sparked in her chest. She unscrewed the lid; it was hot, strong black tea.

"Thank you,"' Elisa whispered, taking a slow sip. The heat spread down her throat, melting the ice in her stomach.

Suddenly, Alastair leaned across the center console.His face was inches from hers.

Elisa froze, her breath catching in her throat. But Alastair didn't look at her lips. He reached past her shoulder, his large hand grasping the heavy metal buckle of her seatbelt.

"You always forget this when you're tired," Alastair murmured, his voice a low rumble. He pulled the strap across her lap and clicked it securely into place. The movement was incredibly close, incredibly intimate,yet completely respectful.
Right at that exact second, the heavy brass doors of the Waldorf Astoria were pushed open.

August Chambers walked out onto the top of the stairs, followed closely by Allena and Cyprian Thatcher.

August was buttoning his overcoat when his dark eyes swept over the driveway. He stopped dead.

Through the massive, clear windshield of the Maybach, illuminated by the interior dome light,August had a perfect, unobstructed view of the back seat. He saw Elisa sitting there. He saw Alastair Cromwell leaning his entire body over her, his arm wrapped around her waist to secure the seatbelt.From August's angle, the physical proximity looked undeniably, intensely intimate.

August's pupils violently contracted.

A massive, burning spike of pure, irrational fury exploded in the center of his chest. The air was suddenly sucked out of his lungs. His hands curled into fists so tight his knuckles popped. The apathy he had felt in the lounge vanished, replaced by a territorial, violent jealousy that made his vision blur
red.
Allena, clinging to his arm, followed his gaze. Her eyes widened as she saw the Maybach.

A toxic, venomous thrill shot through Allena's veins.This was it. This was the proof she needed to destroy Elisa's professional credibility.

"Oh my god," Allena gasped loudly, her voice dripping with fake, theatrical horror. She violently yanked on August's sleeve, pointing her manicured finger at the car. "August, look! Look at her! She is practically sitting in his lap!"

Cyprian stepped up next to them, a nasty, knowing smirk twisting his face.

"Well, I'll be damned," Cyprian sneered, his voice loud enough to carry over the wind. "I guess we finally know how the 'Chief Technical Advisor'secured her position. Cromwell is certainly getting a return on his investment."

Allena leaned her head against August's shoulder,her voice wvhining with malicious delight. "It's disgusting, August. She is doing this right in front of you. She is just a cheap, manipulative...”(
August's entire body went rigid. He didn't shout.He didn't roar. He simply pulled his arm free from Allena's grasp with a sharp, cold motion that made her stumble.

His voice was a low, terrifying monotone, devoid of all warmth. He didn't look at her. He kept his burning eyes locked on the Maybach as it slowly pulled away from the curb.

Allena physically flinched, shocked by his sudden coldness toward her. She bit her lip, tears of frustration welling in her eyes.

August watched the red taillights of the Maybach disappear into the Manhattan traffic. His chest heaved. The image of Alastair touching Elisa was burning a hole in his brain.

"It's nothing," August lied through his teeth, his voice shaking with suppressed rage. He turned violently on his heel and marched toward his waiting SUV/. "Let's go."

Inside the Maybach, the atmosphere was completely different.

Elisa stared out the tinted window, watching the city
lights blur past. The hot tea had steadied her nerves.
"Thank you, Alastair," Elisa said quietly, breaking the silence. "For the ride. For the tea. For... everything tonight."

Alastair kept his eyes focused on the glass partition separating them from the driver.

"You don't need to thank me, Faye," Alastair said, his voice serious. "You are the most valuable asset Aethel Intelligence has ever possessed. Protecting you is my primary directive."

He paused, turning his head to look at her. The streetlights cast shifting shadows across his face.

"And," Alastair added, his voice dropping to a softer,more personal register, "I despise seeing you treated like collateral damage by men who don't possess a fraction of your intellect."

Elisa looked down at her hands, her fingers tracing the rim of the thermos. "I'm used to it. It's the cost of doing business in their world."

"It doesn't have to be," Alastair said suddenly.He shifted his body, turning fully toward her. "Faye, if
you ever decide you are tiredof fighting this war...Aethel will always be your sanctuary. Not just a laboratory. A place where you are actually valued. For everything you are."

The implication hung heavy in the warm air of the cabin. It wasn't a professional offer. It was a deeply personal, incredibly dangerous line being crossed.

Elisa's heart gave a strange, erratic thump. She looked into Alastair's eyes. She saw the genuine respect, the power, and the undeniable attraction.

For a long moment, the only sound was the hum of the tires on the asphalt.

Finally, Elisa offered a small, incredibly tired smile.

"Right now, my brain only has the capacity to execute Project Chiron and protect my daughter.As for the rest of my life... the foundation is still a pile of rubble. "I understand," Alastair said softly. "I'm a very patient architect, Faye. I can wait for the demolition to finish."


The primary executive boardroom at the JHU Medical School was a high-pressure cooker of academic tension and corporate stakes.

It was the first official integration meeting between the JHU clinical team and the Aethel engineering division. The massive mahogany table was covered in laptops, secure data drives, and thick stacks of patient privacy protocols.

Elisa stood at the head of the room. She had shed her blazer, her white dress shirt rolled up to the elbows. She held a black dry-erase marker,rapidly drawing a compex, branching flowchart on the massive glass whiteboard.

"The primary bottleneck is the ingestion rate of the raw MRI data," Elisa explained, her voice sharp and authoritative, cutting through the low hum of the room. "The JHU servers are throttling the upload speed.I need Professor Finch's team to bypass the secondary firewall and grant the Chiron algorithm direct,read-only access to the root directory."
Professor Finch nodded, typing rapidly on his laptop."Understood, Ms. Wilder. We can implement that bypass by tomorrow morning."

Alastair Cromwell sat at the center of the table, his hands steepled under his chin, watching Elisa work with a look of absolute, predatory satisfaction.She was brilliant, and she was dominating the room.

Sitting at the far end of the table, completely ignored by the actual scientists, was Allena.

She was wearing a severe, dark blue suit, trying to look intimidating. But her hands were shaking under the table. She had spent the last forty minutes listening to Elisa speak a language she couldn't comprehend.The jealousy and the humiliation from the gala the night before were boiling in her blood like acid.

She had cornered a junior JHU researcher,Cameron Fields, in the hallway just before the meeting. business, while a bad one courd see them blacklisted. Now, she only needed to give the signal.

"I... I have to agree," Cameron stammered, forcing himself to speak up to protect his backdoor connections. "If the Aethel lead is compromised by a personal relationship, it puts the JHU data at risk."⑤

The words inappropriate physical contact echoed in her head. Allena was actually trying to use Alastair buckling her seatbelt as proof that she was a whore.The sheer, astronomical audacity of a woman who was currently sleeping with a married man accusing someone else of sleeping their way to the top was staggering.

Elisa felt a hot, violent surge of pure rage ignite in her chest. She opened her mouth to verbally slaughter
Allena.

Crack.

Alastair Cromwell's open palm slammed down onto the mahogany table with the force of a sledgehammer. The heavy wood groaned. The coffee cups rattled violently in their saucers.

Alastair stood up.

The temperature in the boardroom seemed to instantly plummet by ten degrees. He didn't raise his voice, nor did he lose his polished tech CEO composure, but his eyes were completely black,radiating a terrifying, absolute coldness. The sheer,suffocating weight of his corporate dominance sucked all the oxygen out of the room.

He slowly walked around the table, his eyes locked onto Allena.

Allena's smug smile instantly vanished. Her breath caught in her throat. She instinctively took a step back, her knees hitting her chair.
"Ms. Mills," Alastair said. His voice wasn't a shout. It was a low, vibrating, demonic whisper that chilled the blood of every person in the room. "What exactly did you just say?"

Allena swallowed hard, her voice shaking uncontrollably. "I... I am protecting the project! We don't need someone who got their job by sleeping with the boss! It's a conflict of interest!"

Alastair stopped three feet away from her. He looked down at her with absolute, unadulterated disgust.

"You are accusing me of nepotism," Alastair stated coldly. "You are accusing me of compromising a multi-billion dollar Al architecture for a sexual favor."

He let out a short, terrifying laugh.

"Ms. Mills," Alastair said, his voice rising in volume,echoing off the glass walls. "Ms. Wilder is the sole creator of the Chiron algorithm. She built the architecture you are currently too stupid to comprehend. She is the most brilliant mind in this hemisphere. Her contract with Aethel is ironclad,and her authority in this room is absolute."

He leaned in, his face inches from Allena's terrified
eyes.
"I protect talent," Alastair stated, his words piercing the air like shards of ice. "I protect genius. What l do not protect, and what I will not tolerate in my presence, is a parasitic, incompetent fraud who tries to project her own pathetic, whoring reality onto a woman infinitely superior to her. Aethel's legal department will be filing a formal complaint regarding this defamatory outburst with the JHU board of trustees and the medical ethics committee, Ms. Mills.I imagine that will be enough to end your academic career."②

The insult was a nuclear bomb.

Allena's face turned a violent, sickening shade of purple. Tears of absolute, crushing humiliation flooded her eyes. Her mouth opened and closed,but no sound came out. She had been publicly, brutally executed.

Alastair turned his back on her, completely dismissing her existence. He looked at Professor Finch.

"Professor," Alastair commanded, his voice returning
to a cold, professional steel. "If this woman speaks another word in this room, Aethel Intelligence will terminate this partnership, pull our servers, and sue JHU for breach of professional conduct. Do I make myself clear?"

Professor Finch didn't hesitate for a microsecond. He looked at Allena with deep disgust.

"Crystal clear, Mr. Cromwell," Professor Finch said firmly. "Ms. Mills, you will remain silent for the remainder of this project, or you will be physically removed from the building by security."

Allena let out a loud, hysterical sob. The absolute rejection, the total destruction of her power play,broke her mind.

She shoved her chair back so violently it crashed to the floor. She grabbed her purse and sprinted toward the heavy double doors, tears streaming down her face. She shoved the doors open and ran down the hallway, the sound of her weeping echoing behind her.

Cameron Fields looked terrified. He quickly gathered his laptop and scrambled out of the room, chasing
after her.

The heavy doors swung shut. The boardroom was quiet again.

Elisa stood by the whiteboard. Her heart was beating a fast, steady rhythm. She looked at Alastair. He was standing by his chair, adjusting his cuffs, his breathing slowly returning to normal.

He had just burned a bridge with the Chambers family to the ground, entirely to protect her honor.

Elisa picked up her dry-erase marker. She turned back to the whiteboard, her hand perfectly steady.

"Now," Elisa said, her voice calm and absolute. "Let's discuss the bypass protocol."
###Chapter 81


"I can't believe he said that to me."

"It must be nice," the woman sneered to the guy sitting next to her. "Some of us have to publish to get ahead.I hear others just need to secure a favorable 'seat assignment' on the way home from a gala."

sniper scope.

If I ran this code, it would misdiagnose a stage-three tumor as a benign cyst.You are playing with human lives, and your math is at a high-school level."

The entire room stared at the screen. The female researcher's face drained of color.

The air inside the JHU Dean's office was thick with the smell of stale coffee and absolute panic.

The Dean slammed the heavy phone receiver down onto its cradle. He had just finished a screaming match with the university's board of directors. Sweat beaded on his forehead, dripping down his temples.

If Aethel Intelligence pulled the Chiron license tomorrow morning, the project was dead. If the project died, the Dean's career was over.
