###Chapter 83


The silence inside Elisa's apartment was heavy and cold.

She sat on the edge of her sofa, staring at the small paper calendar on the coffee table. Tomorrow's date was circled in thick red ink.

It was her grandfather Phineas's eightieth birthday.

A sharp ache twisted in her chest. According to the rigid rules of the Chambers family and the dying embers of their marriage contract, August was legally obligated to attend this milestone event to maintain the public facade.⑤

Elisa closed her eyes, fighting the wave of physical nausea that rolled through her stomach. The thought of asking the man who had just paid fifty million dollars to protect her abuser to come and cut a cake with her grandfather was a violation of her very soul.But the contract was iron, and her grandfather's fragile happiness was paramount.
She bypassed her contact list entirely. There would be no direct communication. She opened a new,secure email draft on her laptop. The recipient was not August, but her own corporate attorney.

The message was brutally efficient:

"To: Marcus Thorne, Esq.

Subject: URGENT - Enforcement of Marital Agreement Clause 4.2

Marcus,

Please inform Mr. Chambers, via his legal counsel, of his mandatory contractual obligation to attend the 80th birthday celebration for Phineas Wilder. Event details are attached. Failure to comply will result in immediate legal action for breach of contract. All further communication is to be conducted through our respective legal offices.

Regards,

E.Wilder"

She attached the Plaza Hotel invitation and hit send.It was a cold, impersonal, legal missile launched into August's world. She wouldn't beg. She would
command.

Less than an hour later, her phone buzzed. It was an incoming call from an unknown number. She let it go to voicemail. A moment later, a text message appeared from her attorney.

"Thorne: Simon, August's EA, is trying to reach you directly. He says Mr. Chambers wishes to speak with you urgently. I advised him all contact must be through me. He is insisting."

Elisa stared at the message, a cold knot tightening in her stomach. August was bypassing the lawyers.He was escalating.She knew what was coming next.

Her phone rang again. This time, the screen flashed:August Chambers. He was using his private, unlisted number.

Elisa's blood ran cold. He was refusing to play by the rules. Her fingers were stiff as she finally swiped to answer, pressing the phone to her ear.

"Hello?"

The voice wasn't August's deep baritone. It was high,breathy, and dripping with venomous satisfaction. It
was Allena.

Elisa's grip on the phone tightened until her knuckles turned stark white.

"Are you calling to ruin someone else's evening,Elisa?" Allena purred, her voice lazy and arrogant."Or are you just lonely?"

in the background, Elisa heard a soft, distinct electronic chime. Ding-dong. It was the custom notification sound of the smart-home system in the Manhattan penthouse.

The reality hit Elisa like a physical punch to the throat. Allena wasn't just answering his phone. She was standing inside the penthouse. She had moved into Elisa's home.

Elisa forced her breathing to remain perfectly steady.She refused to give the parasite the satisfaction of hearing her bleed.

"Put August on the phone," Elisa demanded. Her voice was a flat, freezing blade of ice. "This is regarding a mandatory clause in the family agreement."
Allena let out a soft, mocking laugh.

"I can't do that," Allena sighed, her tone dripping with fake pity. "He's in the shower. We just had a very... intense evening. He is completely exhausted.I'm certainly not going to bother him with your pathetic legal threats."

Elisa's fingernails dug so deeply into her paim that the skin nearly broke. The image of them together in her bed burned behind her eyes, but she shoved it down into the dark, freezing vault in her mind.

"Allena," Elisa warned, her voice dropping to a lethal whisper. "Do not play games with this. Put him on the phone."

The cold authority in Elisa's voice shattered Allena's fake sweetness. The mistress's insecurity flared into vicious anger.

"You are just a discarded piece of trash!" Allena shrieked into the receiver. "He doesn't want you!Stop harassing him, you pathetic bitch!"

Click.
The line went dead.
Inside the luxurious master bedroom of the penthouse, Allena stared at the darkened screen of August's phone. Her chest heaved with toxic adrenaline. She quickly navigated to the recent calls list.

She tapped Elisa's name. She hit Block Caller. Then,she swiped left and permanently deleted the call record from the history.

She tossed the phone casually onto the nightstand just as the bathroom door opened. August walked out, a towel wrapped low around his waist, water dripping from his dark hair.

Elisa sat in her apartment, listening to the dial tone.She pulled the phone away from her ear, her brow furrowing. She immediately hit redial.

We're sorry, the number you have reached is temporarily unavailable.

Elisa stared at the screen. The automated voice was a bucket of ice water poured directly over her head.

She was blocked.

In seven years of marriage, through all their

screaming matches and cold wars, August had never blocked her number. It was a line he had never crossed.

A suffocating wave of isolation crashed over her. She knew Allena had done it. But the fact that August allowed his mistress to handle his private, encrypted phone meant he had completely surrendered his boundaries to her. It was his silent permission.

Elisa threw the phone onto the sofa. She grabbed her laptop and quickly typed out an urgent email to Simon, August's lead executive assistant, cc'ing her lawyer.

She hit send. Three seconds later, a notification popped up.

Automatic Reply: Mr. Chambers is currently on a private holiday. All correspondence will be reviewed upon his return.

Elisa stared at the screen, her vision blurring slightly.She slammed the laptop shut.

Her grandfather was sitting in a memory care facility,his mind fading, waiting for his family to celebrate his eightieth year on earth. He was her only soft spot. He

was the only piece of her heart that wasn't covered in armor.

A cold, hard certainty settled in her gut. August wouldn't come. He would use this wall of silence to punish her for her defiance at JHU. She was on her own.

She stood up and walked to the massive floor-to-ceiling window. She looked out at the glittering,sprawling lights of Manhattan. The city looked cold,sharp, and completely indifferent to her pain.

Miles away in the penthouse, August dried his hair with a towel. He glanced at the nightstand.

"Did my phone ring?" August asked casually.

Allena walked over and wrapped her arms around his waist, pressing her cheek against his bare chest."It was just a spam caller. I hung up for you."

August didn't check the screen. He just nodded, his mind distracted by the soft pressure of her body. He felt a strange, nagging sensation in the back of his mind, like he was forgetting something important,but Allena's lips on his neck quickly erased it.

Elisa stood by the window for exactly one hour. The tears never fell. The pain in her chest slowly hardened into a solid block of black ice.

She walked back to the coffee table. She picked up her phone and dialed the events manager at the Plaza Hotel.

She would pay for the banquet herself. She would stand there alone. She opened her contacts list,found August's name under 'Emergency Contact',and hit delete.

The marriage was legally ending in a few days, but in Elisa's heart, the man was already dead.6


The morning sun sliced through the blinds of Elisa's apartment, casting sharp, prison-like shadows across the hardwood floor.

Elisa stood by the kitchen island, dressed in a sharp,black tailored suit. She was reviewing the final catering checklist for the Plaza Hotel. Her phone,resting on the marble counter, suddenly vibrated violently.

The screen flashed: August Chambers.

Elisa stared at the name. Her heart didn't race. Her stomach didn't flutter. She felt absolutely nothing but a cold, clinical exhaustion.

She let it ring four times. Then, she slowly reached out and swiped the screen.

"Yes?" Elisa answered, her voice as flat and hard as a concrete wall.

"Elisa," August's voice came through the speaker. He sounded irritated, with a faint, unnatural edge of
guilt. "Simon said your lawyer sent a notice last night. What do you want?"

Elisa's eyes narrowed. "I tried to call your private number. Why did you block me?"

In the back seat of his moving Maybach, August shifted uncomfortably. He glanced sideways at Allena, who was pretending to read a magazine next to him. When he woke up and saw Elisa's number on the block list, he had been furious, but Allena had immediately started crying, spinning a ridiculous story.

August cleared his throat. He chose to protect the lie.

"My security protocol automatically blocks any number flagged as a potential nuisance," August said dismissively, his voice cold. "Perhaps you should reflect on the tone of your lawyer's communications.Now,what was the emergency?"

Elisa let out a short, breathy laugh. It was a sound of pure,unadulterated disgust.

The lie was so perfectly corporate, so arrogant, so designed to put the blame entirely on her, that it was
almost impressive. It was a far more effective insult than a childish excuse.

"Is that right?" Elisa said, her voice dripping with lethal sarcasm. "Well. Your protocol is certainly very...efficient."

August felt a flush of anger hit the back of his neck.He hated when she used that tone with him. He hated that she saw right through him.

"Don't start with the attitude, Elisa," August snapped."Just tell me what this is about."

Elisa closed her eyes. She took a slow, deep breath,locking her emotions away.

"Today is my grandfather Phineas's eightieth birthday," Elisa stated coldly. "The banquet is at the Plaza Hotel tonight at seven. According to Section 4of our marriage agreement, you are requiredto attend family milestone events for a minimum of two hours."

August froze.

He looked down at the Rolex on his wrist, checking the date. A sharp, ugly jolt of genuine guilt punched

him in the gut. He had completely forgotten. He had spent the last month so obsessed with Allena and fighting Alastair Cromwell that his wife's dying grandfather hadn't even crossed his mind.

"Right," August muttered, rubbing his forehead."I...I remember. Fine. I will be at the Plaza at seven."

Sitting next to him, Allena's head snapped up. Her eyes went wide.

"The Plaza Hotel?" Allena gasped, grabbing August's arm. She leaned in close to the phone, making sure her voice was loud enough for Elisa to hear. "August,you can't go to a boring old man's party! Today is Tate's birthday! My parents rented the Grand Ballroom at the Plaza for him! You promised you would come!"

August winced. He looked at Allena's pleading,desperate eyes.

He was trapped. But then his arrogant logic took over. Both parties were in the exact same building.He could easily show his face at the old man's dinner for thirty minutes, then spend the rest of the night drinking champagne at Tate's massive gala.

"It's fine, Allena," August said smoothly, patting her hand. "I can do both. I'll stop by Tate's party first."

On the other end of the line, Elisa heard every single word.

She heard the mistress whining. She heard her husband instantly prioritize a spoiled seven-year-old over her dying grandfather. The sheer, suffocating disrespect made her blood run freezing cold.

"I'll see you tonight, Mr. Chambers," Elisa said.

She didn't wait for him to reply. She pulled the phone away and hit end.

In the Maybach, August stared at the disconnected screen. The words Mr. Chambers echoed in his head.It sounded so sterile. So permanent. A sudden,violent wave of anxiety washed over him, but Allena immediately kissed his cheek, distracting him with praise.

Elisa tossed her phone onto the counter. She walked into her bathroom and turned on the faucet.She splashed freezing cold water onto her face, gripping the edges of the porcelain sink until her knuckles ached.

She looked at her reflection in the mirror. Her dark eyes were completely dead.

This is the last time,Elisa swore to herself. This is the absolute last time I ever depend on that man for anything.

She grabbed her dark sunglasses, slid them onto her face to hide the shadows under her eyes, and walked out the door.

Meanwhile, fifty miles away in a heavily secured house in Long Island.

Jewel stood in the sunlit kitchen, gently brushing the soft, dark hair of a five-year-old girl. Kayden sat on a stool, swinging her legs excitedly.

"Auntie Jewel," Kayden chirped, her bright, intelligent eyes shining. "Today is Great-Grandpa's birthday,right?"

"It sure is, sweetie," Jewel smiled, tying a small ribbon into the girl's hair.

Kayden clutched a hand-drawn birthday card to her chest. "I want to givehim a giant surprise! And Mommy too!"
Jewel chuckled, completely unaware of the terrifying genius hiding behind the little girl's innocent smile."I'm sure they will love whatever you made, baby.I need to go check the laundry.Stay right here."

The second Jewel walked out of the kitchen,Kayden's innocent smile vanished, replaced by a look of intense, terrifying focus that perfectly mirrored her mother's.

Kayden slid off the stool. She grabbed Jewel's iPad from the counter. Her tiny fingers flew across the screen with shocking speed. She opened a voice-command app she had been secretly training for weeks, feeding it recordings of Jewel's voice from bedtime stories.

"Hey Siri," Kayden whispered, her voice a perfect,high-pitchedimitation of Jewel's. "Call Delta Airlines."

The call connected. Using a series of practiced,innocent-sounding phrases, Kayden navigated the automated phone tree. When she finally reached a human agent, she spun a tale of a frantic single mother trying to book a last-minute flight for her daughter to see a sick relative, her voice filled with a

child's coached panic. She used the auto-fill credit card information Jewel used for groceries to pay,spelling out the numbers with painstaking clarity.

She booked a standard seat, knowing the Unaccompanied Minor service would be flagged at the airport. That was part of the plan.

A confirmation email pinged on the iPad. A green checkmark appeared on the screen.

A nuclear bomb had just been launched, and it was heading straight for Manhattan.⑤
###Chapter 85


At exactly 10:00 AM, Elisa stepped through the revolving gold doors of The Plaza Hotel on Fifth Avenue.

She walked with the sharp, commanding stride of an executioner. She bypassed the opulent lobby and headed straight for the executive banquet offices.She had paid for the Grand Ballroom-the most prestigious room in the hotel-six months ago, using the first massive paycheck she had earned as Faye.

She pushed open the glass door to the Banquet Manager's office.

The manager, a balding man in a tight suit, looked up from his computer. The moment he saw Elisa, his professional smile vanished. His face instantly paled,and a thick sheen of nervous sweat broke out on his forehead.

"Ms. Wilder," the manager stammered, quickly standing up and wiping his hands on his trousers. "I...I was just about to call you."

Elisa stopped in the center of the room. Her stomach gave a slow,warning twist.

"Is there a problem with the floral arrangements?"Elisa asked, her voice flat and demanding.

The manager swallowed hard, his eyes darting nervously toward the door. "No, ma'am. The flowers are fine. It's... it's the venue. We experienced a catastrophic glitch in our booking software. The Grand Ballroom has been double-booked."

Elisa's eyes narrowed behind her dark sunglasses.

"A glitch," Elisa repeated, the word dripping with ice."I paid in full six months ago. I have the signed contract. A software glitch is your problem, not mine."

The manager physically cowered. "I am so incredibly sorry. But the other party... they have priority status.We have been forced to move your grandfather's banquet to the Oak Room down the hall."

Elisa's jaw locked. The Oak Room was a third of the size. It had low ceilings and no crystal chandeliers.It was a massive, humiliating downgrade.

It was Arthur Sinclair.
Allena's father. And Elisa's biological father.

Elisa's entire body went completely rigid. The blood drained from her face, rushing to her ears with a deafening roar. Her lungs seized.

Arthur Sinclair didn't even look at her. He didn't recognize the tall, imposing woman behind the dark sunglasses. To him, she was just a random, annoying obstacle in his path. He looked directly at the sweating manager.

"The Grand Ballroom better be ready," Arthur barked,his voice booming with authority. "My grandson Tate is turning seven today. I want those Dutch tulips arranged perfectly around the cake table. I will not tolerate any mistakes for my boy."

The words hit Elisa like a physical sledgehammer to the chest.

My grandson.

Her biological father, the man who had abandoned her and her dying mother to starve in the Appalachian mountains twenty years ago, had just

used his billionaire status to steal her grandfather's venue... to throw a birthday party for his mistress's grandchild.

A wave of pure, homicidal hatred violently exploded in Elisa's chest. Her hands curled into fists so tight her fingernails pierced the skin of her palms. She wanted to scream. She wanted to grab the heavy brass lamp on the desk and smash it into his arrogant face.

The manager looked at Elisa, his eyes wide with silent, desperate pleading. Please don't cause a scene.

Elisa bit down on the inside of her cheek until she tasted copper.

She couldn't explode now. If she caused a scene, the hotel would call security.The banquet would be canceled entirely. Her grandfather would have nowhere to go. She had to swallow the razor blades.She had to endure it.

Elisa slowly turned her head. She looked at the manager,ignoring Arthur completely.

"I will accept the Oak Room," Elisa said. Her voice

was shaking slightly, vibrating with suppressed violence. "But I demand an absolute, physical guarantee that your security team monitors the main public hallways. My guests will not interact with the chaotic crowd in the Grand Ballroom. Keep them entirely segregated."

The manager let out a massive sigh of relief. "Yes!Absolutely, Ms. Wilder. I guarantee it."

Arthur Sinclair finally turned his head and looked at Elisa. He let out a harsh, mocking scoff.

"Smart girl," Arthur sneered, looking her up and down with absolute disdain. "You finally realized how the real world works. In New York, capital speaks.Learn your place."

Elisa's spine snapped straight. She slowly turned to face the man who gave her life.

She tilted her chin up. Behind the dark lenses of her glasses, her eyes were burning with black fire.

"Capital can certainly buy a ballroom, Mr. Sinclair,"Elisa said, her voice a freezing, lethal whisper that cut through the room. "But it clearly cannot buy basic human decency. Or a legitimate bloodline."

Arthur's face instantly darkened. His eyes flashed with sudden, violent anger. He took a step toward her."What did you just say to me?"

Before he could unleash his rage, his cell phone rang.

Arthur stopped. He pulled the phone from his pocket and looked at the screen. His furious expression instantly melted into a sickeningly sweet, doting smile.

"Allena, my darling," Arthur answered, his voice dripping with fatherly love. "Yes, the room is secured.Everything is perfect for little Tate."

He turned his back on Elisa and walked out of the office, his bodyguards trailing behind him.

Elisa stood frozen in the center of the room. The sound of him calling Allena "darling" made her stomach violently heave.

She spun around and grabbed the manager by the lapels of his suit, slamming him back against the wall.

"Listen to me," Elisa hissed, her face inches from his."If the service in the Oak Room is anything less than perfect tonight, I will personally ensure that you

never work in this city again. Do you understand?"

"Yes! Yes, ma'am!" the manager gasped.

Elisa shoved him away. She turned and marched out of the office, heading toward the Oak Room to inspect the damage.

She pushed open the heavy wooden doors of the smaller venue. It was dark and cramped.

Just as she stepped inside, her phone vibrated in her pocket.

She pulled it out. It was a text message from Jewel.

Elisa read the words on the screen, and her heart completely stopped beating.

ELISA, CALL ME NOW. KAYDEN IS GONE. SHE'S AT THE AIRPORT.
###Chapter 86


The dim lighting of the Oak Room seemed to spin around Elisa.

She stood frozen on the patterned carpet, staring at the glowing screen of her phone. The words blurred together. Kayden is gone. At the airport.

A cold, paralyzing terror gripped her throat,completely suffocating her. A five-year-old child.Alone. At a commercial airport.

Elisa's thumb violently hit the call button. She pressed the phone to her ear, her hand shaking so hard she could barely hold the device.

"Jewel!" Elisa gasped the second the line connected.Her voice was high, frantic, completely stripped of her usual cold armor. "Tell me this is a joke. Where is she?!"

On the other end of the line, Jewel was sobbing hysterically.

"I'm so sorry, Elisa!" Jewel cried, her voice echoing

with panic. "The airline flagged her at the security gate because she didn't have an adult! She cried and manipulated a sympathetic gate agent into calling me! I panicked, Elisa! If I said no, they would have called airport security and Child Protective Services!Her identity would be all over the news! I had to authorize the unaccompanied minor fee over the phone just to get her on the plane and keep her contained!"

Elisa's knees buckled. She had to lean heavily against a wooden dining chair to keep from collapsing.

Her brilliant, genius daughter had used her intellect to build a bomb.

"What time does the flight land?" Elisa demanded,forcing her brain to override the panic.

"In less than an hour!" Jewel sobbed. "I'm already in the car heading to the private airfield. I'm taking a helicopter to Manhattan, but I won't make it before she lands!"

Elisa's chest heaved. She looked at her watch. 6:00PM.

Her grandfather's banquet was starting in thirty minutes. Arthur Sinclair's massive birthday party for Tate was starting right next door. August Chambers was currently in transit to this exact hotel.

If Kayden landed at JFK, took a cab, and walked into the lobby of The Plaza Hotel...

If August saw a five-year-old girl with his exact dark eyes, his exact jawline, calling Elisa "Mommy"...

The seven-year secret would detonate. August would realize he had an heir. He would use his billions, his lawyers, and his absolute ruthlessness to rip Kayden out of Elisa's arms forever. It was a fate worse than death.

"I'll handle it," Elisa said, her voice dropping to a terrifying, deadly calm. "Get here as fast as you can."

She hung up the phone. She immediately dialed the private security firm Aethel Intelligence kept on retainer. She ordered a four-man extraction team to speed to JFK, but the Friday night traffic was a nightmare. They might not make it in time.

She couldn't leave the hotel. If she left, her grandfather would arrive and be completely alone,

vulnerable to the cruel mockery of Arthur Sinclair and his family.

She was trapped in a suffocating, impossible nightmare.

Suddenly,her phone vibrated again. It was Alastair.

Elisa answered it, unable to hide the ragged,desperate sound of her breathing.

"Faye?" Alastair's deep voice was instantly alert."What's wrong? You sound like you're suffocating."

Elisa closed her eyes. A tear leaked out, burning a hot trail down her cheek. She couldn't tell him the whole truth. She couldn't expose Kayden's paternity.

"Alastair," Elisa choked out. "I have a massive crisis.A... a very important young relative of mine. She booked a flight by herself. She's landing at JFK in forty-five minutes. I can't leave the hotel to get her. If she wanders into the city..."

"Say no more," Alastair's voice was calm and absolute. "I am currently in a board meeting, but my Chief of Security is at JFK right now, extracting another client. He is exactly ten minutes from her

gate. I am dispatching him immediately. He will intercept her and bring her directly to my secure armored vehicle."

Elisa let out a loud, shuddering sob of pure relief."Alastair...thank you. Thank you."

"Breathe, Faye," Alastair commanded softly."I've got her.Focus on your grandfather."

Elisa hung up and rapidly texted him Kayden's photo and flight details.

She wiped her face, forcing her expression to harden back into stone.

The heavy doors of the Oak Room slowly pushed open.

Phineas was wheeled into the room by a private nurse. The old man was wearing a neat,pressed suit.His eyes were cloudy with dementia, but a wide,happy smile stretched across his wrinkled face.

"Elisa, my beautiful girl," Phineas croaked, reaching his trembling hands out toward her. "Are the guests here? Is it time for cake?"

Elisa's heart shattered into a thousand pieces. She

rushed forward and knelt beside his wheelchair,taking his frail hands in hers.

"They are coming, Grandpa," Elisa whispered, forcing a bright, warm smile onto her face. "It's going to be a wonderful party."

Just as she spoke, a massive, deafening blast of bass music vibrated through the floorboards.

The wall separating the Oak Room from the Grand Ballroom shook. Tate's birthday party had started.The sound of a live DJ and screaming children bled through the cheap partition walls, completely shattering the quiet dignity of the Oak Room.

Phineas winced, pulling his hands away from Elisa to cover his ears. "Why is it so loud? Is there a storm?"

Elisa stood up. She glared at the adjoining wall with a look of pure, homicidal fury. The hotel manager had lied. There was no isolation.

She turned to the nurse. "Lock the main doors. Do not let anyone in unless they are on my specific guest list."

Downstairs, in the opulent main lobby of The Plaza.

The revolving doors spun, and August Chambers walked in. He was wearing a stunning midnight-blue tuxedo. He looked like a king arriving to inspect his kingdom.

He didn't look at the directory for the Oak Room. He didn't ask the concierge where his wife's grandfather was celebrating.

He followed the sound of the loud music, walking straight toward the Grand Ballroom to fulfill his promise to Allena.

Upstairs, Elisa stood by her grandfather, her eyes locked on the locked doors. The countdown had reached zero. The bomb was in the building.
###Chapter 87


By 7:30 PM, the Oak Room felt like a beautifully decorated tomb.

Only six guests had arrived. They were elderly friends of Phineas from decades ago, people who didn't care about the tabloid scandals or the Chambers family politics. They sat around a single,large circular table, sipping tea and speaking in hushed,uncomfortable tones.

Because Elisa had been publicly stripped of her status as the "perfect Chambers wife," the wealthy socialites who usually flocked to these events had conveniently found excuses to be elsewhere.

Elisa stood at the head of the table. She wore a forced, gentle smile, carefully slicing a piece of vanilla sponge cake for her grandfather.

"Here you go, Grandpa," Elisa said softly, placing the plate in front of him.

Phineas smiled vaguely, picking up his silver fork. But

his hands trembled, and he kept glancing nervously at the wall.

The noise from the Grand Ballroom next door was deafening. It wasn't just music anymore; it was the sound of a massive, opulent crowd cheering, the clinking of hundreds of champagne glasses, and the booming voice of an MC.

Inside the Grand Ballroom, the atmosphere was electric.

Tate's birthday had morphed into a massive networking gala for Arthur Sinclair. August Chambers was the absolute center of gravity.

Allena clung to August's arm, wearing a glittering gold dress that caught the light of the massive crystal chandeliers. She laughed loudly at a joke made by a hedge fund manager, pressing her body against August to ensure everyone saw his protective arm around her waist.

Rosalind Mills, Allena's mother and Arthur's partner,glided through the crowd with a glass of champagne.She stopped in front of August, her eyes gleaming with predatory satisfaction.

"August, darling," Rosalind said loudly, ensuringthe surrounding guests could hear. "We are so thrilled you could make it. You have been such a rock for our Allena. Tell me, when are you two going to make this.,. official?"

August didn't flinch. He didn't deny it. He simply offered a smooth, arrogant smile and took a sip of his bourbon. His silence was taken as an absolute confirmation of an impending engagement.

Rosalind's smile widened into a vicious grin. She turned and walked toward the stage.

She tapped the microphone. The loud feedback silenced the room.

"Ladies and gentlemen," Rosalind announced, her voice echoing through the massive speakers. "Before we cut Tate's cake, I want to share a very special presentation. A celebration of love and family."

The main lights in the ballroom dimmed. A massive,floor-to-ceiling LED screen behind the stage flared to life.

It was a video. A highly produced, professionally edited clip from an upcoming exclusive interview with

Vanity Fair.

On the screen, August and Allena sat close together on a plush velvet sofa. August was looking at Allena with a soft, manufactured intensity. The interviewer's voiceover declared them "New York's most resilient and passionate power couple."

But Rosalind wasn't satisfied with just showing the video to her guests. She wanted blood.

She smirked, remembering the call she'd made to the hotel's chairman earlier that day-a man whose company relied on Sinclair financing. A "technical request" to broadcast the audio feed to the adjacent room was a small price for his continued prosperity.

Suddenly, inside the quiet, somber Oak Room, the hidden ceiling speakers crackled violently to life.

The hotel's centralized audio system had been hijacked.

The booming, crystal-clear audio of the Vanity Fair interview blasted directly into the Oak Room,drowning out the classical background music Elisa had playing.

"Allena is my absolute priority," August's deep,resonant voice echoed from the ceiling, filling every corner of the small room. "She is the most special presence in my life. She deserves the best of everything."

Phineas violently jumped in his wheelchair. The silver fork slipped from his trembling fingers and clattered loudly onto the china plate.

The six elderly guests froze. They looked up at the ceiling in shock, then slowly turned their heads to look at Elisa. Their eyes were filled with a sickening mixture of deep pity and extreme awkwardness.

Elisa stood frozen by the cake table.

The words hit her like a barrage of physical bullets.It wasn't the declaration of love that hurt-she knew he didn't love Allena, he only loved the control. What shattered her was the absolute, public humiliation being inflicted upon her grandfather on his eightieth birthday.

"Elisa..." Phineas whispered. His cloudy eyes were wide with confusion and fear. He grabbed her wrist with his cold, frail hand. "Elisa... is that August? WVhy

is he talking to someone else? Where is he?"

A hot, burning wave of pure, unadulterated humiliation crashed over Elisa. Her throat closed up.The edges of her vision turned black.

She could endure the beatings. She could endure the financial threats. But she would absolutely not allow these parasites to torture an old man whose mind was already failing him.

Elisa ripped her wrist from her grandfather's grasp.

She lunged toward the corner of the room where the audio control panel was mounted to the wall. She didn't look for a volume knob. She didn't try to press buttons.

She grabbed the thick bundle of black audio cables running from the wall into the amplifier, planted her feet, and violently ripped them out.

Sparks flew. A loud, screeching pop of static echoed through the room, and then... dead silence.

Elisa stood there, her chest heaving violently. She dropped the torn cables onto the floor. Her hands were shaking.

She turned around. She walked back to her grandfather, forcing her facial muscles to relax into a soft, reassuring expression. She knelt down in front of his wheelchair.

"I'm sorry, Grandpa," Elisa whispered, fighting the tears burning in her eyes. "The hotel's speakers are broken. It's too noisy here. Let's go somewhere quiet to eat your cake, okay?"

Phineas nodded slowly, still looking frightened.

Elisa stood up. She looked at the six guests and offered a deep, respectful bow. "Thank you all for coming. I am so sorry, but the banquet is over."

The guests nodded sympathetically, quickly grabbing their coats to escape the suffocating tension.

Elisa grabbed the handles of the wheelchair. She pushed her grandfather toward the side exit door,fleeing the room like a defeated soldier retreating from a massacre.

Next door, the video ended. The Grand Ballroom erupted into thunderous applause.

August stood in the center of the cheering crowd. He
smiled, but as the applause died down, he noticed the heavy silence coming from the other side of the wall.

He frowned. He realized how loud the audio had beeń. He realized it had likely bled through the walls.

A sudden, sharp spike of unease pierced his chest.He set his bourbon glass down on a passing waiter's tray. He pulled his arm away from Allena.

"I'll be right back," August muttered.

He walked quickly out of the ballroom and down the hall. He pushed open the double doors of the Oak Room.

He stepped inside.

The room was completely empty. Half-eaten slices of cake sat on the tables. The audio cables were ripped out of the wall, dangling like dead snakes.

August stared at the empty room, a cold, heavy dread settling into the pit of his stomach. He had missed it. He had ruined it.
###Chapter 88


The service corridor leading to the side exit of The Plaza Hotel was dimly lit and smelled of industrial cleaner.

Elisa pushed Phineas's wheelchair rapidly over the thin carpet. Her jaw was locked so tight her teeth ached. She just needed to get him into a taxi. She needed to get him back to the safety of the memory care center before his fragile mind completely shattered from the stress.

As they approached the main public lobby intersection leading to the exit doors, a loud,obnoxious shrieking echoed down the hall.

Tate Mills, wearing a miniature custom tuxedo,had slipped past the hotel staff and came sprinting around the corner from the public lounge. He was holding a plastic laser gun that flashed bright red lights, completely ignoring the frantic concierge trying to corral him.

He was running full speed, looking backward over his
shoulder.
"Watch out!" Elisa shouted.

She yanked the heavy wheelchair backward with all her strength, but it was too late. Tate slammed hard into the side of the metal wheel.

The impact sent the seven-year-old sprawling onto the floor. The wheelchair violently jerked. Phineas let out a terrified cry, his frail body slumping sideways against the armrest.

Elisa immediately dropped to her knees, checking her grandfather. "Grandpa! Are you okay? Does anything hurt?"

Phineas was shaking, clutching his chest, but he nodded weakly.

Elisa stood up. The adrenaline and humiliation from the Oak Room boiled over into pure, protective rage.She looked down at Tate, who was sitting on the floor,rubbing his knee.

"Be careful!" Elisa snapped, her voice sharp and authoritative. "You could have seriously injured him."

Tate didn't look apologetic. The spoiled,entitled brat
looked up at Elisa with a face full of pure, learned malice. He scrambled to his feet, dusting off his expensive tuxedo pants.

"Watch where you're going, you stupid lady!" Tate yelled. He pointed his plastic laser gun directly at Phineas's face. "He smells gross! My mom says you guys are just poor bloodsuckers! Why doesn't that old zombie just die already?!"

The words hung in the stale air of the corridor.

Elisa's vision went completely red. The absolute,unforgivable cruelty of the child-parroting the exact venom of his mother and sister-snapped the final thread of her self-control.

She moved with a speed that was almost inhuman.She didn't strike him. Instead, her hand shot out and clamped around the boy's wrist. Her fingers applied precise, calculated pressure to a nerve cluster just below his palm. Tate let out a yelp of surprise, not pain, as the plastic gun clattered from his suddenly numb fingers onto the floor.

Elisa leaned down, her face inches from his, her voice a terrifyingly cold whisper that cut through his
childish bluster.

"If you ever use that mouth to speak about my family again," she hissed, "I will make sure you can't speak at all."

For one second, there was absolute silence.

Then, Tate, shocked and frightened by the cold threat more than any physical blow, opened his mouth and unleashed a deafening,hysterical wail.He clutched his tingling wrist, his face turning red.

The heavy doors at the end of the hall burst open.

Arthur Sinclair and Rosalind, who had been searching for the boy, rushed into the corridor. When Rosalind saw Tate screaming on the floor, she let out a piercing shriek and threw herself onto her knees,pulling the boy into her arms.

"Tate! Oh my god, my baby!" Rosalind screamed.She looked up at Elisa, her eyes wide with demonic fury. "What did you do to him?! You monster!"

Arthur Sinclair's face turned a violent, mottled purple. He charged forward, his massive fists clenched.
"You psychotic bitch!" Arthur roared. He raised his heavy hand, fully intending to strike Elisa across the face.

Elisa didn't flinch. She didn't step back.

With terrifying speed, her hand shot up. She caught Arthur's wrist in mid-air. Her fingers locked around his pulse point with the crushing, iron grip of a trained fighter.

Arthur gasped, shocked by the physical strength of the woman standing in front of him.

Elisa stepped into his space. She looked directly into the eyes of the man who had abandoned her.

"What's the matter, Mr. Sinclair?" Elisa hissed, her voice dropping to a surgical, lethal whisper."Are you going to strike me, just like you struck my mother when she begged you not to sell her sunflowers?"

The words hit Arthur like a bolt of lightning.

His entire body froze. His arm went limp in her grip.He stared dowwn into Elisa's face. He looked past the dark sunglasses, past the cold exterior, and saw the exact shape of her jaw, the exact curve of her

cheekbones. He saw the ghost of the woman he had left to die in the mountains twenty years ago.

"Elisa...?" Arthur breathed, his voice trembling with sudden,absolute shock.

Behind him, Rosalind's face drained of all color. She stopped comforting Tate. She stared at Elisa in pure,unadulterated terror. The secret she had buried for two decades had just been dragged into the light.

At that exact moment, August and Allena rushed around the corner.

They stopped dead in their tracks. August heard the name Sinclair. He looked at Arthur's shocked face,then at Elisa. A massive, tectonic shift of pure,unadulterated fury occurred in August's brain. He already knew the twisted bloodline-he had personally forced Elisa to play the loving sister to Allena in front of the press-but he never expected her to weaponize it here. She was dragging the family's most grotesque, buried secret into the open hallway,threatening to shatter the immaculate public facade he had spent millions building. The sheer,reckless audacity of her insubordination made him physically sick.

Allena grabbed August's arm, her face pale with panic. She knew it was over.

Arthur Sinclair recovered from his shock. His survival instinct kicked in. To acknowledge Elisa here, in front of everyone, would destroy his reputation and his marriage.

He violently yanked his arm out of Elisa's grip. His face twisted with ugly, desperate denial.

"You are insane!" Arthur shouted, pointing a shaking finger at her. "I don't know who you are! You are a violent, uneducated savage! You are no daughter of mine!"

Elisa looked at him. She didn't cry. She let out a slow,chilling laugh that made the hair on the back of August's neck stand up.

"You are absolutely right," Elisa said, her voice ringing with absolute, terrifying clarity. "My name is Elisa Wilder. My father died twenty years ago in the dirt. You people are nothing but parasites squatting in a stolen nest. You don't even have the right to speak my name."

She slowly turned her head and looked directly at

August.
August's chest was heaving. He stared at her, waiting for her to scream at him, to demand an explanation.

But Elisa just looked at him with eyes that were completely,totally dead.

"Mr. Chambers," Elisa said, her voice a flat, freezing monotone. "Your taste in garbage is truly spectacular.You and this family are a match made in hell."

August opened his mouth, but his throat was paralyzed. He couldn't speak. The absolute, final disgust in her eyes was a physical blade cutting the last cord between them.①

Elisa turned around. She grabbed the handles of the wheelchair. She pushed her grandfather past the stunned billionaires, her spine perfectly straight,walking out the side doors into the freezing New York night like a conquering queen leaving a burning city.

In the back of a speeding yellow taxi, Elisa held her grandfather's trembling hand.

A single, silent tear slipped out from beneath her

sunglasses and rolled down her cheek.
Suddenly, her phone vibrated.

She answered it.

"Faye," Alastair's deep, steady voice came through the speaker. "My security team has her. She is in the back of my armored car right now.She is completely safe."

Elisa closed her eyes, letting out a long, shuddering breath. The war was just beginning, but her heart was finally secure.
###Chapter 89


The Ivy League Memory Care Center was silent at 11:00 PM.

Elisa stood by the edge of the hospital bed, watching the slow, rhythmic rise and fall of her grandfather's chest. The nurses had administered a mild sedative to calm his racing heart after the trauma at the hotel.

Elisa rubbed her temples, her head throbbing with a vicious migraine. She mentally reviewed every step of her exit from the Plaza, ensuring she hadn't left a single clue that could lead August to Alastair, or worse,to Kayden.

A soft, hesitant knock on the heavy wooden door broke the silence.

The night nurse pushed the door open, her eyes wide and nervous. She stepped aside.

August Chambers walked into the room.

He was still wearing his midnight-blue tuxedo, but the bow tie was gone, and the top buttons of his shirt
were undone. He smelled faintly of expensive bourbon and cold night air.

But what made Elisa freeze was what he was holding.In his large hands, he carried a heavy/, antique velvet jewelry box.

Elisa instantly stepped in front of the bed, her body shifting into a defensive, aggressive stance.

"Get out," Elisa hissed, keeping her voice low so as not to wake Phineas. "You have absolutely no right to be here."

August didn't snap back. He didn't raise his voice.He looked at her with an expression she hadn't seen in years-a complex, desperate mix of guilt, exhaustion,and a sickening attempt at appeasement.

He walked slowly to the small seating area near the window and gently placed the box on the coffee table.

"I know what happened tonight was unacceptable,"August said, his voice a low, rough rumble. "I didn't know Rosalind was going to play that audio. I didn't know it was wired to your room."

He reached out and opened the velvet box. Inside,resting on faded silk, was an exquisite, one-of-a-kind sapphire and diamond necklace. The deep blue of the center stone seemed to drink the dim light of the room.

Elisa's breath caught in her throat. Her pupils dilated in absolute shock.

It was her mother's. The one piece of fine jewelry she hadn't sold, the one she'd worn on her wedding day.It had been stolen during a break-in a year before her death, and Elisa had always suspected the "burglary"was arranged to cover a gambling debt. She thought it was lost forever.

"How..." Elisa whispered, unable to tear her eyes away from the glittering sapphires.

"I have resources," August said quietly, watching her reaction closely. "When I realized your grandfather's party was ruined, I made some calls. I have had my people tracking this on the black market for years. It surfaced in a private collection in Geneva last month.I wanted to bring it to him. To you. As an apology."

Elisa stared at the necklace. The memories of her

mother's soft laughter and the cool weight of the stones rushed into her brain. Her eyes burned.

But then, the cold, analytical mind of Faye slammed the emotional vault shut.

She looked up from the necklace and stared directly into August's eyes. She saw the calculation behind his "gift." He was terrified of losing his grip on her.He had witnessed her absolute detachment at the hotel, and he was using a priceless emotional artifact to chain her back to him.

"An apology," Elisa repeated, her voice dripping with venomous sarcasm. "You let your mistress's family publicly humiliate an eighty-year-old man with dementia. You let a seven-year-old boy curse him to his face. And you think a piece of jewelry fixes that?"

August's jaw tightened. "I handled Rosalind. And I disciplined the boy. It won't happen again."

"And Allena?" Elisa challenged, stepping closer to him. "Did you discipline her? Did you tell her to pack her bags and get out of my penthouse?"

August looked away, his silence a deafening confirmation. He couldn't let Allena go. His ego

needed her constant adoration.

Elisa let out a cold, hollow laugh. "You are pathetic,August. You want to play the loving husband while keeping your pet on a leash. It makes me sick to look at you."

A soft groan came from the bed.

Phineas slowly opened his eyes. The sedative was wearing off. He blinked against the dim light, turning his head toward the voices.

His cloudy eyes bypassed Elisa and landed directly on the coffee table.

Phineas gasped. A sudden, brilliant light of recognition flooded his wrinkled face. He pushed himself up on his elbows, his trembling hand reaching out toward the glittering necklace.

"Mary's necklace..." Phineas wwhispered, tears instantly welling in his eyes. "My Mary's wedding necklace...it came back."

August immediately stepped forward. He walked to the edge of the bed and dropped down onto one knee, placing himself directly in Phineas's line of

sight.

He took the old man's frail hand in his. He completely dropped his billionaire arrogance, replacing it with the gentle,respectful tone of a perfect grandson.

"Happy birthday, Grandpa," August said softly. "I promised I would find it for you. I brought it back."

Phineas began to weep openly. He squeezed August's hand with surprising strength. "You are a good boy, August. Such a good boy. Thank you."

Standing a few feet away, Elisa felt a wave of absolute, terrifying revulsion wash over her.

She watched August manipulate an old man with dementia, using a stolen heirloom to buy affection and paint himself as a savior. It was textbook psychological abuse. He was trapping her by making her family love him.

August stood up. He turned to Elisa, a faint,victorious smirk playing on his lips.

See? his eyes seemed to say. He loves me. You can't cut me out.

"I will have my lawyers send over the transfer of
ownership documents tomorrow morning," August said,adjusting his cuffs.

Elisa didn't scream. She didn't throw the box at him.She simply nodded slowly.

"I'll accept it," Elisa said coldly. "Consider it the penalty fee for your breach of contract tonight. Now,get out."

August frowned, clearly unsatisfied that she hadn't melted into his arms. He opened his mouth to argue,but his cell phone vibrated violently in his pocket.

He pulled it out. The screen illuminated his face. It was Allena.

August didn't hesitate. He answered it right in front of Elisa.

"Allena, I'm busy," August said, though his voice was soft. He listened for a moment. "I know you're upset about the hotel. I'll be home soon. Just wait for me."

He hung up the phone and looked at Elisa. "I have to go. Allena is having a panic attack."

Elisa pointed a rigid finger at the heavy wooden door."Don't let the door hit you on the way out."
August shook his head, turning toward the exit. He reached out and grabbed the brass door handle.

Just as he pushed the door open, Phineas, still staring lovingly at the sapphire necklace, spoke again.

His voice was clear, loud, and completely unfiltered by his broken memory.
###Chapter 90


"The little girl is going to love it so much," Phineas mumbled happily, his eyes crinkling as he smiled at the glittering jewels.

August's hand froze on the brass door handle.

He stopped pushing the door. The silence in the room suddenly became heavy, thick, and incredibly dangerous.

"When are you bringing her to see me, Elisa?"Phineas continued, his voice taking on a wistful,pleading tone. "The house is too quiet. You and August should really have a baby... give me a beautiful little great-grandchild..."

A little girl.

August's entire body went rigid. The words hit his brain like a physical electric shock. He slowly released the door handle. He turned around.

His dark eyes, previously filled with arrogant manipulation, were now sharp, focused, and burning
with a terrifying, predatory suspicion. He stared at the old man in the bed, then slowly shifted his gaze to Elisa.

"A little girl?" August repeated. His voice was a loW,vibrating rumble that shook the air in the room."What little girl? And why is he talking about you bringing her here?"

Elisa's heart violently slammed against her ribs. The blood drained from her face so fast she felt dizzy.The room tilted.

Her greatest nightmare was unfolding in real-time.The secret she had protected with her life, the daughter she had hidden for five years, was being casually exposed by a man who didn't even know what year it was.

She dug her fingernails into her palms, using the sharp physical pain to force her brain into overdrive.She could not panic. If she panicked, Kayden was dead.

Elisa let out a harsh, bitter laugh. She rolled her eyes,injecting her voice with pure, exhausted mockery.

"Are you seriously interrogating a man with stage-
three Alzheimer's, August?" Elisa sneered, stepping between August and the bed.

She pointed a finger at her grandfather. "He doesn't even know who you are half the time! He thinks Arthur Sinclair is still his son-in-law! The 'little girl'he's talking about is me. He thinks it's 1995 and I'm still five years old playing in the dirt!"

August narrowed his eyes. He stepped away from the door, slowly walking back toward the center of the room. He was evaluating her face, searching for the micro-expressions of a lie.

Phineas's dementia was severe. Time-jumping was a common symptom. The explanation was perfectly logical.

But August was a paranoid, controlling billionaire.He remembered the locked doors. He remembered her absolute refusal to let him see her medical records.He remembered the way she guarded her phone.

He closed the distance between them. He backed Elisa up until her shoulders hit the cold plaster wvall.He placed a heavy hand on the wall right next to her head, trapping her in his shadow.
"You have been hiding things from me for years,Elisa," August whispered, his face inches from hers.His breath smelled of bourbon and danger. "If I find out you have been keeping a secret like that from me..you know exactly what I am capable of."

Elisa didn't shrink away. She tilted her chin up, her black eyes burning with absolute defiance.

"I hide a lot of things from you, August," Elisa hissed,her voice dripping with venom. "Like how much l physically despise you. Like how your touch makes my skin crawl. Do you want to investigate that too?"

The air between them crackled with violent,explosive tension. August's jaw clenched. He was inches away from completely losing control.

Bzzzt.Bzzzt.

Elisa's phone, tucked into the pocket of her slacks,vibrated.

It wasn't a standard ring. It was two short, sharp bursts. It was the emergency physical proximity signal she had set up with Alastair.

It meant Alastair was here. It meant Kayden was here.
They were outside the building.

If August walked out of this room right now, if he went down to the lobby or the parking lot, he would walk right into them.

Total, absolute panic seized Elisa's lungs. She had to get him out of here, and she had to make him run in the opposite direction.

She shoved both of her hands against August's chest, pushing him away with violent force.

"Get out!" Elisa screamed, injecting every ounce of her genuine terror into a mask of hysterical anger. "I am sick of your paranoia! I am sick of your face! Take your necklace and go back to your pathetic, whining mistress!"

August stumbled back a step, shocked by her sudden physical outburst. His ego flared. He hated being yelled at.

"You are losing your mind, Elisa," August sneered,straightening his jacket. "Keep the necklace. And keep your mouth shut."

He turned sharply toward the door.
Just as his hand reached the brass handle again, his own cell phone erupted with a loud, piercing ringtone.

August yanked it out of his pocket. He answered it angrily."What?"

"Sir, you need to see this now." It was Simon, his voice tight with an unprecedented level of urgency."There's a leak. A major one. The financial blogs are reporting that Aethel Intelligence just filed a patent for a predictive algorithm that perfectly mirrors the core function of Project Chiron. Our stock is projected to open down twenty percent. They're accusing us of corporate espionage."

August's face went completely, deathly pale. The suspicion, the anger, the interrogation-everything vanished in a microsecond. This was a direct nuclear attack on his company.

"Cromwell," August snarled into the phone. "I'm on my way."

He didn't wait for an answer. He ripped the heavy wooden door open and sprinted out of the hospital room, running down the corridor with frantic,
desperate speed.
The door slammed shut behind him.

Elisa's knees instantly gave out. She collapsed onto the linoleum floor, her back sliding down the wall.She gasped for air, her entire body shaking violently.Cold sweat poured down her spine.

She had survived. The bomb had ticked down to zero, and Alastair's corporate warfare had magically cut the wire.

Elisa scrambled to her feet. She pulled her phone out and dialed Alastair.

"Where are you?" Elisa demanded, her voice breathless.

"Underground parking garage, Level B," Alastair's calm voice replied. "I just saw August's Maybach tear out of the exit ramp like a bat out of hell. The perimeter is clear."

Elisa didn't say goodbye. She hung up, sprinted out of the room, and bolted for the service elevator.

She hit the button for Level B. The doors slid open to the dark, concrete expanse of the underground
garage.
Parked in the shadows of a concrete pillar was a sleek,black SUV.

The heavy rear door swung open.
A tiny figure with dark hair and bright, intelligent eyes launched herself out of the vehicle.

"Mommy!"

Kayden hit Elisa like a cannonball. Elisa dropped to her knees on the dirty concrete, wrapping her arms around her daughter's small body, burying her face in the girl's hair. She held her so tight her muscles ached, tears of absolute, overwhelming relief finally pouring down her face.
She was safe. The secret was safe.④
###Chapter 91


The smell of motor oil and damp concrete filled Elisa's lungs.

She crouched swiftly beside the vehicle, her arms locking tightly around Kayden's small body. She pressed her face into her daughter's dark hair for a single, grounding second.The physical heat radiating from the five-year-old's skin was the only thing keeping Elisa's heart beating, but she forced the maternal panic down, letting the cold, calculating edge of her alter ego take over. Every second they lingered here was a liability.

Jewel stood next to the open door of the black armored SUV. She did not look at them, giving Elisa a fleeting moment of privacy. Her eyes scanned the concrete pillars and the exit ramp, her posture rigid with protective alertness.

Elisa's earpiece crackled. "Faye," Alastair said. His voice was a low, urgent rumble over the secure comms."We need to move. August is paranoid. If he realizes the corporate leak was a distraction,he will
turn that Maybach around and come straight back here."

Elisa's spine stiffened. The cold reality of the threat instantly froze her tears.

She pulled back. She wiped her face with the back of her hand, forcing her facial muscles into a calm,reassuring mask. She stood up and lifted Kayden,placing her gently into the back seat of the SUV.Jewel was already behind the wheel, the engine idling with a heavy, powerful hum.

Kayden reached out. Her tiny fingers locked onto the fabric of Elisa's coat. Her bright eyes were wide with confusion.

"Pourquoi tu ne viens pas, Maman?" Kayden asked,her voice trembling in flawless French. Why aren't you coming,Mommy?

Elisa's stomach twisted into a violent knot. She covered Kayden's small hand with her own.

"I have to stay and take care of Great-Grandpa,baby," Elisa lied, keeping her voice perfectly steady."He is very sick. I will come home to Long Island tomorrow.I promise."
Kayden pouted, but she slowly released the coat.

Elisa stepped back. She looked at Jewel in the driver's seat and gave a single, sharp nod. Jewel nodded back, her face pale but determined. She shifted the SUV into gear. The heavy tires squealed against the concrete as the vehicle shot toward the exit ramp, merging instantly into the dark Manhattan night.

Elisa stood in the empty parking space.She watched the red taillights disappear. She took a slow,deep breath,pulling the freezing air into her lungs until her chest ached.

She turned and walked to the service elevator.

Ten minutes later, she pushed open the door to Phineas's room. The room was completely silent.Her grandfather had fallen back into a deep, medically induced sleep.

Elisa sat down in the vinyl chair next to the bed. Her brain immediately shifted into analytical overdrive.

She replayed the phone call August had received. A predictive algorithm perfectly mirroring Project Chiron. Corporate espionage. A twenty percent stock
drop. Alastair had executed a flawless, devastating financial strike just to pull August out of this room.

The silence stretched on. The digital clock on the wall ticked forward.

Less than ten minutes had passed.

Suddenly, the silence was shattered by a loud,piercing electronic chime.

Elisa flinched. She pulled her phone from her pocket.The screen flashed with Jewel's name.

A heavy, sickening drop occurred in Elisa's stomach.Jewel knew she was supposed to maintain absolute radio silence until they reached the Long Island safe house.Jewel never broke protocol.

Elisa swiped the screen and pressed the phone to her ear.

"Jewel?"

She did not hear Jewel's calm voice. She heard the deafening wail of police sirens. She heard the crunch of metal and the chaotic shouting of angry voices.
And beneath it all, she heard a high-pitched, ragged sobbing.
"Elisa!" Jewel screamed into the phone. Her voice was completely hysterical, breaking on every syllable. "He hit us! A red sports car ran the light at the Queensboro intersection! He T-boned the SUV!"

All the blood instantly drained from Elisa's face. Her fingers clamped around the phone with bone-crushing force.

"Where is Kayden?" Elisa roared, her voice tearing her throat.

"She hit her head on the window!" Jewel sobbed,gasping for air. "There is so much blood, Elisa! She was terrified, and now she is burning up! She has a massive fever!"

Elisa shot up from the chair. She moved so fast the blood ruushed from her brain. A violent wave of dizziness hit her. The room tilted. She slammed her free hand down onto the metal bed frame, gripping it until her knuckles turned white to keep from collapsing.

"Call an ambulance right now," Elisa commanded, her voice dropping to a terrifying, mechanical coldness.

"I tried!" Jewel cried. "But the other driver called the
police first! The cops are here, and they won't let us leave! They locked us in the back of a cruiser!"

The temperature in Elisa's veins dropped to absolute zero.

"I am coming," Elisa said.

She hung up the phone. She grabbed her coat and sprinted out of the room.

She ran down the hospital corridor, her boots slamming against the linoleum. As she ran, her thumb jabbed at her phone, sending a single,encrypted text to Dr. Gottlieb Blevins: 'My daughter.Emergency.' There was no time for more. She hit the speed dial for Alastair.

"Faye?" Alastair answered instantly.

"Queensboro intersection crash. NYPD precinct,"Elisa barked, her breathing ragged. "Send your best criminal defense lawyer right now."

She didn't wait for a reply. She burst through the hospital lobby doors and threw herself into the back of a waiting yellow taxi.

Ten minutes later, the taxi slammed on its brakes in
front of the NYPD precinct. Elisa threw a fifty-dollar bill at the driver and kicked the door open.

She sprinted up the concrete steps and shoved the heavy glass doors open.

The precinct lobby was a chaotic nightmare of pale fluorescent lights, ringing phones, and shouting civilians. Elisa's black eyes swept the room with the precision of a sniper.

She found them in the far corner.

Jewel was sitting on a hard wooden bench. A thick white bandage was taped to her forehead, soaking through with dark blood. Her arms were wrapped tightly around a small bundle of clothes. Two massive, stone-faced police officers stood directly in front of her, blocking her in.

Elisa shoved a civilian out of her way and marched across the room.

"Move," Elisa hissed at the officers.

Before they could react, she pushed her way between them. She dropped to her knees in front of Jewel. She reached out with shaking hands and
pulled back the heavy fabric of the jacket.

Kayden's face was flushed a dark, unnatural red. A thick bandage was wrapped around her head,stained with fresh blood. Her eyes were squeezed shut. Her chest heaved with rapid, shallow breaths.Her tiny fingers were locked into the fabric of Jewel's shirt in a death grip. She was muttering delirious,incoherent sounds.

Elisa pressed her palm against Kayden's forehead.The skin was boiling hot. It felt like touching a live radiator.

Elisa's heart physically shattered. The pain in her chest was so sharp she could barely breathe.

She stood up. She turned around and faced the two police officers. The grief in her eyes vanished,replaced by a black,homicidal rage.

"Why is a bleeding, feverish child sitting in a police station instead of a trauma ward?" Elisa demanded.Her voice was not loud, but it vibrated with a lethal intensity that made the officers step back.

The taller officer recovered his posture. He lifted his chin arrogantly.
"Paramedics are en route, but until they arrive and clear the scene, nobody leaves this precinct," the officer stated with a practiced, bureaucratic coldness. "The other party has filed a formal complaint of attempted hit-and-run. We are required to hold all involved individuals for preliminary questioning."

Elisa let out a harsh, barking laugh. She pointed a rigid finger at Jewel.

"Look at her!" Elisa yelled, her control finally snapping. She pulled her phone out and shoved the screen into the officer's face. It showed a photo Jewel had sent of the black SUV. The entire passenger side was caved in."The impact is on the side of her vehicle! She was T-boned! She is the victim!"

"That is not what the other party claims," the officer sneered.

At that exact moment, the heavy glass doors of the precinct lobby were violently shoved open.

Four men in expensive black suits marched into the room. In the center of the formation was August
Chambers.
He was wearing his long cashmere overcoat. His arm was wrapped tightly around Allena's shoulders,pulling her flush against his side in a posture of absólute, aggressive protection. Allena did not have a single scratch on her face.

Elisa froze.

She looked at her husband. She looked at the way his large hand shielded his mistress from the chaotic room.

Then, she slowly turned her head and looked down at her five-year-old daughter, bleeding and burning with fever on a hard wooden bench.

The last remaining cell of human emotion inside Elisa Wilder died. Her eyes turned into two pits of absolute, freezing black ice.
###Chapter 92

August kept his arm firmly around Allena as they crossed the precinct lobby. His jaw was locked tight. He scanned the chaotic room, his eyes sweeping past the vagrants and the shouting lawyers.
Then, his gaze landed on the far corner. He saw Elisa standing there.

Trailing right behind them was Tate, flanked by a pale,trembling private chauffeur. The seven-year -old boy was wearing a designer jacket. A tiny,cartoon-themed band-aid was stuck to his forehead. He did not look traumatized. He looked bored and incredibly arrogant.

"Did your driver run the red light in the McLaren?"Elisa asked. Her voice was flat, carrying the mechanical precision of a lie detector.
