She stood up from the table and walked to the large window overlooking the dark beach. The moon was hidden behind clouds, leaving the ocean a black,churning void.

"Why is he doing this?" Elisa asked, though she already knew the answer. She wasn't asking Jewel;she was thinking out loud, dissecting the strategy."It's not just about showing off his new toy. August doesn't do anything without a business reason."

"Because he's obsessed with her?" Jewel offered,her tone bitter.

"No," Elisa said, her mind sharpening with every word. "It's because she's vulnerable. Allena's
medical background is a house of cards. The host of Capital Minds is famous for eviscerating guests.He'll smell the blood in the water the second she opens her mouth about clinical data."

She paused, the pieces clicking into place. "If Allena stumbles on live TV, Vanguard's stock tanks. The JHU deal falls apart. August can't let that happen.He's not going as her boyfriend. He's going as her shield."

"Her shield?" Jewel repeated,confused.

"He's sending a message to the host, to the investors, to everyone," Elisa said, her voice dropping to a cold whisper. "He's telling them: 'If you attack her, you attack me.' He's leveraging the entire Chambers empire to protect her lies."

Jewel let out a shaky breath on the other end of the line. "My God. You're right. This is Wall Street warfare."

"Yes, it is," Elisa said, a new, dangerous resolve hardening her features. "And if he wants to stand on that stage and be her shield... then I'll just have to be the one to break it."
"Elisa, what are you going to do?"
"I'm going to do what I should have done a long time ago," Elisa said, her gaze fixed on the dark horizon."I'm going to burn her down. Professionally. Publicly.And'there won't be a damn thing his money can do to stop it."

She hung up the phone. She didn't go back to the interview specs. Instead, she opened a different file-a secure, encrypted drive that Alastair had given her weeks ago.

It contained the raw, unredacted data from Allena Sinclair's time at Johns Hopkins. Every patient file.Every research note. Every discrepancy.

Elisa started to read. She wasn't looking for gossip.She was looking for the fatal flaw. The lie that would unravel everything. She knew it was there. Allena was too arrogant, too lazy to cover her tracks perfectly.

And when she found it, she would use it. Not with anger. Not with tears. With the cold, hard precision of a surgeon excising a tumor.

Meanwhile, miles away in Manhattan, a black town car was parked across the street from The Willow
Creek Center for Child Development.
August sat in the back seat, the engine idling. He stared at the building-a beautiful, ivy-covered brick structure that looked more like a country club than a medical facility. The lights in the windows were warm and inviting.

But August felt nothing but a cold, creeping dread.

He had read the dossier Simon provided. The center was exclusive, catering to the children of billionaires and diplomats. It specializedin "gifted and troubled"youth.

Why would Jewel Gilmore bring a child here? A child she claimed to have adopted from nothing? The fees alone would bankrupt a normal family. And the psychological evaluation... that was for children with severe behavioral issues, or for establishing a baseline for prodigies.

Kayden Gilmore was five years old. What kind of baseline did she need?

The suspicion that had been a seed in the school hallway had taken root, twisting around his spine.He had to get inside those walls. He had to see her file.
He picked up his phone and dialed Simon. "The meeting with the director. MAake it happen. I don't care what you have to promise. Chambers Foundation donations, gala sponsorships, whatever it takes. I want access to that child's records."

He hung up, his eyes never leaving the building. He felt like a man standing on the edge of a cliff,about to step into the dark. And the terrifying part was, he wasn't sure if he wanted to step back.
###Chapter 144


The backstage area of the NBC studios was a maze of cables, rushing production assistants, and the low,anxious hum of people about to go live.

Elisa sat in her assigned dressing room, staring at her reflection in the brightly lit mirror. The makeup artist had done a masterful job-her skin glowed, her eyes were sharp, and the dark blue suit she wore made her look like a force of nature. She looked like Faye.

A commotion in the hallway caught her attention.She heard Allena's distinctive, high-pitched laugh,followed by the low murmur of August's voice.

Elisa stood up. She opened the dressing room door and stepped into the corridor just as August and Allena were being escorted past.

August saw her first. His step faltered for a fraction of a second, his eyes narrowing into cold, hard slits.Allena, who was clinging to his arm like a vine,looked up and saw Elisa. A smirk instantly curled her lips.
"Elisa," Allena cooed, her voice dripping with fake sweetness. "What a surprise. I didn't know they let the support staff backstage."

Elisa ignored her. She looked directly at August. "|need to speak with you. Five minutes."

Allena tightened her grip on August's arm. "August,we don't have time for her drama. Hair and makeup is waiting."

August held up a hand, silencing Allena. He looked at Elisa, his expression bored. "You have two minutes."

He nodded to Allena, who shot Elisa a venomous glare before flouncing off toward her own dressing room.

Elisa led August down the hall to a quiet alcove near the fire exit. The moment they were out of earshot,she turned to face him.

"I'm not asking you as your wife," Elisa said, her voice low and steady. "I'm asking you as someone who understands the vulnerability of the Chambers empire. Do not go on that stage with her."

August let out a short, mocking laugh. He leaned
against the wall, crossing his arms over his chest. "Is that right? The woman who blocked my number is now using the trust to get my attention? How predictable."

"This isn't a game, August," Elisa said, her eyes flashing. "If you sit next to her on national television,you aren't just making a personal statement. You're making a financial one. You're tying the Chambers name to Vanguard Medical. If she fails, you fail.The stock will tank. The board will revolt."

She took a step closer, her voice dropping. "Your grandfather built this empire on respectability. Are you really willing to risk it all for a woman who isn't even your wife?"

The mention of his grandfather was a spark to powder. August's face darkened, the lazy amusement vanishing. He pushed off the wall, using his height to loom over her.

"You don't get to lecture me about my family," he snarled, his voice a low, dangerous rumble. "You don't get to use my grandfather's name to manipulate me. I know exactly what you're doing,Elisa.You want me off the stage so you can have the
spotlight. You think if you embarrass Allena, you'll somehow win me back?"

He leaned in, his face inches from hers. "It's pathetic.You're pathetic. And you need to understand your place. It's not on that stage."

Elisa stared at him. The sheer, impenetrable wall of his arrogance was breathtaking. He truly believed this was about her wanting him. He couldn't fathom a world where she simply wanted to destroy a liar.

She took a step back, the last ember of hope in her chest extinguishing with a soft,final hiss.

"I understand perfectly," she said, her voice completely devoid of emotion.

"August!" Allena's voice called out from down the hall. She had changed into a stunning white dress,looking every inch the angelic victim. She walked toward them, a bright smile on her face, "Are you done? They need us in makeup!"

August's expression instantly smoothed over, He turned to Allena, his eyes softening. "I'm done," he said.
He offered Allena his arm. She took it, deliberately looking at Elisa as she snuggled closer to him. "Don't let her get to you, baby," Allena said loudly enough for Elisa to hear. "She's just jealous."

August didn't look back at Elisa. He simply walked away, Allena glued to his side.

Elisa watched them go. She didn't feel angry. She didn't feel hurt. She felt a profound, chilling clarity.

She pulled out her phone and typed a message to Jewel.

Plan B is a go.

The reply was instantaneous.

Control room is ready. Let's burn them down.

Elisa then slipped her hand into her briefcase and pulled out a small, featureless black device, no bigger than a credit card. She attached it to the back of her phone. A simple interface appeared on the screen, showing a single, ominous button labeled 'Execute.' She pressed it without hesitation. A green progress bar began to fill, silently counting down to digital chaos.
Elisa turned and walked back into her dressing room.She closed the door, locking it with a soft click. She sat down at the vanity and opened her briefcase.Inside, nestled among her legal pads, was a thick sheaf of papers.

It was the Allena Sinclair dossier. The raw data. The lies.

She picked it up, her fingers tracing the red highlights she had made. She had tried to warn him.She had tried to save him from himself.

Now, she would just save herself.
###Chapter 145


The studio lights were blinding, casting a harsh,unforgiving glare over the sleek, modern set of Capital Minds. The live countdown clock in the corner of the monitor ticked down from thirty seconds.

Elisa sat on one end of a curved, white sofa. August and Allena sat on the other end. The host, a man with a reputation for journalistic vivisection, sat in the middle.

Allena was practically in August's lap, her hand resting possessively on his thigh. She was glowing,basking in the reflected power of his presence.August sat stiffly, his face a mask of controlled authority.

Elisa sat perfectly still, her hands folded in her lap.She looked calm, composed, and utterly dangerous.

"Ten seconds!" the director called out.

The red "ON AIR" light blinked on.
The host launched into his introduction, his voice booming with false excitement. He welcomed the audience to a special edition of the show, focusing on the future of medical Al. He introduced Dr. Allena Sinclair as the "visionary" behind Vanguard Medical's new initiative, and August Chambers as the "power couple's" financial backbone.①

When he got to Elisa, the introduction was noticeably shorter. "And representing Aethel Intelligence, we have their Chief Technical Architect, Faye."

The host turned to Allena first. "Dr. Sinclair, Vanguard Medical has been making waves with its claims about revolutionizing patient care. Can you tell us about the science behind the hype?"

Allena smiled, a practiced, camera-ready expression."Of course. At Vanguard, we believe in the synergy of human compassion and technological precision.Our new platform integrates-"

She was cut off by a sudden, frantic movement at the edge of the set. Simon, August's assistant,rushed onto the platform, his face pale and panicked.
He ignored the cameras and the stunned host,leaning directly into August's ear.

"Mr. Chambers, emergency call from the London office. The Zurich servers are under a targeted DDoS attack. The firewall for the family trust has been breached. We're facing a potential loss of control over core offshore assets. They need you now."

August's face went white. A server attack of this magnitude could cost the company billions. This wasn't a drill. This was a crisis.

Allena grabbed his arm. "August, what is it? We're .live!"

August gently but firmly removed her hand. He looked at the host, his expression apologetic but unyielding. "I'm sorry. Family emergency. I have to take this."

He stood up, straightening his jacket. As he did,his eyes met Elisa's. She looked back at him,her face an unreadable mask. There was no triumph in her eyes,no surprise. Just a calm, steady gaze that felt like a knife slipping between his ribs.

A chill ran down his spine, but the screaming phone
in Simon's hand left him no time to tink. He turned and strode off the set, vanishing through the heavy studio doors.

The host was momentarily flustered. "Well, folks, it seems business never sleeps. We wish Mr. Chambers the best."

Allena was left alone on the sofa. The shield was gone. The protector had vanished. She suddenly looked very small, very exposed, and very, very nervous.

In the control room above the studio,Jewel Gilmore leaned into her microphone, a cold, satisfied smile on her face.

"Host, go to Plan B," she commanded. "All cameras on Faye.Now."

The director hesitated. "But Dr. Sinclair is the guest of honor-"

"Dr. Sinclair is yesterday's news," Jewel cut in, her voice like ice. "Faye is the story. Cut to her. Now."

On the studio floor, the camera operators smoothly pivoted. The red tally lights shiftedfrom Allena's
panicked face to Elisa's serene one.
The host, a professional, immediately adjusted."Well, with Mr. Chambers called away, let's dive deeper into the technology itself. Faye, Aethel Intelligence has been called the 'black box' of medical Al. Can you explain the ethical implications of your diagnostic algorithms?"

Elisa leaned forward slightly, her voice clear and commanding."Certainly. The primary ethical concern isn't the 'black box' nature of the Al, but the opacity of the data used to train it. At Aethel, we believe that if the data is flawed, the diagnosis is not just wrong-it's dangerous."

She launched into a detailed, brilliant explanation of Al ethics, her words precise and compelling. She spoke of bias in clinical trials, of the dangers of overfitting, of the responsibility of the architect.

The host was captivated. The audience was captivated.

Allena sat on the othe end of the sofa, completely invisible. She tried to interject once, mumbling something about "patient outcomes," but her voice
was weak, her knowledge shallow. The host didn't even look at her.

In the control room, Jewel gave another order. "Wide shot on Allena. No audio. Let America see the prop."

The camera cut to a wide shot. The visual was devastating. On one side, a brilliant, articulate woman shaping the future. On the other, a pretty,silent ornament, fidgeting with her dress, her face a mask of growing humiliation.

The contrast was brutal. It was surgical. It was perfect.

By the time the first commercial break arrived,Allena was on the verge of tears. The moment the red light went off, she stood up, muttering something about "feeling faint," and fled the set, her white dress trailing behind her like a flag of surrender.

Elisa didn't watch her go. She spent the break reviewing her notes for the next segment. The stage was hers. The war had just begun, and she had already won the first battle.
###Chapter 146


The morning after the broadcast, Elisa was sitting in a private corner booth at an exclusive,members-only cafe near Aethel HQ. The sun streamed through the windows as she checked her laptop, sorting through a flurry of congratulatory emails. The internet was buzzing with clips of her segment. "Faye" was trending. Allena was a punchline.

The peace was shattered when two women suddenly slid into the booth across from her. Allena, wearing oversized sunglasses to hide what looked like puffy eyes, and her mother, Rosalind Sinclair. They were dressed to the nines, radiating the cold, hard arrogance of old money.

Elisa considered calling security to throw them out.Then she considered the satisfaction of facing them down.She slowly closed her laptop.

"What do you want?"

Rosalind leaned forward, her smile a thin, red slash."Elisa, darling. We need to talk. About your future."
They looked around the sleek, modern cafe with barely concealed disdain. Rosalind took in the tech executives having quiet meetings nearby, her lip curling.

"Charming," Rosalind said, her tone implying the exact opposite. "Very... corporate."

"What do you want?" Elisa repeated.

Allena pulled off her sunglasses. Her eyes were red,her face blotchy. "You humiliated me," she hissed."On national television. You made me look like a fool."

"You did that yourself," Elisa said calmly. "I just didn't stop you."

Rosalind stepped forward, placing a restraining hand on Allena's arm. "We're not here to rehash the past,Elisa. We're here to offer you a way out. A dignified exit."

Allena reached into her Hermes bag and pulled out a thick document. She tossed it onto the coffee table.It landed with a heavy thud.

"Sign it," Allena said, her voice trembling with anger.
"Just sign it and disappear."

Elisa didn't touch it. "What is it?"

"A divorce settlement," Rosalind explained, her voice smooth and condescending. "Germaine has already approved it. You sign this, you agree to leave New York, to never speak to the press, and you walk away with a very generous sum."

She named the figure. "Fifty million dollars."

Elisa stared at her. The number hung in the air,meant to be a knockout blow, a sum so vast that a girl from the Appalachian mountains couldn't possibly refuse.

Rosalind smiled, a predator confident in its kill."That's more money than your entire family has seen in generations. You can go anywhere. Buy a little house in the country. Forget all about this silly war."
Allena crossed her arms. "Take the money, Elisa. It's the only win you're going to get."

Elisa looked fromn the document to their expectant,arrogant faces. A slow, icy sensation spread through her, but it wasn't anger. It was a profound, almost physical disgust.
She reached for her coffee cup on the side table. She took a slow, deliberate sip, savoring the heat, the bitterness. She placed the cup back down with a soft clink.

Then she looked at Rosalind, and she spoke.

For seven years, she had been forced to endure Germaine Chambers's lessons. Not just in flower arranging and etiquette, but in the old woman's favorite weapon: the precise, cutting Parisian French she used to humiliate her guests. Elisa had learned it,perfected it in silence. Now, she would turn that weapon back on its own kind. Her voice was different. The accent was gone. The soft, American cadence was replaced by something else. Something rapid, fluid, and razor-sharp.

"Madame Sinclair, votre tentative de m'acheter est àla fois pathétique et insultante."

Rosalind blinked. The smile froze on her face. She had not expected this. She spoke some French-every good society woman did-but the speed, the accent, the sheer venom in Elisa's voice caught her off guard.
Elisa turned her gaze to Allena, who was staring in open-mouthed confusion.

"Quant à vous, ma chère," Elisa continued, her French flawless, her tone dripping with ice, "vous vous battez pour un homme qui n'est rien de plus que mes déchets d'occasion."

Allena's mouth snapped shut. She didn't understand the words, but she understood the tone. It was a slap.

"What did you say?" Allena demanded, her voice high and shrill. "Speak English!"

Elisa switched back to English, her voice slow and deliberate, savoring every syllable. "I said, he's second-hand trash. And you're welcome to him."

The words hit Allena like a physical blow. Her face flushed a deep, ugly red. "You bitch!"

Elisa picked up the settlement agreement. She didn't read it. She held it up, looking at the dense legal text,the signatures waiting at the bottom.

Then, with both hands, she tore it down the middle.The sound was sharp, satisfying. She tore it again.
And again. Until it was nothing but a pile of confetti.

She let the pieces fall from her fingers, drifting down onto the coffee table like dirty snow.

"Get out," Elisa said, her voice dropping to a lethal whisper. "The exit is behind you. Don't let the trash dirty my table on the way out."

Rosalind was trembling with rage. "You... you ungrateful little wretch. You'll regret this. You'll be left with nothing!"

Allena lunged forward, her hands clawed, her face twisted with hatred. "I'll kill you!"

Elisa sidestepped with the effortless grace of a dancer. Allena stumbled past her,catching herself on the edge of the table.

Elisa picked up her phone. "Leave," Elisa said, her voice calm. "Or I'll have the cafe's security escort you out in front of half of Wall Street's tech sector."

Rosalind grabbed Allena's arm, dragging her toward the door. They fled out the entrance, their heels clicking frantically on the stone.

Elisa watched them go. She turned off the alarm. The
silence rushed back in, broken only by the sound of her own steady breathing.

She walked back to the coffee table and picked up her coffee. It was cold. She didn't care. She took a sip, the bitter taste a perfect match for the cold, hard victory in her heart.

###Chapter 147

Three days later, Elisa's phone buzzed with an email.It was from Maeve Kearney, a friend who ran one of the top PR firms in the city.

Hey babe! Big Baby Shower for Charlotte Van der Woodsen at the St. Regis this afternoon. You have to come! I know you've been through hell, and I feel awful for being stuck launching the London office for the last six months, but I'm back now. You need to get out, show your face, and remind these people who you are. It's going to be the event of the season.Details attached.

Elisa smiled. Maeve was a good friend, and a distraction sounded nice. A room full of powerful women, champagne, and tiny shoes. It was exactly the kind of normal, civilized afternoon she needed after the chaos of the past week.

She dressed carefully-a simple, elegant cream dress that whispered money without shouting it-and took a car to the St. Regis.
The hotel was a hive of activity. The ballroom was draped in white silk and overflowing with tens of thousands of dollars' worth of white peonies. The guest list was a who's who of New York society.

Elisa walked to the check-in table, feeling a rare moment of relaxation. The woman behind the table, a young assistant with a headset, scanned the list.
"Name?" the assistant asked, not looking up.

"Elisa Wilder," Elisa said.

The assistant's finger scrolled down the iPad. She stopped, her brow furrowing. She looked up at Elisa,a polite, slightly confused smile on her face. "Oh! You must be Ms. Sinclair's assistant. Right this way,please. The staff entrance is through the side door."

Elisa froze. "Ms. Sinclair's assistant?"

"Allena Sinclair," the assistant clarified, gesturing to the side. "The VIP lounge is for guests only, but we have a designated area for the personal staff."

The cold, sick feeling of humiliation washed over Elisa. She was on the list. But not as a guest.She was listed as the assistant to her husband's mistress.
Before she could respond, a frantic voice called out."Elisa! Oh my god, you made it!"

Maeve Kearney rushed over, looking stunning in a pink suit, but her face was a mask of horror. She grabbed Elisa's arm and pulled her away from the desk, into a quiet alcove behind a massive floral arrangement.

"Maeve, what is going on?" Elisa asked, her voice tight. "Why am I on the list as Allena's assistant?"

Maeve pressed a hand to her forehead, her eyes squeezed shut. "I am so, so sorry, Elisa. I don't know what happened. This feels... deliberate."

"What do you mean?"

"My new assistant swears she listed you under my name as a VIP guest," Maeve said, opening her eyes,her expression agonized. "But the final list that went to the hotel... something changed. Allena's team was also cc'd on the final coordination. I don't know how,Elisa,but this has their fingerprints all over it."

Elisa understood instantly. This wasn't a mistake. It was a message, a public shaming orchestrated with cruel precision.
"It's fine," Elisa said, her voice hardening. "I'Il leave."

"No!" Maeve grabbed her arm. "Don't leave. If you walk out now, it'll look like you were thrown out. It'll be all over Page Six by tonight. 'Chambers' Ex Flees in Téars from Allena's Party.'"

Elisa hesitated. The thought of giving Allena that satisfaction was unbearable.

"And besides," Maeve lowered her voice, her eyes darting toward the ballroom. "They're here. August and Allena. They just arrived. Everyone is treating them like royalty."

Elisa's spine straightened. "They're here?"

"Yes. And if you leave, they win the narrative," Maeve said, her PR instincts kicking in. "Stay. You don't have to do anything. Just be seen. Your presence is a statement. You're the legal wife. You have every right to be here."①

Elisa took a deep breath. She looked through the gap in the flowers into the ballroom. She saw them.August, tall and imperious in a dark suit. Allena,glowing in a pale blue dress, her hand resting on his arm. They were surrounded by a gaggle. of
sycophants, laughing, air-kissing, holding court.
The perfect couple.
"If I stay," Elisa said slowly, "I'm not hiding in the corner."

"Of course not," Maeve agreed. "Just... blend in. Be graceful."

Elisa nodded. She took a champagne flute from a passing waiter and walked into the ballroom.

She found a spot near the edge of the crowd. She was a ghost, a shadow. People glanced at her, their eyes sliding past without recognition. She was nobody's assistant. She was nobody.

She watched August and Allena. They were standing with the guest of honor, an elderly, regal woman who looked like she had stepped out of a Gilded Age portrait. The woman was fawning over Allena,touching her arm,laughing at her jokes.

And then, the woman's gaze drifted. It landed on Elisa.

The woman detached herself from the group and walked over, her movements slow and deliberate.
She stopped in front of Elisa, her eyessharp and assessing.

"Hello," the woman said, her voice crisp. "I don't believe we've met. Who are you? I know everyone,and 1 don't know you."

Elisa opened her mouth to speak, but Maeve materialized at her side like a fairy godmother.

"Duchess," Maeve said, her smile bright and professional. "This is my dear friend, Elisa Wilder.She's helping me out today with some PR coordination."

The Duchess's eyes cleared. "Ah, the help. Of course. Very efficient-looking." And with that, she turned on her heel and walked away, her interest vanishing the instant Elisa was categorized as a worker.

Elisa stood there, the champagne flute cold in her hand. She had been introduced, and she had been dismissed. She was in the room, but she was not of the room. She was an intruder in her own life.
###Chapter 148


Elisa tried to fade further into the background, but it was impossible. The room was a cage, and the predators were circling.

Allena spotted her first. Elisa saw the moment it happened-a flicker of surprise, followed by a slow,malicious grin. Allena whispered something to August, then tugged on his arm, steering him directly toward Elisa.

They stopped a few feet away. August's eyes found Elisa. There was no surprise, only a cold, hard flash of anger. He looked at her the way one might look at a stain on a favorite carpet.

"Well, well," Allena said, her voice carrying. "Look who decided to join the party. I didn't know they issued invitations to the help."

The Duchess, who had been watching from across the room, drifted back over, drawn by the tension like a moth to a flame. She smiled at August.
"August, darling," the Duchess said, her voice loud enough to carry. "Your fiancée is just charming. You must bring her to my gala next month."

The word hit the air like a bomb.

Fiancée.

Elisa's breath caught in her throat. She looked at August, waiting. Waiting for the correction.Waiting for him to say, "She's not my fiancée. She's my mistress. My wife is standing right there."

He didn't.
·August didn't even hesitate. He smiled, a small,polite curve of his lips, and said, "You're too kind,Duchess.We'll be there."

He had confirmed it. In front of half of New York society, he had confirmed Allena as his fiancée.

Allena beamed, pressing closer to him. She shot Elisa a look of pure, unadulterated triumph.

The Duchess turned her sharp gaze back to Elisa."You there," she said, snapping her fingers. "Be a dear and fetch me a soda water. With a twist of lemon.And be quick about it."
The room went quiet. Every eye was on Elisa. The wife, being ordered to fetch drinks for the guests of her husband's mistress.

August stood there, his face a mask of stone. He didn't speak. He didn't intervene. He simply watched, his eyes cold and hard, as if daring Elisa to make a scene.

Elisa felt the blood drain from her face. Her fingers tightened around the stem of the champagne flute until she thought it might snap. The humiliation was a physical thing, a heavy, sufocating weight pressing down on her chest.

But she didn't scream. She didn't cry. She didn't throw the champagne in his face.

She simply nodded. "Of course, Duchess."

She turned and walked away, her spine straight, her head held high. She walked to the bar, the whispers following her like a swarm of bees. She ordered the soda water. She watched the bartender slice the lemon.

When she returned, she handed the glass to the Duchess with a steady hand. "Your soda water,
madam."
The Duchess took it without a word of thanks. She turned back to August and Allena, dismissing Elisa as if she were a piece of furniture.

Elisa looked at Maeve, who was standing nearby, her face white with fury. Maeve opened her mouth,but Elisa shook her head slightly.

"I'm not feeling well," Elisa said, her voice barely a whisper."I need to go."

She didn't wait for a response. She set her untouched champagne on a nearby table and walked toward the exit. She didn't run. She walked with the slow, deliberate pace of a woman who had nothing left to lose.

As she reached the door, she felt it. A burning sensation on her back. She didn't have to turn around to know that August was watching her go.

And she didn't look back. Not once.
###Chapter 149

Elisa didn't go far. She couldn't. Her legs were trembling, and the sheer weight of what had just happened was pressing down on her, making it hard to breathe. She found a quiet corridor that led to a terrace, the cool air a relief after the stifling atmosphere of the ballroom.

She leaned against the wall, closing her eyes. The image of August's face, cold and indifferent, flashed in her mind. The word "fiancée" echoed in her ears.

The sound of running footsteps broke the silence.

"Elisa! Elisa,wait!"
Maeve came skidding around the corner, her heels in her hand. She was panting, her face flushed with anger. "I am so sorry. That was... that was obscene.August Chambers is a mnonster."

Elisa opened her eyes. "It's not your fault, Maeve."

"It is my fault! I brought you here!" Maeve exclaimed,pacing in front of her. "And that bitch! Standing
there, acting like she's the queen of the world. And he just let her! He let that old bat treat you like a servant!"

Elisa let out a hollow laugh. "That's what I am to him now, Maeve. A servant. A nuisance. A ghost."

"No," Maeve said, stopping her pacing. She looked at Elisa, her eyes fierce. "No. You are his wife. You are still his legal wife. And you have power. You just have to use it."

"What power?" Elisa asked, her voice bitter. "He just announced his engagement to another woman in front of half of New York. He has all the power."

Maeve glanced nervously down the empty corridor,pulling Elisa further into the alcove. She lowered her voice to a conspiratorial whisper, one that wouldn't carry. "There is one thing he can't buy, Elisa. One thing his money can't protect him from."

Elisa looked at her, confused. "What?"

"A baby," Maeve said, the words dropping like
stones. "You need to get pregnant, Elisa. You need to have a Chambers heir."①

Elisa stared at her, stunned. "Are you insane?"

"Just listen!" Maeve urged, grabbing her hands."You're still married. The divorce isn't finalized. Ifyou get pregnant nowN, with his child, Germaine will have to acknowledge it. The trust will have to acknowledge it. Allena will be out in the cold. A baby is the only thing that can secure your position and give you leverage over that woman."

Elisa pulled her hands away, shaking her head. "I can't do that, Maeve. I won't bring a child into this mess."

"It's not a mess, it's a strategy!" Maeve argued,her voice rising. "It's the oldest strategy in the book, but it works! Stop taking the pill. Seduce him if you have to. Just get pregnant! It's your only way out!"③
Elisa opened her mouth to refuse, to tell Maeve that she already had a child, that she would never use a baby as a weapon.

But before she could speak, a shadow detached itself from the far end of the corridor.
August stepped into the light.
He hadn't left. He'd started to, his jaw tight with a cold, possessive anger at seeing her there, but his phone had buzzed with an urgent text from his London office. He had stepped backinto the relative quiet of the corridor to read it, his back turned. The message was trivial, a distraction, but it had kept him there just long enough. As he was about to turn and leave for good, Maeve's voice, sharp and conspiratorial, snagged his attention like a fishhook.He froze, melting back into the shadows, compelled to hear the rest of the venomous plot. His face was a .mask of cold, hard fury. His eyes, fixed on Elisa, were filled with a disgust so profound it was like a physical blow.

Maeve saw him and gasped, stepping back."August...I didn't see you there."

August ignored her. He walked slowly toward Elisa,his footsteps echoing in the quiet hall. He stopped a few feet away, his gaze sweeping over her like a searchlight.

"A baby," he said, his voice low and dangerous. "So that's your grand plan. You want to trap me with a
bastard child to get your hands on the trust."

Elisa felt the blood drain from her face. "No, August,that's not-"

"Don't," he cut her off, his voice like a whip. "Don't insult my intelligence. I heard every word. You think I don't know what kind of woman you are? You're desperate. You're cheap. And you think a pregnancy test is a winning lottery ticket."①

He leaned in, his face inches from hers. "Let me make one thing perfectly clear, Elisa. You will never have my child. I will make sure of it. And if you try to pull a stunt like this, I will destroy youso thoroughly,you'll wish you'd never been born."

He straightened up, his lip curling in a sneer. He turned to Maeve, who was trembling with fear.

"And you, Kearney. If I hear one more word of this 'strategy' from you or your firm, Chambers Group wil pull every contract, every sponsorship, every piece of business we have with you.Do you understand?"

Maeve nodded frantically. "Yes. Yes, Mr. Chambers.I understand."
August didn't look at Elisa again. He simply turned on his heel and walked away, his footsteps fading into the distance.

The silence he left behind was deafening.

Elisa stood there, frozen. The misunderstanding was a chasm, wide and unbridgeable. He thought she was a schemer. He thought she was trying to trap him. He didn't know the truth-that she already had his child, a child he had just called a "bastard" and a "stunt."

The irony was a knife in her heart.
Maeve let out a shaky breath. "Elisa... I'm so sorry.I shouldn't have-"

"It doesn't matter," Elisa said, her voice hollow."None of it matters."

She turned and walked away,leaving Maeve standing alone in the corridor. She walked past the ballroom,past the laughter and the music, and out into the cold New York night.
###Chapter 150


Elisa didn't go home. She couldn't face the quiet of the safe house, not with the echo of August's words ringing in her ears. She needed a drink. She needed noise. She needed to disappear.

She walked back into the St. Regis, but instead of going to the ballroom, she went to the hotel's famous bar. It was dark, crowded, and loud. The perfect place to be alone.

She sat at a corner table, nursing a glass of whiskey,staring at nothing. The burn of the alcohol was a welcome distraction from the cold emptiness in her chest.

She didn't notice the man until he was standing right next to her.

"Hey there, sweetheart."

Elisa looked up. The man was tall, with a flushed face and bloodshot eyes. He was wearing an expensive suit that was rumpled and stained, and he reeked of
expensive scotch and cheap cologne.

"Alone?" he slurred, leaning in close. "You look like you could use some company."

"I'm,fine," Elisa said, turning away. "Please leave."

The man didn't move. He placed a heavy hand on the back of her chair, trapping her. "Come on, don't be like that. I saw you at the party. You're one of a kind,right? The pretty little assistant. I bet you're looking for a real tip."

Elisa's skin crawled. "I said, leave me alone."

She tried to stand up, but the man moved his other hand, grabbing her wrist. His grip was tight, his fingers digging into her skin.

"Don't be a tease," he sneered, his breath hot and foul on her face. "I know your type.You play hard to get, but you're all the same. How much? Name your price."

Elisa's instinct kicked in. She twisted her arm,breaking his grip with a sharp, practiced move. She brought her other hand up, ready to strike.

But before she could, a different hand shot out.
A strong, elegant hand grabbed the drunk man's wrist, twisting it backward with a vicious snap. The man howled in pain, stumbling back.

Preston Vance stepped between them, his face a mask of cold fury. He was wearing a perfectly tailored tuxedo, his dark hair slicked back, his eyes glittering with a dangerous light.

"I believe the lady said no," Preston said, his voice soft and lethal.

The drunk man, emboldened by alcohol and humiliation, took a swing. Preston dodged it easily,then drove his fist into the man's jaw. The crack of bone was sickening. The man crumpled to the floor,out cold.

The bar went silent.Everyone was staring.

Elisa stared at Preston, her heart pounding. She looked past him, toward the entrance of the bar.

August hadn't gone far, the fury from their confrontation coiling in his gut. He'd turned on his heel,heading for the first place he could find a drink-the hotel's famous bar-only to see a scene that made his blood run cold. August was standing there.
He had seen the whole thing. The drunk man grabbing her. Preston stepping in. The punch.

And he didn't move. He simply watched, his expression cold and unmoved, as if observing a low-class street brawl that had unfortunately spilled into his line of sight. His lip curled into a faint, cruel sneer of vindication. This, his eyes seemed to say, was her world. This was the kind of trouble she attracted. He thought she deserved it. He thought she had provoked it.②

The sneer was worse than a slap. It was a complete and utter rejection. He didn't see her as his wife.He didn't even see her as a human being. He saw her as garbage.

August turned, his expression of contempt fading into boredom, and walked out of the bar. He didn't say a word. He didn't offer to help. He just left.

The drunk man on the floor started to groan, trying to get up. Preston kicked him, hard, in the ribs. "Stay down."

The man screamed. The bartender reached for the phone. "I'm calling the police!"
Preston ignored him. He turned to Elisa, his expression softening into a look of intense,possessive concern. "Are you alright? Did he hurt you?"

Elisa couldn't speak. She could only stare at the empty doorway where August had stood.

The next few minutes were a blur of shouting and flashing lights. The NYPD arrived quickly, their sirens screaming outside. They separated Preston and the unconscious man, taking statements from the witnesses.

'"You," a cop said, pointing at Elisa. "You're the one he grabbed. We need you to come down to the precinct. Give a statement."

"I'l come with her," Preston said immediately,stepping to her side. He placed a proprietary hand on the small of her back. "I am Preston Vance,and my legal team will be representing her."

Elisa wanted to shrug him off, but she was numb.She let him guide her out of the bar, past the flashing cameras of the paparazzi who had somehow already gathered, and into the back of his waiting armored
SUV, trailing the police cruiser.
As the car pulled away from the St. Regis, Elisa looked out the window. She saw the lights of the hotel, the glittering facade of the world she had just beeń thrown out of.

And she knew, with a cold, absolute certainty,that the woman she had been was gone. The wife who had hoped for a reconciliation, the woman who had still cared what August thought-that woman was dead.

She had been publicly humiliated, falsely accused,and left to rot by the man she had married. And now,she was sitting in the back of a billionaire's car with a man she didn't trust, heading to a precinct to explain why she had been harassed in a bar.

She looked at Preston, who was typing furiously on his phone. She didn't trust him. But she didn't have to. He was a tool. Just like the law. Just like the data.

She would use whatever she had to. She would climb out of this hole, and she would drag August Chambers down into it. It was about survival.
###Chapter 151

The fluorescent lights of the NYPD Midtown South Precinct hummed with a sterile, indifferent buzz.They bleached all the color from the room, turning the pale green walls a sickly gray and Elisa's skin to porcelain.

She sat on a cold metal chair, her hands resting on the steel table. The angry, purple bruise on her right wrist, where the drunk man had grabbed her, stood out like a brand.

A tired-looking officer across from her was typing slowly on a keyboard, his voice a monotone drone."So, you're stating that the suspect made an unwanted advance, and when you refused, he grabbed you."

Elisa nodded, her throat tight."Yes."

The officer sighed, a sound that suggested this was just another Saturday night drama between people with too much money and not enough sense.
"And you want to press charges?"

Before Elisa could answer, Preston Vance, who had been holding court with another officer in the corner,strode over. "Of course she's pressing charges. This was'a clear case of assault. I'm advising Ms. Wilder in this matter, and I can assure you, we have the resources to see this is pursued to the fulest extent of the law."

Elisa's stomach turned. He wasn't her advisor. He was an opportunist.

She reached for her phone, needing to hear a real ally's voice. She dialed Harvey Vance. Voicemail. She tried Alastair. Straight to voicemail. The feeling of being utterly, terrifyingly alone began to creep in,cold and familiar.

Just then, the atmosphere in the precinct shifted.The low hum of activity quieted. Maeve Kearney's frantic,near-hysterical phone call had been like a drill to August's skull. He had been on his way out of the city, intending to put the entire disastrous evening behind him, but her words had stopped him cold.The image she painted-of Elisa being hauled to a precinct, with Preston Vance playing the role of her
white knight-ignited a possessive, territorial fury that eclipsed his earlier disgust. He wasn't there to rescue her. He was there to reclaim what was his and put the Vances back in their place. The precinct captain emerged from his office, a forced,obsequious smile plastered on his face.

He was followed by August.

He wore the same impeccably tailored suit from the party, though the knot of his tie was slightly loosened, a rare sign of haste. He moved through the grimy police station like a king inspecting a slum. On his arm, clinging to him like a designer accessory,was Allena Mills.

A top corporate lawyer from Skadden, Arps flanked them, his briefcase held like a shield.

August's eyes swept the room, cold and dismissive.They passed over the officers, the drab furniture,and then, for a fraction of a second, over Elisa. There was no recognition. No concern. It was the look you give a piece of furniture that's in your way.

Allena, however, met Elisa's gaze. A flicker of triumph, sharp and cruel, flashed in her eyes before
being replaced by a mask of perfectly calibrated worry.

The lawyer spoke in low tones with the captain.Papers were produced. A pen was signed. The entire bureaucratic process, which had been dragging on for over an hour, was condensed into less than three minutes.

Preston saw his moment. He walked toward August,a challenging smirk on his face. "Chambers. You're late."

August finally turned his head, his gaze landing on .Preston. His voice was devoid of all wvarmth, like the surface of a frozen lake. "My wife. It's not your place to worry about her."①

The words "my wife" were a territorial claim,a statement of ownership, not affection. They were a warning shot fired at Preston, not a comfort offered to Elisa.①

Allena tugged gently on August's arm, her voice a soft murmur. "August, let's go. The air in here...it's making me feel ill."

Her gesture was a silent, powerful declaration to
everyone in the room: He is with me. He cares about my comfort.

The lawyer nodded to August. "It's all taken care of,Mr. Chambers. You're free to leave."

August turned, ready to walk out with Allena, without a single word to Elisa. He was simply going to leave her there.

"Ms. Wilder," Preston's voice cut through the silence,loud enough for August to hear. "Since your husband doesn't seem to care, allow me to see you home safely."

The words were a calculated strike, and they hit their mark.

August stopped. He turned back slowly. His eyes, for the first time, settled on Elisa. It wasn't a look of concern. It was a look of assessment, the way you'd inspect a damaged asset that was causing you a problem.

Elisa had remained silent through the entire grotesque theater. She watched them all-the preening rival, the self-serving opportunist, the cold,indifferent husband. Her heart felt like a dead weight
in her chest.
She stood up, the metal chair scraping harshly against the linoleum floor. She picked up her handbag from the table. She walked toward the precinct door, her gaze fixed forward, looking at no one.

Seeing Elisa's cold indifference, a flash of annoyance crossed Allena's face. She needed to be the center of his world. She lifted her hand, the one not clutching August's arm, and let out a tiny, breathy gasp.

'"Darling," she said, her voice laced wih a sudden,fragile pain. "My hand. I think I scratched it on the door earlier. It's starting to sting."

August's attention snapped to her instantly. His entire demeanor changed. The cold mask of indifference dissolved, replaced by a look of tender,urgent concern.

"Let me see," he murmured, his voice low and gentle.He took her hand as if it were a priceless, wounded bird, turning it over under the harsh fluorescent light to examine a scratch so faint it was practically
invisible.
Elisa paused at the door, her hand on the cold metal push bar. She turned her head just enough to see it.

To see her husband, who hadn't even glanced at the deep,ugly bruise on her own wrist, now fussing over a phantom scratch on his mistress's hand as if it were a mortal wound.

Preston moved to stand beside her, his voice a low,venomous whisper in her ear. "You see? That's the man you chose."

She didn't respond. She pushed the door open and walked out into the biting chill of the New York night.

Inside, August looked up from Allena's hand, his eyes drawn to the empty doorway. He saw her back,ramrod straight, disappearing into te darkness. A deep frown creased his brow, and his expression hardened into something dark and unreadable.

Allena felt his focus shift. She tightened her grip on his hand, her voice a soft, needy whine. "August, it really hurts."

He looked back down at her, his attention
successfully recaptured.
Outside, the city noise was a dull roar that did nothing to fill the echoing silence in her soul. She pulled her thin jacket tighter, the cold seeping into her bones, mirroring the chill that had taken root in her heart. For a few blocks, she just walked,aimlessly, letting the indifferent crowds of a Saturday night swallow her whole. The feeling of being terrifyingly alone, which had begun in the precinct,now bloomed into a vast, desolate certainty. She was an island, and the tide was rising.

A black Maybach glided to a silent stop at the curb beside her. The tinted rear window slid down. It was a driver, his face calm and professional.

"Ms. Wilder," he said."Mr.Cromwell sent me. He was in a secure meeting and couldn't take your call, but he was notified you tried to reach him on the secure line and asked me to find you immediately. He said you wouldn't call that line unless it was an emergency."
###Chapter 152

August's phone rang just as he was guiding Allena toward his own waiting car. He glanced at the caller ID. Maeve Kearney. He answered, his voice clipped.
"Maeve."
Her voice came through, tight with a frantic,tearful energy. "August, thank God. I am so sorry about what happened. I feel just awful. Please, tell me Elisa is okay. You have to make sure she gets home safe.·Please,for me."

He hung up without another word, his jaw tight with irritation.

Allena sensed the shift in his mood. "What is it,darling?" she asked, rubbing his arm.

August's eyes found Elisa, who was standing by the open door of the Maybach a few yards away."Nothing," he said, his voice cold. "A small favor."

He strode toward Elisa, his long legs closing the distance in seconds. He didn't ask. He commanded.
"Get in my car. I'll take you."

Elisa didn't even turn to look at him. She spoke to Alastair's driver. "Let's go."

August's authority, so absolute in his world, had just been publicly nullified. A flash of raw anger crossed his face. He moved faster than she expected, his hand shooting out and clamping down on her arm.His grip was brutal, and his fingers dug into her flesh,coincidentally pressing near the fresh bruise on her wrist.

A sharp, involuntary gasp of pain escaped her lips.She didn't cry out, but she turned, her eyes meeting his, cold and defiant.

"Don't make me say it again," August said, his voice a low growl. "You've caused enough trouble for one night. Don't make Maeve call me again."

Elisa knew he was capable of making a scene, of physically forcing her into his car right there on the street. She didn't want to give him the satisfaction of another public spectacle. She stepped away from the open door of the Maybach and gave a small, almost imperceptible nod to Alastair's driver, who
understood immediately. The Maybach's door closed, and the car pulled silently away into traffic without her.

A moment later, August's black Rolls-Royce Phantom matérialized at thecurb. His driver, a man trained to be invisible, opened the rear door.

August didn't move to help Elisa. Instead, he walked around to the other side, opened the door for Allena,and placed a gentlemanly hand over the doorframe to protect her head as she slid inside.

The gesture was a small, brutal masterpiece of cruelty. It screamed his preference louder than any words could.

Elisa pulled open the front passenger door herself and got in. The seat was cold.

The cavernous interior of the car was filled with the scent of Allena's expensive perfume and a silence so heavy it felt like a physical weight.

Allena broke it, her voice syrupy sweet. She snuggled against August's shoulder. "August, can we go to the estate in Connecticut next weekend? I'd love to visit Dr. Blevins. I heard he's working on a new anti-aging
therapy."

She mentioned Dr. Blevins deliberately, a calculated probe to see if Elisa would react, a way to flaunt her access to Elisa's own mentor.

August stroked her hair, his voice a low, indulgent murmur. "Of course. I'll have Simon handle it through the Senator's office. Blevins is a difficult man to see,but he won won't refuse a request from the Senator."

Their conversation was a bubble of intimacy,designed to exclude her, to render her invisible. Elisa kept her gaze fixed on the blur ofstreetlights passing outside the window.

Her lack of reaction seemed to annoy Allena more than an outburst would have. Allena pulled out her phone, opened Instagram, and angled the screen forward.

"Oh, look," she said with a small, theatrical gasp."Devon is so naughty, always posting things."

On the screen was a photo of August and Allena from the party, their heads close together, smiling. The caption read: "The future is here."
Allena laughed lightly. "He got your good side,August. You look so handsome."

It was a direct, blatant provocation. A public declaration of their status, displayed for the benefit of the wife sitting just a few feet away.

Elisa's expression didn't change. Her reflection in the window was a pale, impassive mask. Her stillness was a form of resistance Allena couldn't comprehend. The carefully crafted insults, the flaunting of affection, all of it sid off her like water off stone.

Finally, the Rolls-Royce pulled up in front of her safe house.

The moment the car stopped, Elisa unbuckled her seatbelt. She pushed the door open, stepped out,and closed it behind her without a word or a backward glance.

She walked up the path to her frontdoor,the sound of the heavy car door shutting her out, leaving the world of power,money, and humiliation behind her.

###Chapter 153

As the front door of the safe house clicked shut,August's gaze lingered on it for a moment too long.His face was a thundercloud of unreadable emotions.Allena saw the flicker of distraction. It was a tiny thing, but to her, it was a five-alarm fire.

She immediately pressed her hand to her chest, her fingers covering the invisible scratch, and let out a soft, pained hiss of breath.

"Allena?" August's attention snapped back to her, his voice instantly laced with concern. "What's wrong?"

Her eyes were wide and suddenly glistening with unshed tears. Her voice was a fragile whisper. "It's nothing, August. I think... I think I was just so tense at the police station. It's only now that I can feel how much it...hurts."

She held up her hand, presenting the microscopic red line on her finger as if it were a gaping wound.

August looked at the "injury." In his mind,he saw it
juxtaposed with the ugly, purple-and-blue bruise he had seen on Elisa's wrist. The two images flickered,and his judgment, already clouded by bias, made its choice.

Elisa's injury was a consequence of her own reckless behavior, a mark of her association with men like Preston Vance.

Allena's injury, however, was a wound she had sustained by his side, a testament to her innocence and loyalty.

A wave of guilt and protective tenderness washed over his face. He took her hand, his touch infinitely gentle. "I'm sorry," he said, his voice thick with self-reproach. "This is my fault. You shouldn't have had to go through any of this."

He leaned forward, his voice sharp and commanding to the driver. "Change of plans. Go to the private wing at Mount Sinai."

The driver hesitated for a fraction of a second. "Sir,we're right here. If Mrs. Wilder needs anything..."

"She doesn't," August cut him off, his voice laced with venom. "She has her 'friends' to take care of
her."
The implication was ugly and clear. The driver said nothing more, expertly executing a three-point turn and heading back toward Manhattan.

Allena leaned against August's chest, a triumphant glint in her eyes. "Are you sure? It's just a tiny scratch. We don't need to bother the doctors."

August cupped her face, his thumb stroking her cheek. "Anything that concerns you," he said, his tone absolute, "is not a small thing."

Inside the safe house, Elisa stood at the kitchen sink,letting ice-cold water run over her swollen wrist.The stinging subsided to a dull, throbbing ache. The bruise was already darkening, an ugly map of the night's violence.

She found a tube of arnica cream in the first-aid kit and awkwardly applied it with her left hand.

Her phone rang. It was Jewel.

"Elisa? Are you okay? I just heard what happened at the party! And the police?"

"I'm fine, Jewel," Elisa said, her voice flat. "I'm home
now."
"Where's August? Did he tear you to shreds?"

Elisa looked out the kitchen window, watching the red taillights of the Rolls-Royce disappear down the long driveway. A bitter, humorless smile touched her lips.

"He's busy," she said. "He's taking a patient with a very serious injury to the hospital."

Jewel fell silent, sensing the deep well of irony in Elisa's tone. Elisa didn't want to explain. She ended the call quickly.

She finished tending to her wrist, then walked into her office. She didn't turn on the lights. She sat in the dark and opened her laptop. The screen illuminated her face, casting it in a cool, blue glow.

Complex data streams from the Aethel-JHU project filled the display. She began to type, her fingers flying across the keyboard. She channeled the pain,the humiliation, the white-hot rage into lines of clean,logical code.

This was her world. A world of order and reason. A
world she could control.
Miles away, in a brightly lit, sterile VIP suite at Mount Sinai, a senior attending physician was carefully cleaning Allena's paper-thin scratch with an antiseptic wipe.

August stood beside the bed, his arms crossed, his brow furrowed with an expression of grave concern,as if he were observing a delicate heart surgery.

His phone buzzed in his pocket. A text. Then another.Three missed calls from the President of Chambers Group's European division. He ignored them all.

In his mind, nothing in the world was more important than the tiny, insignificant scratch on Allena Mills's finger.
###Chapter 154


The next morning, the conference room at Aethel Intelligence was buzzing with low, anxious energy.Elisa stood at the head of the table, a cup of black coffee untouched beside her.

Tessa Vaughn burst into the room, her face a mask of indignation. She didn't speak; she just thrust her tablet in front of Elisa.

On the screen was an email from the JHU project coordinator. It was a formal notice, cc'd to the entire leadership team.

Due to an unforeseen personal injury sustained by Dr. Mills last night, she requires a period of rest and recovery. The submission of the phase-one clinical data, scheduled for today, will be postponed indefinitely.

"Indefinitely?" Tessa hissed, her voice trembling with rage. "It's a data transfer! Does she have to type it with her own two hands? This is a pathetic excuse!"
Elisa read the email, her expression unreadable. This was Allena, extending her victory from the personal realm into the professional. It was a power play, a middle finger delivered via corporate email.

"Get her on the phone," Elisa said, her voice quiet and cold.

Tessa made the call. A snooty assistant answered."Dr. Mills is resting and cannot be disturbed."

"Tell her," Elisa said, her voice loud enough for the assistant to hear over the speakerphone, "that if l don't get a call back in ten minutes, Aethel's legal department will be handling all future communications."

The call came in less than five.

"Faye," Allena's voice was lazy, laced with a triumphant purr. "So aggressive, so early in the morning."

"The data," Elisa said, cutting straight to the point. "I want it by five p.m. today."

Allena gave a soft, theatrical sigh. "I'm afraid that's not possible. The doctor's orders. And August
agrees. He said my health should be my top priority."
She was using his name like a shield, a weapon.

"This is a multi-billion-dollar project, Allena," Elisa said, her tone flat and devoid of emotion. "Your personal life is not a valid reason for delay."

"How can you be so heartless? My injury was because of-"

Elisa hung up.

She picked up the internal line and dialed August's executive assistant, Simon.

"Simon," she said, her tone leaving no room for argument. "I need to speak with August. Now."

Simon sounded flustered. "Ms. Wilder, I'm sorry, Mr.Chambers is in a meeting."

Elisa heard the lie in his hesitation. "Is he in Allena Mills's office at the JHU building?"

The silence on the other end of the line was a confession.

Tessa, listening beside her, clapped a hand over her mouth in disbelief. The CEO of Chambers Group was
skipping his own meetings to play nursemaid to his mistress at a partner company's office?

A dangerous calm settled over Elisa. "Simon, give him a message for me. I am on my way over there righf now. If he is still in her office when Iarrive, I will walk straight into the project director's office and formally request that Dr. Allena Mills be permanently removed from this project for gross professional misconduct."

She hung up, grabbed her jacket, and walked out.

When she reached the JHU satellite office, the door to Allena's corner suite was slightly ajar. Elisa could hear their voices from the hallway-August's low,soothing murmur, and Allena's soft, pleased laughter.

She didn't knock. She pushed the door open and walked in.

The scene was grotesquely domestic. Allena was reclining on a chaise lounge, her hand wrapped in an absurdly large bandage. August was sitting on the edge of the sofa, patiently peeling an apple for her with a small silver knife.

They both looked up, startled. August's face
hardened into a mask of fury. He shot to his feet,instinctively placing himself between Elisa and Allena.

"What are you doing here?" he demanded.

Elisa ignored him. Her eyes were locked on Allena."I've submitted a formal request to the oversight committee," she said, her voice like ice. "To replace the clinical data consultant,effective immediately."

The color drained from Allena's face. She looked at August, her eyes wide with panic.

"Elisa, that's enough!" August strode forward and grabbed Elisa's arm, his fingers digging into her bicep. "Have you lost your mind?"

He was seething, his voice a low, vicious snarl."You're just jealous. You can't stand to see her succeed, so you attack her at work. Can't you keep your personal feelings out of the office? You are completely unprofessional!"①
###Chapter 155


Allena let out a faint, wounded whimper from the chaise lounge. Her body went limp, and she slid sideways, slumping onto the plush carpet in a theatrical faint.

"Allena!" August's anger instantly morphed into raw panic. He released Elisa, shoving her aside as he lunged toward Allena's side.

Elisa had just picked up a fresh cup of black coffee from the credenza, the ceramic mug hot in her hands. She was standing directly in his path.

August didn't see her. He plowed into her like a freight train, his shoulder slamming into her chest.

The mug flew from her grasp. A wave of scalding hot coffee cascaded down her neck and chest,soaking through the thin silk of her blouse.

A sharp, searing pain shot through her skin. She let out a choked gasp, stumbling backward.

August didn't even notice. He didn't look back.
He was already on his knees beside Allena, gathering her into his arms, his voice frantc. "Allena? Allena,wake up!"

Elisa stood there, the burning on her skin a dull counterpoint to the icy void opening in her chest.She watched him cradle the other woman, his face a mask of terror and devotion.②

She walked calmly to the desk, pulled a handful of tissues from a box, and began to blot at the angry red skin on her neck.

"Somebody get a doctor!" August roared at the open door.

Aides and assistants rushed in, their faces a mixture of alarm and confusion. The office descended into chaos.

Elisa moved to the window, turning her back on the scene.She took out her phone and dialed Jewel.

"Jewel," she said, her voice low and steady, "I need you to run a final check on Tate Mills's school transfer. I have a bad feeling. Confirm for me that his name has been formally withdrawn from the registry."
Jewel's voice was hesitant. "Elisa, I messed up. I'm so sorry. I saw the initial withdrawal submission confirmation and I relaxed. I should have kept checking. I just ran it again after you called, and the withdrawal request was canceled three days ago.Tate is still enrolled. God, I should have been watching it like a hawk. I'm so sorry."

The floor seemed to drop out from under Elisa.

He had promised her. In that school hallway, he had looked her in the eye and promised to handle it. And then he had turned around and broken that promise,undoubtedly to appease Allena.

It meant Kayden-her brilliant, precious daughter-would be in the same school, possibly the same classroom, as that cruel, violent little boy.

Kayden was in danger.

The fear that shot through her was a thousand times more painful than the burn on her skin. It was a cold,primal terror that eclipsed everything else.

Paramedics arrived, loading Allena onto a gurney.August hovered over them, his face pale, never leaving her side.
As they wheeled the gurney past Elisa, August stopped. He turned, and the look he gave her was pure hatred.

"If anything happens to her," he bit out, his voice shaking with rage, "I will never forgive you."
He blamed her. He blamed her for Allena's collapse.

Then he was gone, following the gurney out the door.The office was suddenly, blessedly quiet.

Tessa Vaughn rushed in, her eyes wide. She saw the huge, red, blistering patch on Elisa's chest. "Faye!My God, you're burned! Let me take you to the emergency room!"

Elisa shook her head. Her eyes were calm. Too calm.

"Tessa," she said, her voice perfectly level. "Cancel my afternoon. And get me a meeting with Aethel's top litigator. The one who specializes in custody and restraining orders."

Tessa just stared at her, confused.

"I'm filing for an emergency protective order," Elisa said, each word a chip of ice. "To prohibit Tate Mills
from coming within one hundred yards of my daughter. Under any circumstances."

This was no longer about business. It was no longer about a broken marriage.

This was war. And she had just drawn a line in the sand around her child.③
###Chapter 156

Back in the safe house, the sharp, chemical smell of burn cream hung in the air. Elisa sat on the sofa, a thin layer of white ointment covering the angry red patch on her neck and chest. The pain was a constant,hot thrum against her skin.

She had just gotten off the phone with the lawyer. He was drafting the petition for the restraining order but had warned her it was an uphill battle. He'd said they needed to prove a "clear and present danger," and a single playground incident might not be enough for a judge. She'd cut him off, her voice sharp. "It won't be a single incident. Start digging into Tate's records at his previous school. I want every disciplinary note,every complaint from other parents. I want a pattern.Find it." The lawyer, taken aback by her cold command, had simply said, "Yes, ma'am."

A wave of exhaustion washed over her. She leaned her head back, closing her eyes, and mindlessly picked up her phone to scroll through the news.
An Instagram notification popped up at the top of her screen. Devon Browning had tagged August Chambers and Allena Mills in a new post.

Like a moth to a flame, she tapped on it.

The photo was taken in a pristine, sun-drenched hospital room. Allena was propped up against a mountain of fluffy white pillows, looking pale and beautiful, a damsel in distress. August sat on the edge of the bed, holding her hand, his expression one of deep, soulful devotion.

But the focal point of the photo, the thing it was .clearly designed to showcase, was the matching watches on their clasped wrists.

They were new. Patek Philippe. The Nautilus, in rose gold. A his-and-hers set, notoriously difficult to acquire, worth a small fortune.

Devon's caption was a masterpiece of sycophantic drivel: "Some people guard their love with their lives.Praying for my friends. @AugustChambers @AllenaMills PowerCouple Unbreakable"

The comments section was a flood of fawning support from New York's elite, praising their "true
love" and wishing Allena a speedy recovery.

Elisa felt a surge of nausea so intense she thought she might be sick.

So this was his response. After Allena's collapse,after the chaos, he hadn't reflected. He had rewarded her. He had gone out and bought a monumentally expensive symbol of their unity and broadcast itto the world.

The photo wasn't just a declaration of love. It was a public, vicious mockery of everything Elisa had endured that day. It was a middle finger, crafted in Swiss machinery and polished to a perfect shine.

Her thumb, trembling slightly, found the "Block"button next to Devon Browning's name. She pressed it without hesitation. One more enemy erased from her digital world.

She tossed the phone onto the cushion beside her and forced herself to breathe. Anger was a luxury she couldn't afford. She needed to think. A moment later, her phone buzzed with an encrypted message from Alastair. It was a single sentence: Interesting timing for a PR push. Browning Venture Capital is
leading Vanguard's Series A round, and it's been stalling. This photo is a shot of adrenaline for nervous investors.

That was the missing piece. This wasn't just about showing off. August didn't do things without a purpose. The watches, the photo, the hashtag... it was a strategic move.

Allena's position on the JHU project was now vulnerable after Elisa's threat. Her "fainting spell,"August's dramatic rush to her side, and now this photo-it was all a carefully constructed narrative. He was using their "unbreakable" love story as a form of corporate armor for her. He was sending a message to the board, to the investors, to everyone: Her value is tied to mine. An attack on her is an attack on me.

It was a business play, dressed up in the language of romance.

The realization doused her anger like a bucket of ice water, leaving behind only cold, clear contempt.

You couldn't fight this kind of enemy with emotion.You had to fight them on their own terms. You had to go after what they truly valued: their money and their
image.
She picked up her laptop and opened a secure file Alastair had compiled for her-a deep dive into the financials of Allena's shell company, Vanguard Medical.

Her eyes scanned the documents. Vanguard was in the middle of a Series A funding round. And the lead investor, the one whose commitment all the others depended on, was Devon Browning's firm, Browning Venture Capital.

It all clicked into place. Devon's post wasn't just a friendly gesture. It was an advertisement. He was shoring up the public image of his investment's "visionary" founder.

A slow, cold smile spread across Elisa's face.

She opened an encrypted messaging app and sent a single line to Alastair.

Time to cause some trouble for Mr. Browning's fund.

The reply was almost instantaneous.

My pleasure.
Elisa closed the laptop. The pain in her chest had subsided, replaced by the quiet, humming focus of a predator that has just spotted a fatal weakness in its prey.

You want to build an empire of lies on a foundation of money and public perception?

Fine.

I'll just pull the foundation out from under you.
###Chapter 157


In a temporary, glass-walled office overlooking Bryant Park, Allena was scrolling through the comments on Devon's Instagram post, a triumphant smile on her face. The new Patek Philippe on her wrist caught the light, sending a shower of sparkles across the room.

"See?" Devon said from the leather armchair opposite her, swirling a glass of whiskey. "I told you it would work. Every gossip column in the city is running the photo. You're not the 'other woman'anymore. You're the brave, beloved fiancée. No one at JHU will dare to question you now."

"You're a genius, Devon," Allena said, finally looking up from her phone. "Unlike Elisa, with her crude,unsophisticated tactics."

"This is just the beginning," Devon said, leaning forward, his eyes gleaming. "We need to elevate your brand. Move you from 'August's girlfriend' to 'industry icon'."
Allena's interest was piqued. "What do you have in mind?"

"A Netflix documentary," Devon said, the words tasting of ambition. "We'll call it Heart of a Healer:The Allena Mills Story. A profile of a brilliant,compassionate doctor revolutionizing medicine.Think Elizabeth Holmes, but, you know, successful."

Allena's smile sharpened. "I see what you're getting at, Devon," she said, her voice dropping to a conspiratorial murmur. "The narrative of the misunderstood female genius, fighting against the establishment. It's a powerful story. Investors love it.We can use that."

Devon hid a smirk. "Exactly." He continued, "l've already spoken to my contacts at Netflix. They're hungry for a female-led STEM story. If we can get August on camera, just for a few key interviews,they'll give us their top production team and a massive marketing budget."

A flicker of doubt crossed Allena's face. "August hates that kind of thing. He's intensely prvate."

Devon laughed. "He needs this as much as you do.
He needs to re-establish control of the narrative after Elisa's little TV stunt. And besides, if you're the one asking, how can he possibly say no?"

Just then, Allena's phone rang. She glanced at the screen and held up a finger. "Just a moment, Devon."Her voice instantly sweetened. "Rosalind, darling.Yes... oh, you're going to be in Connecticut this weekend? How lovely. Of course, you should bring Tate by to say hello to Dr. Blevins. August and I are planning to be there ourselves."

That night, in August's penthouse, Allena lay curled against his chest, the city lights twinkling below them. She pitched Devon's idea as her own, her voice soft and full of carefully constructed vulnerability.

"It's not for me, August," she whispered, tracing a pattern on his shirt. "It's for us. For our work.I want the world to see the incredible things we're building together. I want to silence the people who doubt me."

August was silent for a long time. He hated the idea of cameras following him around, of exposing his life to public scrutiny.
But then he looked down at Allena's face,saw the pleading in her eyes, the faint tremor of her lip. He thought of Elisa in that office, so cold, so strong,so utterly unbreakable.

Elisá's strength felt like a threat. Allena's weakness felt like a home.

He needed to prove to the world-and to himself-that he had made the right choice. This documentary would be his proof.②

He stroked her hair. "Okay," he said softly. "I'Il do it."

Allena's face lit up with a joy so pure it was almost blinding. She kissed him, a deep, passionate kiss filled with the taste of victory. This was more than just a film. This was August Chambers, agreeing to publicly brand himself as her partner. It was a de facto engagement announcement, broadcast to the entire world.

She immediately texted Devon the good news.

He called her back, ecstatic. "Perfect! The production team is on standby. First stop: we go to Connecticut and get an interview with the old man himself,Dr.Blevins."
"Blevins?" Allena asked.

"Of course," Devon said. "He's the gatekeeper. The kingmaker. If we can get him on camera, praising your 'vision' and 'talent,' the documentary becomes instántly credible. No one will ever question you again."

Allena felt a surge of confidence. "He'll do it. With August by my side, and a seven-figure donation to his research foundation from Chambers Group, he won't have a choice."

After she hung up, she stood in front of the floor-to-ceiling window, looking at her reflection in the dark glass.

It's over, Elisa Wilder, she thought. You're about to find out what real power looks like.

She had no idea she was gleefully planning her own public execution.
###Chapter 158


The late afternoon sun cast long, golden shadows across the rolling lawns of Dr. Blevins's Connecticut estate. It was an oasis of tranquility, a world away from the noise and poison of the city.

Elisa was sitting on a checkered blanket under a massive oak tree, watching Kayden chase a monarch butterfly through a field of wildflowers. Dr. Blevins sat in a nearby wicker chair, a fond, grandfatherly smile on his face as he watched the little girl's joyful dance.

He had insisted they come for a few days, to give Elisa a place to breathe and to keep Kayden safe and happy after the trauma of the school incident. It was the first time Elisa had felt her shoulders relax in weeks. The burn on her chest had faded to a pale pink, and the country air felt clean in her lungs.

"I'm so glad you and Kayden could come," Dr. Blevins said warmly. "It's a busy day, unfortunately. I'm expecting Mr. Cromwell shortly to discuss a new research grant, but the west wing and the gardens
will be all yours. No one will bother you."

A movement from the main house caught her eye.The butler was walking briskly across the lawn, his expression troubled.

He leaned down and spoke in a low voice to Dr.Blevins. "Sir, a Mr. August Chambers and a Ms. Mills are here. They say they have an appointment,arranged through Senator Cartwright's office."

The names hit Elisa like a physical blow. The blood drained from her face, and her body went rigid.

Dr. Blevins's smile vanished, replaced by a deep,angry scowl. "I told my secretary I wasn't available to see them."

"I know, sir," the butler said, looking pained. "But Mr.Chambers was... insistent. He said he was bringing a donation for the foundation that you would not want to refuse. Also, a Mrs. Rosalind Mills is on the line,saying she's on her way from the stables with her son."

Elisa's first instinct was pure, primal panic. She had to get Kayden. She had to hide. He couldn't see her here.Not here.
Her hands shook as she started to scramble,grabbing the edges of the blanket.

Dr. Blevins placed a firm, steadying hand on her arm."Elisa. It's alright," he said, his voice a calm,reassuring rumble. "This is my home. No one will harm you or the child here."

He turned to the butler. "Show them into the main library and tell them I will be with them shortly." Then he looked back at Elisa. "Take Kayden to the conservatory in the west wing. It's quiet and out of the way. They won't go there."

Elisa gave him a look of profound gratitude. She scooped up Kayden, who was chattering happily about the butterfly, and carried her quickly toward the sprawling glass structure of the conservatory.

In the library, August and Allena were looking around with an air of proprietary appraisal. A two-person film crew was already setting up discreet cameras.

"Get a shot of that bookshelf," Allena whispered to the cameraman."And the fireplace. It looks so...intellectual."

Dr. Blevins entered the room, his face a mask of cold
displeasure. He didn't invite them to sit. He walked over to the grand French doors and pushed them open, letting the scent of roses and freshly cut grass fill the stuffy room.

"Mr. Chambers," he said, his voice clipped. "I amn a very busy man. You have five minutes."
