August smiled, unfazed by the rudeness. He gestured to his assistant, who stepped forward with a leather-bound portfolio. "Dr. Blevins, I believe you'll find we have all the time in the world. This is a signed pledge for an eight-figure donation to your foundation."

Just as he spoke, a new figure appeared in the library doorway.

Alastair Cromwell.

He took in the scene-August, Allena, the cameras-and a slow, dangerous smile spread across his face.

August's expression darkened. "Cromwell? What the hell are you doing here?"

"I could ask you the same thing," Alastair said smoothly, strolling into the room. "I'm visiting an.old
friend. You, on the other hand, I heard your European servers were a bit... unstable. I'm surprised you have time for a country vacation."

Alastair's presence instantly shattered the illusion of control August had tried to create with his money.

In the humid warmth of the conservatory, surrounded by giant ferns and exotic orchids, Elisa's phone buzzed. A text from Jewel.

Alastair is there. Total coincidence, he had a meeting with Blevins about a new partnership. Looks like fate is on our side.

Elisa held Kayden a little tighter, her heart still hammering against her ribs. She could hear the low murmur of voices from the main house. She prayed Kayden wouldn't get restless, wouldn't decide to explore.

She was trapped. They were all trapped, in the wrong place, at the worst possible time. And the risk of her deepest secret being exposed had never been higher.②
###Chapter 159


August ignored Alastair's jab. He had the checkmate move, the eight-figure donation sitting on the antique desk. He was in control.

He pushed the portfolio across the polished wood toward Dr. Blevins. "That money, Doctor, will fund your research for the next decade. No strings attached."

Dr. Blevins didn't even glance at it. His cold, blue eyes were fixed on August. "You're lying, Mr.Chambers. There are always strings. State your terms."

August smiled, a thin, self-satisfied curve of his lips.He glanced at Allena, who preened under his gaze."My terms are simple. In addition to accepting the donation, I would like you to appoint Dr. Mills here as a Special Appointed Fellow in your research institute."

The audacity of the request hung in the air. Alastair let out a short, sharp bark of derisive laughter.
A "special fellow"? Allena Mills? A woman whose most significant contribution to science was a plagiarized article? It was a grotesque insult to everything the man, and the institution, stood for.
Dr. Blevins didn't get angry. He became unnervingly calm. He walked behind his desk, sat down, and picked up a heavy fountain pen, turning it over and over in his long, elegant fingers.

He looked at August as one might look at a particularly ignorant child. "Mr. Chambers, do you know the last time I appointed a Special Fellow?"

August said nothing.

"It was seven years ago," Dr. Blevins continued,his voice soft and precise. "The appointment went to a young woman who, in less than two years,completely redesigned the algorithmic model for genomic sequencing. Her name was Faye."

The name dropped into the room like a grenade.

August's smile froze. The color drained from Allena's face.

Dr. Blevins's gaze shifted to Allena, and it was
withering. "Dr. Mills, I took the liberty of reading your paper in The Lancet. I was particularly fascinated by your conclusions regarding the antagonistic properties of the neuropeptide Y receptor. Perhaps you could elaborate on the mnethodology that led you to a result that contradicts every other piece of published research on the subject?"

He had asked her a direct, highly technical question about her own supposed work.

A deep, blotchy red flush crept up Allena's neck. She opened her mouth, then closed it. She stammered,unable to form a single coherent syllable.

August tried to intervene. "Dr. Blevins, we're not here for an academic debate-"

"In my house," Dr. Blevins's voice rose, sharp as a whip, "we are only here for an academic debate!"

He stood up, his tall frame radiating pure, undiluted authority. He walked around the desk and pointed a trembling finger at the donation pledge.

"Your money means nothing to me. And my laboratory will not be turned into a brothel for the intellectually bankrupt."
He picked up the leather portfolio. And with slow,deliberate movements, he tore it in half.
The sound of tearing paper was the only thing in the silent room.

August's face was a mask of thunderous, humiliated rage. Allena looked as if she was about to burst into tears.Alastair was enjoying the show immensely.

The tension in the room was thick enough to choke on.

And then, from somewhere outside, came a new sound.

The high,piercing wail of a small child crying.

In the conservatory, Elisa's blood ran cold. Kayden.She had been so focused on the confrontation, she hadn't noticed her daughter slip out the side door.

In the library, August heard the cry and felt a strange,inexplicable pang in his chest. He turned instinctively toward the sound, his face a mixture of confusion and a dawning, terrible suspicion.
###Chapter 160


Elisa was the first one out the door, running across the manicured lawn, her heart a frantic drum against her ribs. Dr. Blevins and Alastair were right behind her.

Drawn by the commotion, August and Allena followed them out of the library.

In a small, secluded rose garden, they saw her.

.Kayden was on the ground, her little white dress stained with dirt and grass. Her knee was scraped raw on the gravel path, a trickle of bright red blood running down her shin. She was crying, her small hands reaching for something just out of her grasp.

Standing over her, a triumphant sneer on his face,was Tate Mills.

Allena's seven-year-old brother. He must have arrived with a different family member.

In his hand, he held a small, sophisticated-looking robot with a custom-painted shell.
"It's mine now!" Tate shouted at the crying girl."You're just a baby! You don't know how to use it!"He had pushed her down and stolen her toy.

Elisa reached her in a second, scooping her daughter into her arms, murmuring soothing words, her mind a white-hot blur of maternal fury.

Dr. Blevins's face was dark with rage. "Young man,"he boomed, his voice echoing in the quiet garden."You will return that to her and you will apologize.Immediately."

Allena, seeing her brother being scolded, rushed forward and pulled Tate behind her, shielding him."Dr. Blevins, how dare you yell at a child!" she said,her voice dripping with indignation. "They're just children. Kids fight over toys. It's normal."

Her casual dismissal of Tate's cruelty, of Kayden's tears and bloodied knee, lit a fuse in Elisa.

She stood up, holding Kayden tight against her.Her voice was lethally quiet. "Allena. Tell him to give it back.Now."

August stood frozen a few feet away, watching the
scene unfold. But he wasn't looking at the adults. His gaze was locked on the little girl in Elisa's arms.

On her face.

The,shape of her eyes when she cried, the stubborn set of her small chin, the way her dark hair curled at her temples. It was like looking at a ghost. A ghost of himself, from a long-forgotten childhood photograph.The pieces of the puzzle-the school, the fierce protectiveness, the uncanny resemblance-slammed together in his mind with the force of a physical blow.

"It's just a stupid robot!" Allena was saying defensively. "We'll buy you a new one! Tate, let's ggo."

"A stupid robot?" Dr. Blevins roared, his face purple with rage. He pointed at the toy in Tate's hand. "Do you have any idea what that is? That is a one-of-a-kind prototype from a pivate project at MIT's media lab, a gift to my goddaughter! The innovation in its chassis design alone is more brilliant than anything your entire plagiarized project could ever hope to produce!"

The statement landed with the force of a thunderclap. August and Allena stared,
dumbfounded.
A five-year-old... had a prototype from MIT?

Elisa had no more words for them. She looked at Alastair."Get it."

Alastair nodded, took two long strides forward,and gently but firmly plucked the robot from a stunned Tate's hand.

Tate immediately burst into a fresh set of wails,running to hide behind Allena.

Allena was sputtering, her face contorted with fury and humiliation. She pointed a trembling finger at Elisa. "Who even are you? Why is your child here?What is going on?"

The question hung in the air. All eyes turned to Elisa and the crying girl in her arms.

August took a step forward. His voice was hoarse,thick with a dawning, terrifying understanding. He stared at Elisa, his eyes boring into hers, demanding the truth.

"Elisa," he asked, the single word cracking with the weight of a thousand unasked questions. "Who is
this child?"

###Chapter 161


The hoarse, raw sound of August's question hung in the quiet air of the rose garden.

Elisa, who is this child?

Elisa felt her heart stop beating. A cold, heavy stone dropped into her stomach, pulling all the warmth from her limbs. Her breath caught in her throat,trapped behind a sudden wall of pure panic.

She looked down. Kayden was still crying, her small chest heaving. Bright red blood trickled down her pale knee, staining the white lace of her sock.

The sight of that blood acted like a switch. The paralyzing panic vanished, instantly replaced by a wave of maternal fury so intense it made her vision blur at the edges.

She pulled Kayden tightly against her chest,wrapping her arms around the small body to shield her from view. She lifted her head and met August's stare.
Her eyes were flat and hard, devoid of any warmth or history.

"That is none of your business," Elisa said. Her voice was a low, steady hum, like the sound of a live wire ready to snap.

August's jaw tightened. The muscles in his neck strained against his collar. He took a heavy step forward on the gravel path, his hand reaching out as if he meant to pull the child from her arms to get a better look.

Before his foot could land, Alastair moved.
He stepped directly into August's path. His tall,broad frame formed a solid wall of muscle and expensive fabric between August and Elisa.

"Take another step, Chambers," Alastair said. His voice was casual, but his shoulders were locked and ready for violence. "I highly suggest you step back."

August stopped, his fists clenching at his sides. The tension between the two men was thick enough to suffocate on.

As the adults' voices rose, Tate, seeing he was no
longer the center of attention and sensing the dangerous shift in the atmosphere, scrambled away from the fallen toys and ran to hide behind his mother's legs.③

From behind Allena's legs, Tate poked his head out.He pointed a sticky finger at Kayden, who was still sobbing into Elisa's shoulder.③

"Crybaby!" Tate yelled, a nasty grin spreading across his face. "She's just a stupid crybaby!"①

Elisa's blood roared in her ears. She waited for Allena to correct the boy. She waited for a shred of basic human decency.

Instead, Allena let out a soft, breathy laugh. She placed a manicured hand on Tate's head,smoothing his hair.

"Oh, let him be," Allena said, looking at Elisa with a mocking, superior smile. "Kids fight over toys all the time. There's no need to be so dramatic and hysterical about a little scrape."

The final string of Elisa's self-control snapped. The sound of it breaking was almost audible in her own mínd.
She turned to Dr. Blevins, who was standing close by,his face dark with anger. She gently placed Kayden into the older man's arms.

"Hold her," Elisa whispered.

She turned back around. She did not run. She walked with deliberate, heavy steps across the gravel,closing the distance between herself and Allena in three seconds.

Allena's mocking smile didn't even have time to fade.

Elisa raised her right hand and swung.

The slap sounded like a gunshot in the quiet garden.The impact was brutal. Elisa put the entire weight of her body and years of suffocating rage into the strike.

Allena's head whipped to the side. Her body stumbled backward, her expensive heels twisting on the stones. A bright, angry red handprint bloomed instantly across her left cheek.①

Complete silence fell over the garden. The only sound was the rustle of the wind through the rosebushes.
Allena slowly brought her hand up to her burning face. Her eyes went wide with shock, staring at Elisa as if she had just seen a monster.

Then, she opened her mouth and let out a high,piercing scream.

"You crazy bitch!" Allena shrieked. She lunged forward, her arms flailing wildly, her manicured nails aiming for Elisa's face.

Elisa did not step back. She planted her feet firmly,her eyes locked on her target. She raised her right hand high into the air, ready to deliver a second,harder blow to the other side of Allena's face.

She never got the chance.

August broke out of his shock. His body reacted before his brain could process the situation. He lunged forward, throwing his large framein front of Allena to shield her.

His hand shot out and clamped down on Elisa's raised wrist.

His grip was a vice of bone and muscle.He squeezed with all his strength, his fingers digging directly into
the ugly, fading bruise on her wrist, a sickening yellow-green reminder of their last encounter at the police station.

A sharp, blinding pain shot up Elisa's arm, traveling straight into her neck and making her teeth click together. The force of his grab jerked her body forward,throwing her off balance.①

"Are you out of your mind?" August roared, his face inches from hers. Spittle flew from his lips. "You attack her like a wild animal in front of a child?"

Elisa looked up at him. She looked at the man she had been married to for seven years. She looked at the way his broad shoulders completely covered the woman cowering behind him. She felt the crushing pain in her wrist, administered by his hand.

The last, microscopic ember of feeling she had for him turned to ash. Her chest felt completely hollow,scraped clean of anything resembling a heart.⑩

She ignored the throbbing agony in her arm. Her voice was flat, carrying the chill of a morgue.

"Let go of me."
August stared down at her. He saw the absollie,terrifying emptiness in her eyes. It was a look of total erasure. It unnerved him so deeply that his fingers instinctively loosened their death grip by a fraction of an inch.
Elisa yanked her arm back, breaking his hold. She stumbled back half a step, cradling her throbbing wrist against her stomach.

A cold, humorless smile touched the corners of her mouth.

She raised her uninjured hand and pointed a trembling finger toward the heavy iron gates of the estate.

"I'm done. Take your trash," Elisa said, her voice echoing off the stone walls, "and stay out of my life."She then turned her head slightly to look at her
mentor. "Doctor, please ask your guests to leave."
###Chapter 162


The words hung in the air, cold and final. August's face turned a mottled, ugly shade of purple. The veins in his neck bulged against his collar.

Behind him, Allena grabbed the fabric of his suit jacket. She twisted the expensive wool in her fists,burying her face against his spine.

"August, please," Allena sobbed, her voice trembling with perfectly calibrated terror. "Take me away from here. She's completely lost her mind. She's going to hurt me again."

She pressed closer to him, using her body to physically pull his attention away from Elisa and back to her own fabricated victimhood.

A few feet away, Tate was watching the adults scream. The boy looked at Dr. Blevins, who was glaring at him with a terrifying, thunderous expression. Panic finally pierced through the boy's spoiled exterior.
Tate decided to run.
He spun around, his small sneakers kicking up gravel. He didn't look where he was going. He just put his head down and sprinted blindly toward the maiń house, desperate to escape the consequences of his actions.

Elisa was standing right in the middle of the path,stil cradling her bruised wrist, her body turned slightly sideways.

Tate slammed into her like a small bowling ball.

His hard shoulder drove directly into her stomach.The sudden, violent impact knocked the breath out of her lungs. Her feet slipped on the loose gravel.

She lost her balance completely. Her arms flailed,finding nothing but empty air.

She fell backward.

Her back hit the edge of the raised stone border that lined the rose beds. The sound of the impact was a sickening, hollow thud that seemed to vibrate through the ground.②

A sharp, jarring shock radiated from her hip where
she hit the stone, sending a wave of nausea through her. For a terrifying second, the ghost of her Aspen injury flared-but Elisa gritted her teeth, forcing the phantom pain back. She was no longer that fragile woman; she refused to let a child's stumble break her again.

The pain was so absolute it erased her vision. The world went black. Her mouth opened in a silent scream, but her lungs refused to draw in air. Cold sweat instantly soaked the back of her shirt, sticking the fabric to her skin.

"Elisa!" Alastair's voice tore through the garden.

He sprinted forward, dropping to his knees beside her on the sharp gravel. He hovered his hands over her, terrified to touch her and make the injury worse.

Dr. Blevins handed Kayden to a nearby nanny and rushed over, his medical instincts taking over. He knelt on her other side, his face pale with worry.

August heard the heavy thud. He heard Alastair's panicked shout.

He turned his head. He saw Elisa lying flat on the stones, her face the color of chalk, her body
completely rigid.

His stomach dropped. A sudden, sharp spike of genuine fear pierced through his anger. His foot moved, taking an involuntary step toward her.

At that exact moment, Allena let out a dramatic,gasping shriek.

"Tate!" she screamed.

She let go of August's jacket and threw herself onto the gravel next to her brother, who had fallen on his bottom after the collision. Allena gathered the uninjured boy into her arms, rocking him back and forth,crying hysterically.

"My baby brother! Are you hurt? Did she hurt you?"Allena wailed, burying her face in the boy's neck.

August's foot froze. He looked at Elisa, then at the DNA report clutched in Alastair's hand nearby. The truth she'd just unleashed in the garden was a sun he couldn't look at directly. Allena's sob provided the perfect exit-a chance to flee the confrontation he was losing. He didn't choose Allena because he believed her; he chose her because she was the only lie he had left to hide behind.
August turned his back, not just on his wife, but on the terrifying, uncontrollable instinct that had just made him step toward her in genuine fear.

He walked over to Allena. He bent down, wrapping his 'strong arms around her waist, and lifted her effortlessly off the ground. He held her against his chest, murmuring soothing words into her hair.

He did not look back at the stone path. He did not look at the woman lying there, unable to breathe through the pain.

"Pack up the equipment," August barked at the stunned camera crew standing near the library doors. "We are leaving."

He carried Allena away, his long strides eating up the distance to the driveway.

Elisa forced her eyes open. Through a haze of tears and blinding pain, she watched his broad back disappear around the corner of the house.

The physical agony in her spine was nothing compared to the cold, crushing weight settling in her chest. Any lingering fantasy she had about him possessing a shred of humanity was ground into the
dirt beneath his expensive shoes.③

The distant roar of a car engine starting up drifted over the lawns. The sound faded away, leaving the garden in a heavy, ringing silence.

"Mama?"

A tiny, terrified voice broke the quiet. Kayden wiggled out of the nanny's grasp and ran over. She dropped to her knees, her small hands gently touching Elisa's arm. Her large eyes were swimming with tears.

The warmth of her daughter's touch cut through the fog of pain.

Elisa gritted her teeth. She tasted blood where she had bitten her own lip. She forced her lungs to expand, drawing in a ragged, agonizing breath.

She reached out with a trembling hand and stroked Kayden's soft hair.

"I'm okay, baby," Elisa whispered, her voice barely a rasp.

She looked up at Alastair. Her eyes were no longer empty. They were filled with a dark, freezing resolve.
"Help me up," she commanded.

Alastair slid his arms under her shoulders. With agonizing slowness, he pulled her to her feet. Every millimeter of movement sent shockwaves of fire down her legs, but she did not cry out.

She stood there, swaying slightly, leaning heavily on
Alastair's arm. She looked at the empty space where August and Allena had been standing.

She was going to make them bleed. But first, she looked at the folder Alastair held-the truth about the child's bloodline. August could run to the ends of the earth with Allena, but he couldn't run from the DNA that was about to dismantle his entire world. "The results," she rasped, "don't let them leave this garden."②
###Chapter 163


The private medical room in the west wing of Dr.Blevins's estate smelled sharply of rubbing alcohol and sterile gauze.

Elisa lay face down on the padded examination table.Her hands gripped the metal edges of the table so hard her knuckles were completely white.

Dr. Blevins stood over her, wearing blue nitrile gloves.His face was set in deep, worried lines. He pressed his thumbs gently along the inflamed skin of her lower back.

Elisa squeezed her eyes shut. A sharp, electric jolt of pain shot down her sciatic nerve. She bit down hard on her lower lip to trap the scream in her throat.

A drop of fresh blood welled up on her lip, sliding down her pale chin and soaking into the crisp white paper covering the table.

Alastair stood by the door, his arms crossed tightly over his chest. His jaw was locked, the muscles
ticking erratically as he watched her suffer.

Dr. Blevins stepped back and pulled off his gloves with a sharp snap.

"The L4 and L5 discs are severely compressed," the older man said, his voice heavy with frustration. "The impact tore the scar tissue from your previous injury.You are millimeters away from a herniation that would require immediate surgery."

He walked over to the stainless steel sink and washed his hands.

"You are to remain flat on your back in bed for the next two weeks. No walking, no sitting, no working.Absolute rest. I will prescribe a heavy course of anti-inflammatories and muscle relaxants."

Elisa opened her eyes. She took a shallow breath,testing the limits of the pain. She planted her hands on the table and slowly, agonizingly, pushed her upper body off the mat.

Sweat beaded on her forehead. Her arms shook violently.

"I can't rest," Elisa said. Her voice was weak, but the
tone was made of iron. "The data integration for the joint project is in its final stage. We launch the beta test in three days."①

Dr. Blevins slammed his hand down on the counter.The metal instruments rattled in their trays.

"Damn the project!" he roared, his grandfatherly demeanor vanishing. "You have one spine, Elisa! If that disc ruptures, you could lose motor function in your legs. Is a piece of software worth a wheelchair?"

Elisa slowly turned her head to look at him.

."If I stop now," she said, her breathing shallow and fast, "Allena will use her position as clinical director to seize control of the testing phase. She will lock me out of my own architecture."

She shifted her gaze to Alastair.

"You know what they are doing," Elisa said to him."The fake compliance audit Vanguard initiated last week was just the first step. They are trying to steal the core algorithms of Project Chiron. They want to strip it down, patent the pieces under Vanguard, and leave Aethel with an empty shell."
Alastair uncrossed his arms. He walked over to the table, his expression grim. He nodded slowly.
"She's right, Gottlieb," Alastair said quietly."Browning Venture Capital is pouring money into Vanguard's legal team. They are preparing a hostile intellectual property claim. If Elisa isn't there to physically secure the final code compile, they might find a backdoor."

Elisa looked back at her mentor. "I built this. I survived seven years of hell to get to this point.I will not let them take it from me at the finish line."

Dr. Blevins stared at her. He saw the stubborn,terrifying fire burning in her eyes. It was a fire that could not be put out with medical logic. He sighed,a long,defeated sound.

He walked over to a tall medical supply cabinet and unlocked it. He reached inside and pulled out a heavy,rigid object.

It was a custom-molded spinal orthosis. It was constructed of thick black carbon fiber, lined with dense medical foam, and reinforced with steel struts.

He carried it over and set it on the table next to her.
"This is going to feel like a medieval torture device,"Dr. Blevins said coldly. "'It will restrict your breathing.It will dig into your ribs. It will make every movement miserable. But it will lock your lumbar spine in place and keep the discs from collapsing entirely."

Alastair stepped forward. "Let me help."

Elisa sat up fully, biting back a groan. Alastair gently guided the heavy back piece into place.He brought the front panel around her ribs and began to pull the thick Velcro straps tight.

As the rigid brace clamped down around her torso,Elisa's face drained of all color. The pressure was immense, squeezing her injured spine into a forced,unnatural alignment. She gasped, her hands flying to the edges of the plastic shell.

"Tighter," Dr. Blevins ordered.

Alastair pulled the straps until the Velcro shrieked.

Elisa closed her eyes and waited for the nausea to pass. She slowly slid her legs off the table and placed her feet on the floor. She stood up.

She felt like she was encased in concrete. She could
not bend, could not twist. Every step she took sent a dull, throbbing ache radiating through her pelvis, but her spine remained perfectly straight.

Dr. Blevins walked up to her. He placed his large hands on her rigid shoulders.

"Listen to me," the old man said, his voice dropping to a low, intense rumble. "Never show weakness to capital. Capital is a predator. It smells blood and it tears the weak to pieces."

He looked deeply into her eyes. "You are walking into a war zone. Do not be the meat on the cutting board,Elisa.Be the butcher holding the knife."

Elisa took a shallow, restricted breath against the tight foam of the brace. She pushed the physical agony deep down into a dark box in her mind and locked it.

"I will," she promised.
She turned her head and looked out the small window of the clinic. The sun had set, leaving the grounds in absolute darkness.

Alastair picked up her dark, oversized wool sweater
from a chair. He draped it carefully over her shoulders, helping her pull it down to conceal the bulky medical armor strapped to her body.

She walked toward the door. Her movements were stiff'and slow, but her posture was flawless. She was ready to return to the city.
###Chapter 164


The glass-walled headquarters of the Aethel and JHU joint project usually hummed with the quiet,intense focus of academic research. Today, it was a circus.

Massive LED lighting panels were set up in the main lobby. Thick black cables snaked across the polished concrete floors. A camera operator balanced a heavy Steadicam rig on his shoulders, circling the center of the room.

They were filming the Netflix documentary: Heart of a Healer: The Allena Sinclair Story.①

Allena sat on a sleek modernist sofa in the center of the lights. She wore a tailored, pristine white blazer that made her look like a high-fashion scientist. Her makeup was flawless, designed to look as if she wore none at all.

"Project Chiron isn't just code," Allena said to the interviewer off-camera, her voice dripping with practiced empathy. "It's about bringing a human
touch to artificial intelligence. I realized early on that the engineers lacked the clinical compassion needed to truly heal people. That's why I had to step in and guide the architecture."

She was stealing the entire philosophy of the project,word for word, from a memo Elisa had written three months ago.

Suddenly, the heavy glass doors of the headquarters swung open.

Simon, August's chief executive assistant, marched in. He was flanked by two large men in dark suits.Behind them, catering staff pushed three silver carts loaded with tiered trays of delicate pastries, finger sandwiches, and silver pots of tea.

"Excuse me, everyone!" Simon announced, his voice carrying over the chatter of the film crew. "Mr.Chambers wanted to send a small token of his appreciation to Dr. Sinclair and the hardworking documentary team. Afternoon tea from Le Bernardin."①

A collective gasp of delight went up from the production assistants and junior researchers. They
swarmed the carts, marveling at the Michelin-starred food.

Allena placed a hand over her heart, looking perfectly surprised and touched. She looked directly into the camera lens.

"August is always so incredibly supportive of my work," she murmured, a soft blush creeping up her neck. "He knows how stressful these final testing phases can be."

The message was clear to everyone in the room.Allena wasn't just a clinical director. She was the untouchable future queen of the Chambers empire.

Far away from the lights and the expensive pastries,in her private office, now cold and sidelined near the back of the floor, Elisa sat staring at her monitors.She had overruled Alastair's vehement protests,ignored Dr. Blevins's twenty furious text messages,and checked herself out of the estate's clinic before dawn.Absolute rest, the doctor had ordered. But rest was a luxury for people who weren't at war. For her,it was a death sentence.

She wore a thick, oversized black sweater that
swallowed her frame, hiding the rigid outline of the carbon-fiber brace. Her face was the color of old parchment. Dark circles bruised the skin under her eyes.

She kept her hands flat on the desk to steady herself.The pain in her back was a constant, grinding static that made it hard to think. She reached into her pocket, pulled out two white pills, and swallowed them dry.

A group of junior coders walked past her office,carrying plates of fancy sandwiches. They looked at Elisa, then quickly looked away, whispering behind their hands.

"Did you hear what happened at Dr. Blevins's estate over the weekend?" one of them muttered. "I heard she actually slapped Allena right in the middle of the garden. No wonder Mr. Chambers is furious."

"I heard Faye is being officially ousted once the beta test is done," another whispered back. "Allena is just waiting for the board to hand her the title."

The toxic gossip floated through the half-open door,designed to be heard. Someone had even taken a
stack of Elisa's printed data reports and dumped them carelessly on a chair outside Allena's temporary office.

Elisa ignored them. She blocked out the noise,the smell of the food, and the throbbing in her spine. She focused entirely on the cascading lines of green code on her screen.

Suddenly, a red warning banner flashed across her secondary monitor.

UNAUTHORIZED ACCESS ATTEMPT: CORE ALGORITHM MODULE.

Elisa's fingers flew to the keyboard. She bypassed the standard security interface and dropped directly into the command line.

Someone was trying to brute-force their way into the neural network training weights.

She ran a rapid trace route. The IP address bounced through three proxy servers in Eastern Europe, but the origin signature was sloppy. It carried a specific digital footprint she recognized.

It was coming from an external server registered to
Vanguard Medical. Allena's shell company.
They weren't just trying to push her out socially.They were actively trying to steal the source code right under her nose while the cameras were rolling.

Elisa's eyes narrowed into cold slits. She didn't just block the IP. She wrote a rapid, aggressive counter-script. She built a digital honey-trap, feeding the invading server a loop of corrupted, useless garbage data that would crash their local systems if they tried to compile it.

She hit execute. The red warning banner vanished,.replaced by a green confirmation.

She looked up through the glass partitions. She could see Allena laughing, holding a delicate teacup,surrounded by adoring fans.

You want to steal my work? Elisa thought, her jaw tight.Try it.

But the digital block wasn't enough. The Vanguard hackers had found a vulnerability in the external network bridge. To secure the core permanently before the beta launch, she needed to physically disconnect the external routing switch in the main
server room.
Elisa placed her hands on the edge of the desk. She took a deep breath, bracing herself against the plastic shell of her back brace, and pushed herself up.

Her legs trembled. The room spun for a fraction of a second.

She grabbed her tablet, tucked it under her arm,and began the long, agonizing walk down the back corridor toward the server room, leaving the noise and the vanity of the film set behind her.
###Chapter 165


The corridor leading to the main server room was a stark contrast to the bright lobby. It was narrow, lined with cold gray marble, and illuminated only by harsh,flickering fluorescent tubes.

It was completely empty.

Elisa walked slowly, dragging her right foot slightly.With every step, the heavy carbon-fiber brace dug into her ribs, restricting her lung capacity. The anti-inflammatory pills hadn't kicked in yet. The pain in her L4 disc was a hot, jagged knife twisting with every shift of her weight.

Cold sweat beaded on her forehead, stinging her eyes. She pressed her left hand against the cold marble wall to steady herself, leaving a trail of damp palm prints.

She was ten feet away from the heavy metal door of the server room.

Suddenly, a violent muscle spasm ripped through her
lower back. It was a brutal, involuntary contraction that completely bypassed her pain tolerance.

Her right leg gave out instantly.

Her,high heel skidded violently on the polished marble with a loud, sharp screech. The tablet slipped from under her arm, crashing onto the floor. The glass screen shattered into a spiderweb of cracks.

Elisa pitched forward. She had no way to catch herself. The sharp, metal edge of the server room doorframe rushed up to meet her face.

From the intersecting hallway on her left, a tall figure stepped out.

It was August. He had left the lobby to take a private phone call, away from the noise of Allena's film crew.

He turned the corner just in time to see Elisa falling.

He didn't think. The rational part of his brain, the part that hated her, the part that had declared war on her,didn't have time to engage.

His body reacted on pure, ingrained instinct.
August lunged forward like a coiled spring. His long
legs covered the distance in two massive strides.He threw his arms out just as Elisa's face was inches from the metal frame.①

He caught her.

His left arm wrapped tightly around her waist, pulling her hard against his chest. His right hand shot up,cupping the back of her head, burying his fingers in her short hair to protect her skull from whiplash.

The momentum carried them both forward. August's back slammed against the opposite wall with a heavy grunt, but he absorbed the entire impact.

For two seconds, they were frozen in the dim hallway.

Elisa's face was pressed against the crisp cotton of his shirt. The familiar scent of his expensive cologne,mixed with a faint trace of tobacco, flooded her senses. It was a smell that belonged to a different lifetime. Her brain short-circuited, unable to process the sudden, overwhelming physical proximity.

August held her tight. His heart hammered against his ribs. Through the thick wool of her oversized sweater, his arm felt something strange.
It wasn't the soft curve of her waist. It was hard.Rigid. Like a shell of armor.

He frowned, his protective instinct warring with sudden confusion. He squeezed his arm slightly,feeling the unyielding carbon fiber beneath the wool.

"What is this?" August demanded, his voice a low,rough whisper near her ear. "What are you wearing under here?"

The sound of his voice broke the spell.

Panic and revulsion exploded inside Elisa. The memory of him grabbing her bruised wrist, of him carrying Allena away and leaving her in the dirt,rushed back with sickening clarity.

She pushed against his chest with both hands, using all her remaining strength.

"Don't touch me!" she hissed, her voice shaking with pain and absolute disgust.

She shoved him hard enough to break his grip. She stumbled back, her spine screaming in protest, and hit the opposite wall. She stayed there, breathing heavily, glaring at him like he was a venomous snake.
August's hands dropped to his sides. He looked at his empty palms, a flash of genuine hurt crossing his eyes before the cold, arrogant mask slammed back into place.

Before either of them could speak, a red light blinked in the shadows of the intersecting hallway.

A cameraman from the documentary crew stepped around the corner. He had the heavy Steadicam rig strapped to his chest, the red recording light glowing steadily in the dim corridor. Just moments ago in the lobby, Allena had specifically instructed him to follow her fiancé. "Find August," she had whispered,pointing down the corridor. "And teIl the crew to get some candid shots of him interacting with the space.We need more of his powerful presence on camera."The cameraman had dutifully followed the billionaire's path.

He had captured the entire sequence. The desperate, protective lunge. The tight, intimate embrace. The hand cradling the back of her head.

Right behind the cameraman, the sharp clicking of heels echoed on the marble.
Allena rounded the corner, a bright smile on her face,expecting to see her fiancé looking stoic and authoritative.

Her eyes landed on August, standing close to Elisa in the isolated hallway. She saw the tension in his shoulders, the lingering look in his eyes.

The smile fell off Allena's face, replaced by a mask of pure, unadulterated jealousy.

"August!" Allena shrieked. The sound was shrill and ugly, echoing off the marble walls.

August flinched as if he had been shot. He spun around to face Allena, his posture instantly changing.The protective alpha male vanished, replaced by a man caught in a trap.

He took a large, deliberate step away from Elisa,putting as much physical distance between them as the narrow hallway allowed.

"She tripped," August said quickly, his voice loud and defensive. "I was just stopping her fromhitting her head."

He didn't look back at Elisa. He walked quickly
toward Allena, reaching his hands out to soothe her.

Elisa leaned against the cold wall, her breathing ragged. She watched the red light on the camera finally blink off.

The physical pain in her back was nothing compared to the absolute absurdity of the situation. He had saved her on instinct, and then discarded her out of fear.

And now, the entire disaster was recorded in high definition.
###Chapter 166

Within an hour, the incident in the hallway had been mutated, weaponized, and distributed.

Someone on the camera crew had recorded the playback monitor on their phone. The blurry,shaky video was now circulating through the private group chats of the Aethel and JHU teams like a virus.

In the luxurious VIP breakroom, Allena threw a ceramic coffee mug against the wall. It shattered,sending brown liquid splattering across the white paint.

"She planned it!" Allena screamed, her face red and blotchy. "She knew the cameras were rolling! She threw herself at him to make me look like a fool!"

Did you hear about the desperate ex at Aethel?Fakéd a fainting spell in the hallway just to get Chambers to touch her. Pathetic. She's losing her mind over Allena's success.

carbon-fiboer brace held her posture rigid. She didn't flinch. She didn't step back.

frankly,pathetic."

She looked at Allena, then at Devon, and finally swept her gaze across the room of judgmental faces.

"If anyone in this room," Elisa said, her voice carrying clearly through the silence, "cannot tell the difference between a woman falling and a man lunging to grab her, I suggest you schedule an appointment with an optometrist."

She turned her eyes back to Allena. A razor-sharp smirk touched her lips.

"And Allena," Elisa added, her tone dropping to a lethal whisper. "If you are so insecure about your
hold on your fiancé that a five-second accident sends you into a panic, perhaps you should keep him on a shorter leash. Or better yet, lock him in a cage."

Allena's face turned a violent shade of purple. She opeñed her mouth to scream, but no words came out.

Elisa didn't wait for a response. She turned and walked out of the breakroom, her posture perfect,leaving them choking on their own manufactured outrage.

She walked back to her isolated cubicle. She sat down,ignoring the throbbing in her back, and pulled out her encrypted phone. She dialed Jewel.

"I'm on it," Jewel said, her fingers already clacking on a keyboard in the background. "What do you want me to do with it?"

Elisa looked at the calendar on her desk. Tonight was the phase-one wrap party for the film crew at a restaurant in Manhattan. Everyone would be there.

Elisa hung up. She swallowed her painkillers. The rumors could swirl for a few more hours. She didn't care.

Because tonight, she was going to burn their narrative to the ground.
###Chapter 167

The private dining room at the upscale Manhattan restaurant was bathed in warm, golden light. Crystal glasses clinked, and the low hum of wealthy, self-important conversation filled the air. It was the hallway.

In high definition, slowed down to half speed, the truth was undeniable. He didn't just reach out to catch a falling employee.

The video continued. The camera panned slightly as Allena walked into the frame.

The slow motion captured the exact micro-expression on August's face when he heard Allena scream. The terror vanished, replaced instantly by guilt and panic. He shoved Elisa away from him as if she were made of fire, his movements jerky and cowardly.

###Chapter 168

###Chapter 169

###Chapter 170

###Chapter 171

Allena's breath was hot and smelled of copper from her bleeding lip. The threat hung in the air between them, sharp and lethal. **Allena had just threatened to use the family trust to seize the Ainsworth Antique Gallery-**the last remaining pieces of her grandfather's legacy.

They rushed forward, their heavy boots thudding against the granite floor, trying to form a physical barrier between the screaming Meredith and the bleeding Allena. One of the guards, seeing the situation spiraling out of control, spoke urgently into his shoulder-mounted radio. "Code Three in the main lobby. All units to the west fire exit. Mr. Chambers's security team is requested."
Taking advantage of the sudden chaos, Allena's hand shot out. HHer manicured fingers, slick with her own blood, clamped down on Elisa's wrist like a vice.

Allena's eyes were wide, manic, and filled with a toxic desperation.

"We are going to talk," Allena hissed, her voice a ragged scrape against Elisa's ear. "Now."

Elisa felt a sharp spike of pain shoot up her arm,but the threat to her grandfather's artifacts paralyzed her natural instinct to fight back. She swallowed the bile rising in her throat. She allowed Allena to drag her away from the center of the lobby, moving toward the isolated corner where the emergency exit signs glowed a dull red.

Allena pushed her weight against the heavy metal fire door. It groaned open, revealing a dim,concrete stairwell.

They stepped inside. The heavy door slammed shut behind them, instantly cutting off the noise of the lobby. The silence in the stairwell was thick and oppressive. There were no security cameras here.Just bare concrete and harsh fluorescent lighting.
Before Allena could even open her mouth to dictate her terms, the fire door was violently thrown open again. It crashed against the concrete wall with a deafening bang.

Meredith Hastings stood in the doorway. Her chest heaved. Her eyes were completely bloodshot, fixed on Allena with the terrifying focus of a rabid animal.

"You think you can run from me?" Meredith screamed, her voice echoing shrilly up the concrete shaft.

She lunged.

Meredith pointed a trembling finger directly at Allena's face. "You filthy, cheap whore! You ruined Julian! You destroyed his entire life just so you could play house with August!"

Allena wiped the blood from her chin. The panic in her eyes vanished, replaced by a cornered, vicious arrogance. She had nothing left to lose in front of Meredith.

"Julian ruined himself," Allena spat back, her voice dripping with absolute contempt. "He was weak.He was a pathetic loser who was only good for keeping
my bed warm until August finally noticed me. He deserved to lose his trust fund."

That sentence was a lit match dropped into a powder keg.

Meredith let out a sound that was barely human-a raw, guttural roar of pure maternal fury. She threw herself at Allena. Her hands shot out, her fingers curling into claws, and locked directly around Allena's throat.

The impact sent both women crashing against the metal railing.

Elisa was standing two steps higher on the landing.Her body tensed, her instinct urging her to step back. But the heavy carbon-fiber brace strapped tightly around her torso restricted her movements.She could not bend. She could not twist. She was physically trapped, forced to watch the violent struggle unfolding inches away.

High heels scraped frantically against the concrete.Allena gagged, her hands tearing at Meredith's wrists.

In a desperate bid for air, Allena's nails dug deep into
Meredith's cheek, dragging downward and leaving three bloody parallel tracks.

Meredith shrieked in pain. The sting of the torn skin pushed her completely over the edge of reason.

With a surge of adrenaline-fueled strength, Meredith shoved Allena backward. Hard.

Allena's expensive stiletto heel caught the very edge of the concrete step. Her ankle twisted violently.

For a fraction of a second, time seemed to stop.Allena's arms flailed in the empty air. Her eyes locked onto Elisa's, wide with sudden, absolute terror.

Then, gravity took over.

A piercing scream ripped from Allena's throat as she fell backward. She tumbled downthe steep,narrow flight of stairs like a broken doll. Her body slammed against the sharp concrete edges.

The sickening thud of her head glancing off the metal landing post echoed through the stairwell.

Allena crumpled onto the lower landing. She lay perfectly still. Bright red blood immediately began to pool beneath her head, soaking rapidly into the
pristine white fabric of her blazer.
Meredith stood at the top of the stairs, her chest heaving violently. She stared down at her own hands,then at the unmoving body below. The blind rage drained from her face in an instant, replaced by a stark,paralyzing horror.

The heavy fire door above them was suddenly yanked open.

August burst onto the landing, flanked by two massive bodyguards. He had heard the scream and been directed by the security call.

His eyes swept the scene. They bypassed Meredith.They bypassed Elisa. They locked instantly onto the blood pooling around Allena's head on the lower landing.

August's pupils dilated. The color completely drained from his face. His heart physically stuttered in his chest.

Meredith saw August. She saw the absolute teror in his eyes. In a fraction of a second, the survival instinct of a woman who had spent decades protecting her wealth kicked in. If August realized
she had nearly killed his fiancée, she would lose her family trust shares forever. She would be destitute.

Meredith collapsed against the wall. She forced her body to shake violently. She raised a trembling hand and'pointed a finger directly at Elisa.

"August!" Meredith wailed, her voice cracking with perfectly feigned hysteria. "Elisa has lost her mind!She pushed her! I tried to stop her, but she pushed Allena down the stairs!"

Elisa's breath caught in her throat. Her stomach plummeted as if she had just stepped off a cliff. She stared at Meredith, completely stunned by the sheer audacity and speed of the lie.

Down on the landing, Allena was not unconscious.

Through the haze of pain and the blood dripping into her eye, Allena heard Meredith's accusation. Even with a severe head wound and a possible concussion, her opportunistic malice remained intact.

Allena slowly, agonizingly turned her head. She looked up at August. She did not say a word. She just let a single, thick tear roll down her blood-stained
cheek, her eyes wide with fabricated terror as she looked at Elisa.

It was the perfect performance of a victim.

August's head snapped toward Elisa.

The look in his eyes made Elisa's blood run cold.There was no question. There was no doubt. There was only pure, unadulterated hatred. He looked at her not as a wife, not even as a human being, but as a monster who had just tried to murder the woman he loved.

"August, she-" Elisa started to speak, her voice tight.

She didn't get to finish.

August lunged down the stairs. He didn't walk past Elisa; he drove his shoulder directly into hers,violently shoving her out of his way.

against the rough concrete wall, a choked gasp escaping her lips as the pain blinded her.

She opened her eyes just in time to see August drop to his knees in the pooling blood. He gathered Allena into'his arms, his hands shaking as he pressed his expensive suit jacket against her bleeding head.

Elisa stood against the cold wall. She looked at the blood. She looked at Meredith's fake tears. She looked at August holding the woman who had stolen her life.

The trap had snapped shut. And she was locked inside.
###Chapter 172

"Call an ambulance!" August's roar tore through the concrete stairwell, raw and frantic. "Get the medics in here now!"

He didn't look up. His large hands were stained crimson, pressing desperately against the wound on Allena's head. The bodyguards scrambled up the stairs, barking orders into their radios.

Elisa remained pressed against the cold wall. Her lower back throbbed with a sickening,rhythmic pulse. She watched August cradle Allena. She watched the absolute panic in his broad shoulders.

He never once glanced back to see if his violent shove had hurt her.

Within minutes, the wail of sirens pierced the thick walls of the building. EMTs burst through the fire doors, carrying a rigid backboard and trauma bags.They swarmed Allena, shouting medical jargon,their boots slipping slightly in the blood.

garage.

When she finally pushed open the heavy oak door, the world swam in a gray haze at the edge of her vision. She tasted blood where she'd bitten through her lip to keep from screaming at every pothole on the interstate.

Allena's PR team, funded by August through Devon Browning's firm, had moved with terrifying speed.The official Vanguard Medical Twitter account had released a statement ten minutes ago.

Dr. Allena Mills has suffered a severe, malicious physical attack at the Chambers Group headquarters. She is currently in critical condition
with severe head trauma. Due to this tragic act of violence, the FDA approval phase for the Vanguard-JHU joint initiative will be postponed indefinitely.We ask for prayers for her recovery and demand justice against her attacker.

It was brilliant in its evil. Allena's skull was cracked,yes, but the timing was too perfect. Allena had been failing to produce the core code for the FDA review.This "attack" gave her the uItimate, unquestionable excuse to delay the project while simultaneously destroying Elisa's reputation.

Her encrypted phone vibrated violently against the desk.It was Jewel.

It was a video currently trending at number one on Twitter. The footage had been masterfully sliced.It showed Elisa in the hallway two days ago, reaching out as if to grab August's arm. Then it abruptly cut to Allena appearing around the corner, screaming in terror. The context of Elisa falling and August catching her was completely erased.

The caption below the video read: Aethel's 'genius'architect is nothing but a deranged stalker.Sources confirm she is the mole leaking Vanguard's secrets.Today, she tried to kill Dr. Mills.

They were trying to isolate her. They were trying to
destroy her allies.

"We are going to the hospital," Elisa said. "If they want a media circus, I will give them the main event."③
Elisa closed the laptop. She walked to the hall closet.She bypassed her comfortable sweaters and pulled out a sharply tailored, dark charcoal trench coat. The structured wool perfectly concealed the bulky outline of her spinal brace. She slipped it on, tying the belt tightly around her waist. She looked like a soldier strapping on armor.

Forty minutes later, Elisa's Porsche pulled up to the curb outside Mount Sinai Hospital. Jewel was already waiting by the entrance, looking pale but determined.

The hospital's main plaza was a war zone.

sea of reporters, cameramen, and paparazzi choked the entrance, waiting for any scrap of news about the billionaire's injured fiancée.

Elisa stepped out of the car. The cold night wind whipped the hem of her trench coat.

"It's her!" the man screamed, pointing his heavy camera directly at Elisa. "It's the attacker!"

The entire crowd shifted like a massive,multi-headed beast. Sixty pairs of eyes locked onto Elisa.

Then, the stampede began.

They surged forward, a tidal wave of flashing lights and shoving bodies. The hospital security guards tried to form a line, but they were instantly swallowed by the aggressive mob.

Microphones bearing the logos of every major network were thrust violently into Elisa's face,stopping inches from her nose.

"Are you facing attempted murder charges?"

"Did you leak the Vanguard data?"

Someone in the crowd shoved Jewel hard from behind.Jewel stumbled, crashing back into Elisa.

But she did not bend. She did not flinch.

reporters. The sheer, freezing intensity of her stare made the front row of journalists instinctively take a half-step back.

###Chapter 173

This was the VIP wing. It was supposed to be a fortress of privacy, guarded by strict security protocols and locked doors.

Instead,the wide corridor was packed.

At least twenty elite journalists and camera crews were crammed into the hallway, their lenses pointed directly at the open double doors of the master suite at the far end.

Elisa's jaw tightened. The realization hit her like a physical blow. This wasn't a hospital ward; it was a soundstage. Allena had intentionally bypassed security to let the media in. She was broadcasting her victimhood live.

hospital bed. Allena lay propped up against a mountain of white pillows. A thick, dramatic bandage was wrapped around her forehead. An IV line snaked into the back of her pale hand. Her eyes were half-closed, staring blankly at the ceiling. She looked like a fragile, dying swan.

Elisa was drowning in a sea of lies, condemned by a jury of cameras. The physical pain in her back was nothing compared to the suffocating, crushing weight of the injustice.

She had brought the truth to a place that only traded in fiction. And she had lost.③
###Chapter 174

August Chambers stood in the doorway.

###Chapter 175


The heavy wooden door clicked shut behind Elisa,sealing August inside the consultation room.

Her mind was operating with the cold, frictionless efficiency of a machine. August's arrogant "logic"had confirmed one crucial fact: there were no cameras in the stairwell. It was a dead end for physical evidence.

But Elisa knew something August didn't. She knew the exact psychological profile of her enemy.

She stepped into the private elevator and hit the button for level B2. As the car descended, she reached into the deep pocket of her trench coat. She pulled out her encrypted phone, unlocked it, and opened the voice memo app. She hit the red record
button and slipped the phone back into her pocket,leaving the microphone exposed.

Elisa didn't wipe the spit from her cheek. She didn't step back.

"You're right," Elisa said softly. "The police tend to believe the evidence."
Elisa slowly pulled her right hand out of her coat pocket.

She held up her phone. The screen was brightly lit in the dim garage. The red audio wave was bouncing rhythmically, recording every single word.
Meredith's eyes locked onto the red light. All the blood drained from her face in an instant. Her mouth fell open, but no sound came out. The cigarette slipped from her fingers, landing on the concrete.

"No," Meredith whispered, her voice trembling with sudden,absolute terror.

She lunged forward, her manicured hands clawing desperately for the phone.

the file and uploaded it directly to a secure cloud server.
