August blinked in surprise. He looked down at Allena. He had no idea she had saved the patriarch's life. He felt a sudden surge of pride in his investment.
Harrison did not smile. His cloudy eyes narrowed into sharp, dangerous slits. He stared at Allena's face for five agonizing seconds.
"You saved my life?" Harrison's voice dropped an octave. The ambient temperature around the table seemed to plummet.
"Yes," Allena nodded eagerly. "Your condition was critical. I used my extensive clinical experience to diagnose your ventricular fibrillation and-"
Allena began reciting the medical jargon she had stolen directly from Elisa's nursing charts.
Harrison raised a single, liver-spotted hand. The gesture instantly killed Allena's voice.
"My vision was blurred that day," Harrison said slowly. "But I remember the exact rhythm of the compressions on my chest."
Harrison shifted his gaze downward. He stared directly at Allena's hands. He looked at her long,manicured acrylic nails.
"Your hands lack the physical strength required to compress a human sternum two inches deep,"Harrison stated brutally. "Furthermore, the woman who saved my life smelled strongly of clinical sanitizer and lavender soap."
Harrison leaned forward in his wheelchair. "You smell like cheap Tom Ford perfume."
Allena's fake smile shattered. The blood drained from her face so fast her skin turned a sickly gray.The silence around the table became suffocating.The admiring looks from the guests instantly morphed into disgust and morbid curiosity.
"L.. I must have changed my perfume that day..."Allena stammered. Her voice shook violently.

couple. He refused to speak another word to them.Allena stood trembling in her red dress. She felt the burning stares of a hundred billionaires piercing her skin. She wanted to sink into the floor.

###Chapter 267

###Chapter 268

###Chapter 272

She summoned every ounce of fear, rage, and adrenaline in her body.

###Chapter 273

The slap, delivered with all the force Elisa could muster, left a bright red imprint of her five fingers stark against August's pale skin.

The sharp, stinging pain was an anchor, a single point of clarity in the chemical chaos overwhelming his senses. It sliced through the drug-induced haze, and for a moment, a sliver of the real August surfaced.

His gray-blue eyes, now wide with dawning horror,focused on the woman beneath him. He saw her torn dress, the terror and defiance warring in her eyes, and the raw, red mark on her skin where his grip had been too tight. A wave of self-loathing so powerful it was physically nauseating washed over him.

He scrambled off her as if he'd been burned,tumbling off the side of the bed and landing heavily on the floor. He stayed there on his hands and knees, head bowed, gasping for air like a drowning man.

Elisa reacted instantly. She crab-walked backward across the vast bed, pressing herself into the
headboard,pulling the torn pieces of her dress over her chest. She watched him, her body coiled and ready to fight again, her breathing shallow and fast.

The silence in the room was thick and suffocating.

Then, a shrill, piercing ringtone shattered the tension.

It was August's phone, lying on the floor inside the pocket of his discarded tuxedo jacket. Germaine's signal jammer was military-grade, designed to block all external communication indefinitely. But even her paranoia had not accounted for the hotel's own high-level security protocols. At precisely midnight, the entire building's network performed a mandatory thirty-second system reboot and security sweep, a standard procedure that momentarily disabled any unauthorized devices connected to its grid. In that brief window of digital silence, a single call slipped through.

With a trembling hand, August reached for the jacket and pulled out the phone. The name Allena glowed on the screen.

He swiped to answer, his thumb clumsy. Allena's voice, a high-pitched, theatrical wail, filled the silent bedroom.
"August! Help me! Oh god, I'm so scared! They just threw me out on the road, and there's a car... I think it's following me! I think I'm going to die!"

Her performance was over-the-top, a poorly acted scene from a cheap movie. It was a transparent,desperate ploy to regain his attention after her public humiliation.

But for August, trapped in a self-made hell, that call was a lifeline. It was an excuse. A way out.A reason to run from the monster he had just become.

"Don't be afraid," he said, taking a deep,shuddering breath. He forced down the violent,drug-induced static still raging in his blood, his voice carrying a slight, uncontrollable tremor as he tried to mimic the cold, decisive tone of a CEO. He was holding onto his sanity by a thread. The beast was temporarily restrained, replaced by the man."Send me your location. I'm coming right now."

He pushed himself to his feet. He didn't look at Elisa. He couldn't. He grabbed his jacket and strode toward the bedroom door, his shame a physical cloak around his shoulders.

He pulled the thin, black card from his wallet-the same master keycard that had failed him hours ago-and swiped it over the electronic lock on the suite's main door. A soft beep confirmed that the
system reboot had ended Germaine's lockdown.

The door swung open. August didIn't hesitate. He walked out, leaving the woman he had just assaulted alone in the gilded cage his mother had built.

The heavy door clicked shut, plunging the suite back into silence and stealing the last bit of warmth from the air.

Elisa sat on the bed, staring at the empty doorway.A laugh, dry and utterly devoid of humor, escaped her lips.

This was the man she had loved. The man who,moments after nearly raping her, would abandon her like a piece of trash for his mistress's fake emergency.

The last flicker of emotion in her eyes died. There was nothing left now. No pain, no anger. Just a vast, cold emptiness.

She forced her trembling legs to obey her, pushing herself off the bed. She walked into the living room and bent down, her fingers closing around the blue diamond brooch on the floor. She squeezed it in her palm, the sharp edges of the setting digging into her skin.
She went into the bathroom and turned on the cold water, splashing it on her face and neck, washing away the lingering scent of his bourbon-laced breath. She smoothed her torn dress as best she could, pulling the fabric together to maintain a shred of dignity.

Ten minutes later, Elisa Wilder walked out of the penthouse suite. Her back was ramrod straight,her steps steady and deliberate as she rode the elevator down to the lobby.

She pushed through the hotel's revolving doors and stepped out into the night.

A torrential downpour had broken over the city.Wind and rain lashed the streets, turning the neon lights of Manhattan into a blurry, watercolor mess.

A bellboy rushed toward her, holding a large black umbrella. "Ma'am, can I get you a cab?"

She waved him away with a sharp, dismissive gesture.

She would not accept a single thing from the Chambers family. Not even the shelter of an umbrella.

Elisa walked down the steps and into thestorm.The icy rain hit her instantly, soaking her velvet
gown, plastering her hair to her skull. The water ran down her face in cold rivulets, mixing with tears she wasn't aware she was crying.

She didn't feel the cold. Her heart was a block of ice in her chest, a dead weight that no storm could ever chill further.

She walked, one step at a time, through the flooded streets of New York.

The rain was a baptism. It washed away seven years of weakness, of hope, of love. It washed away the wife.

When the sun rose, only the avenger would remain.Only Faye.


The torrential rain of that New York night had not washed away the humiliation, but it had tempered her resolve into something unbreakable. For the next seventy-two hours, Elisa did not sleep. She returned to her secure apartment, incinerated the torn velvet gown, and treated the bruising on her wrists with the cold, detached precision of a surgeon. She packed for the summit with ruthless efficiency. By the time she boarded the private jet,the weeping wife left in the storm had been entirely buried.

Three days later, Elisa stepped out of the arrivals gate at Geneva Airport. The air was crisp and cool,a clean, sterile cold that felt like a purge after the humid rot of New York.

She wore a sharply tailored white pantsuit, the fabric as severe and unforgiving as the look in her eyes. The woman who had stumbled through the rain was gone. This was Faye, an architect of digital empires and personal destruction.

A black Mercedes S-Class was waiting at the curb.
A local representative from Aethel Intelligence, a young man in a dark suit, held the door for her with
a respectful nod.

The car moved silently along the shore of Lake Geneva. In the back, Elisa didn't look at the scenery. Her gaze was fixed on the tablet in her lap,scrolling through the agenda for the high-level corporate retreat.

Thirty minutes later, the Mercedes pulled up to the grand entrance of the Grand Hotel Kempinski, the official venue for the summit.

Elisa stepped out, her heels clicking with authority on the polished stone. She walked into the cavernous lobby, the light from a massive crystal chandelier glinting off the cold, hard surface of her sunglasses.

She was heading for the VIP check-in desk when her eyes drifted to the right, toward the hotel's Michelin-starred restaurant.

Her steps faltered. She stopped dead in the middle of the lobby.

Through the massive, floor-to-ceiling glass windows, at the best table with a panoramic view of the lake, sat August. He was dressed in a casual but obscenely expensive hand-stitched blazer.

And laughing across the table from him, her head
thrown back in a display of pure, theatrical delight,was Allena.

She had clearly recovered from her banishment from the Browning estate. A new, glittering diamond necklace adorned her throat. She was basking in the luxury, the picture of a cherished mistress.

August picked up a piece of seared Kobe beef with his fork and, with a look of hollow, mechanical patience, placed it on Allena's plate. He wasn't laughing. His eyes were shadowed, a dark,brooding storm cloud lingering over his features,his gaze frequently drifting past her to the cold waters of the lake. He was merely going through the motions, a man haunted by the ghost of his own monstrous actions three nights ago.

Elisa stood in the relative shadow of a large marble pillar, watching the scene. A cold, deeply cynical smile touched her lips.

He had torn her dress and her dignity to shreds in a New York hotel room, and now he was playing the devoted lover with his mistress against the backdrop of the Swiss Alps. The hypocrisy was so profound it made her stomach churn.

Just then, Allena turned her head. Her gaze swept across the lobby and landed directly on Elisa.
Allena's smile froze for a fraction of a second.Then, it morphed into something ugly and triumphant. She deliberately reached across the table and placed her hand over August's.

August followed her gaze. He saw Elisa, standing alone with her luggage, a solitary figure in the vast,opulent space.

His brow furrowed instantly. An expression of pure annoyance crossed his face, as if her very presence was an unwelcome intrusion on his perfect getaway. He didn't pull his hand away from Allena's.He simply stared at his wife, his eyes cold and appraising.②

Elisa's fingers tightened on the handle of her suitcase. She would not break. She would not give him the satisfaction of seeing her flinch.

She straightened her spine, preparing to meet his gaze head-on.

Suddenly, a dark cashmere overcoat, smelling faintly of cedar and cold mountain air, was draped gently over her shoulders.

"The wind off the lake is sharp, Faye," a low,resonant voice murmured beside her ear. "You'll catch a chill."
Elisa turned. L. Thorne stood beside her, his handsome face impassive, his dark eyes calm and steady.

He smoothly took the suitcasefrom her hand with one hand. With the other, he placed a light,proprietary hand on the small of her back, a gesture of unmistakable protection.

The entire scene was perfectly framed by the restaurant window.

August's eyes widened. The muscles in his jaw went rigid. His hand, still under Allena's, stiffened into a claw. The color drained from his face.

Thorne looked past Elisa, his gaze locking with August's through the glass. He gave a look that was both a warning and a declaration of ownership.It was utterly chilling.

Elisa didn't pull away. She allowed Thorne to guide her, the two of them moving as one toward the private VIP elevators.

As the polished brass doors slid shut, the last thing she saw was August violently yanking his hand free from Allena's and rising from his chair.
###Chapter 275

Night fell over Geneva. The hotel's grand ballroom glittered, the lights reflecting off the dark, still surface of the lake outside. The Chambers Group's private welcome gala, an opulent precursor to the official summit with Aethel Intelligence, was underway.

Elisa, no longer hiding behind the alias Faye, sat in her rightful capacity as Aethel's Chief Architect at a VIP table near the front. She wore a simple but lethally elegant black gown, its clean lines a stark contrast to the fussy, ostentatious dresses around her.

At the head table in the center of the room,August sat with a thunderous expression, a storm cloud in a tuxedo. Beside him, dressed in a ridiculously extravagant haute couture gown, was Allena. Her presence at a high-level corporate event was a glaring breach of professional etiquette, a brazen provocation that had already drawn murmurs of disapproval from the Aethel executives.

It was, apparently, Allena's birthday. And she was using that fact to transform a serious business dinner into her own personal coronation.
A team of waiters wheeled a massive, five-tiered fondant cake into the center of the room. The small orchestra hired for the evening began to play a soft, instrumental version of "Happy Birthday."

Every head in the room turned. A few of August's sycophants, eager to curry favor, started a round of polite applause.

Allena stood, pressing her hands together in a gesture of fake humility. A saccharine smile was plastered on her face as she soaked in the attention.

She picked up a champagne flute and walked to the microphone set up on a small stage.
"Thank you all so much for being here to celebrate with me," she began, her voice dripping with manufactured emotion. "But tonight... tonight has another, even more special meaning for me."

Her eyes locked onto August, her expression turning coy and suggestive.

"Someone very special promised me that on this day, he would give me a 'promise of forever."

The implication was as subtle as a sledgehammer.A wave of knowing murmurs and chuckles went through the room.
"Proposal! Proposal! Proposal!"

A few people, clearly planted in the audience by Allena's PR team, started the chant. It quickly spread.

The collective gaze of hundreds of the world's most powerful business leaders fell on August, a spotlight of expectation.

August's hand tightened around his water glass,his knuckles turning white. His jaw was clenched. This was not his plan. This was a public ambush,a hostile takeover of his own event.

But he was trapped. In a room full of partners,investors, and media, a public rejection would be a corporate disaster. It would make him look weak,controlled by a woman he was supposed to be controlling.

So he did nothing. He didn't stop the chanting. He didn't deny her claim. His silence was his consent.
Seeing his tacit approval, Allena's eyes glittered with triumph. She turned her head, her gaze sweeping the room until it found Elisa.

Elisa sat perfectly still. At her table, the atmosphere among the Aethel executives was thick with awkward tension. Everyone in the room--especially
her own team-knew exactly who she was after the explosive FDA hearings. They knew she was Elisa Wilder, the genius behind Aethel, and more importantly, August Chambers' actual legal wife.

"I can't believe the audacity," one of the senior developers whispered, shooting a fierce glare at the stage before glancing sympathetically at Elisa."Is she trying to force a proposal when everyone knows he's still married to you?"

Elisa watched the grotesque pantomime on stage,a cold, contemptuous smile playing on her lips.

"Let her dream," she murmured in response, her voice low and laced with ice. "It's a business transaction, wrapped in a birthday bow."

But the celebratory atmosphere was suffocating.The air was thick with congratulations for a wedding that hadn't happened, for a love that didn't exist.

She was August Chambers' legal wife, a fact now known to the entire industry, yet she was being forced to sit there while his mistress orchestrated this circus. The humiliation was no longer about being a secret-it was the sheer public disrespect of it all, a physical thing, a snake coiling in her gut,squeezing the air from her lungs.
Allena, high on her victory, walked off the stage.She moved through the tables, her champagne flute held high, until she stood before Elisa. She looked down at her, her eyes glittering with malice.

"Such a shame you have to watch from the cheap seats, Elisa," Allena whispered, her voice a venomous hiss only Elisa could hear. "Tonight, I get the one thing you never could."

Elisa slowly placed her silver fork on her plate. She lifted her head, her eyes as cold and dead as a winter sky.

"You think you've won?" Elisa's voice was quiet, but it cut through Allena's smugness like a scalpel.
"You're not winning anything. You're begging for scraps he's throwing to keep you quiet."

The barb hit its mark, but the feeling of being trapped, of being so brazenly disrespected in front of her peers, was reaching a breaking point.
###Chapter 276

Allena, her face flushed with a mixture of triumph and rage, spun on her heel and marched back toward the head table, a conquering hero returning to her throne.

Just then, the hotel's French manager, dressed in a formal tuxedo and white gloves, entered the ballroom. He carried a silver tray, covered with a black velvet cloth, held aloft wvith practiced reverence.

A single spotlight followed him, cutting through the ambient light of the ballroom. A hush fell over the crowd.Everyone knew what was under that cloth.

The manager stopped beside August and bowed slightly. "Monsieur Chambers. The item you requested."

It was a lie. August hadn't requested a thing. This was Allena's final, decisive move, a checkmate she had arranged with ahefty bribe.

August's face went rigid. His eyes, cold and dark,fixed on the manager with an expression of pure murder.
But the guests were already lost in the drama. The chanting started again, louder this time. "Open it!Open it!"

Allena was practically vibrating with excitement.She knew August was trapped. For the sake of appearances, for the sake of the Chambers name,he would not expose this lie. She had won.

She didn't even wait for him. She stepped forward and, with a theatrical flourish, whipped the velvet cloth off the tray.

Nestled on the silver were two signature red boxes from Cartier. They were open, revealing a pair of stunning,diamond-encrusted wedding bands.

The room erupted. The applause was deafening,mixed with whoops and cheers.

Allena turned back to August, her eyes shining with fake,crocodile tears.

"August, darling," she cooed, her voice amplified by the microphone she was still near. "Since you're being so shy, I guess it's up to me to be the modern woman."

She gathered the skirt of her gown. In front of hundreds of people, she was about to get down on one knee and propose to a married man.
A wave of dizziness washed over Elisa. The cheering of the crowd dissolved into a high-pitched ringing in her ears, a wall ofwhite noise.

That was it. Enough.
She pushed her chair back abruptly, the legs scraping loudly against the polished floor. The sound was lost in the general din, but it was a declaration of war to herself.

She grabbed her clutch and turned, walking quickly toward the large double doors at the side of the hall. She had to get out. She couldn't breathe in this room filled with lies and poison.

From the corner of her eye, Allena saw Elisa's retreat. A cruel, triumphant smile spread across her face. She had broken her. She had finally,completely broken her.

Elisa's pace quickened, her heels sinking into the thick carpet. She was almost running now,desperate for the exit, for air, for escape.
Her hand reached for the cold brass handle of the door.

Just as her fingers were about to close around it,another hand shot out from the side. A large,masculine hand, a Patek Philippe watch gleaming
on the wrist, pressed flat against the wooden door panel.

Thump.
The door was held shut.

Elisa stopped, startled. She turned her head and found herself looking into a pair of eyes as deep and dark as the ocean.

L. Thorne stood there, dressed in an immaculate three-piece suit, his body an immovable wall between her and the exit.

"Running away is not your style, Faye," he said.His voice was low, yet it cut through the noise of the ballroom with perfect clarity.

"Get out of my way, Thorne," she said, her voice trembling with suppressed rage and humiliation."I'm not in the mood for games."

He didn't move. He took a step closer, trapping her in the small space between his body and the door.

"I'm not playing a game," he said, his eyes narrowing, becoming sharp and dangerous. "I'm here to end this farce."
###Chapter 277

Thorne removed his hand from the door. In a single,fluid motion, he took Elisa's cold,trembling hand in his. His grip was firm, resolute, and offered no room for argument. He turned her around to face the ballroom.

He led her away from the shadows of the exit and directly toward the center of the room,toward the head table,toward the spectacle.

The crowd, sensing a new and more powerful player had entered the game, instinctively parted for them, creating a clear path.

Allena, who had just begun to lower herself to one knee, saw them coming. Her movement faltered,her body freezing in an awkward, half-crouched position.

Thorne didn't stop until he was standing directly in front of the manager holding the silver tray. He glanced down at the Cartier rings, his expression one of profound disdain.

"Poor taste," he said. His voice, low and dismissive,was picked up by the nearby microphone and broadcast throughout the entire, now silent,
ballroom.
The air went still. Allena's face, which had been flushed with triumph, turned a pasty,sickly white.

Before anyone could process what was happening,Thorne reached into the inner pocket of his suit jacket and produced a small, square box covered in dark blue velvet.

He turned to face Elisa. And in front of August, in front of Allena, in front of the entire international business community, L. Thorne dropped gracefully to one knee.

He opened the box.

A gasp, sharp and collective, swept through the room. Inside, nestled on a bed of white satin,was a pink diamond. It was flawless, easily over ten carats, and it captured the light from the chandeliers, exploding into a thousand points of blinding,brilliant fire.

"Elisa" Thorne said, his voice deep and resonant,his eyes locked on hers. He was performing, but the intensity was utterly convincing. "I'm done waiting."

"Marry me. Let me be the one to protect you,always."
The room didn't just buzz. it aetonaleu.

L. Thorne! The reclusive, untouchable billionaire,head of a dynasty that dwarfed the Chambers empire, was proposing. And he was proposing to Aethel's mysterious chief architect.

Elisa was stunned into silence. Her mind went blank. This was his plan? This was how he intended to end the farce? By escalating it into a nuclear event?

She looked down at him, at the hard, determined set of his jaw, and she read the message in his eyes.Play along.

Allena was forgotten. She was a statue, a clown frozen mid-act, the now-cheap-looking Cartier rings still in her hand.

August shot to his feet, knocking his chair over with a loud crash. His face was a mask of disbelief and pure, possessive fury. His gray eyes were fixed on Thorne, burning with a hatred so intense it was almost visible.

In that frozen moment of tension, Alastair, Aethel's CEO,burst from the crowd.

"My God! Thorne, you kept this quiet!" he yelled,his voice booming with feigned shock and delight.
He began to applaud, loud and enthusiasucany."Say yes,Elisa! Say yes!"

The Aethel executive team, understanding their cue, immediately joined in. The sound of their cheers and applause was a tidal wave, completely overwhelming the dying embers of Allena's moment. The narrative had been hijacked. The stage she had so carefully built was now the backdrop for a far grander show.

"It's fake! They're not together!" Allena shrieked,her voice thin and hysterical.

But no one was listening. Her voice was swallowed by the roar of the crowd. She was irrelevant.

Elisa took a deep breath. This was it. This was the perfect weapon. A way to humiliate Allena and August so completely, so publicly, that they would never recover.①

She slowly, deliberately, extended her left hand.
"Yes," she said, her voice clear and steady, ringing through the microphone."I will."

A slow, victorious smile spread across Thorne's lips. He took the priceless pink diamond and slid it,with perfect precision, onto Elisa's ring finger.
###Chapter 278


The pink diamond on Elisa's finger was a supernova. It caught the light and threw it back,a blinding flash that seemed to burn itself directly onto August's retinas.

Thorne rose to his feet. He placed a possessive hand on Elisa's waist, drawing her to his side as he accepted the congratulations of the stunned but ecstatic crowd.

August could not tolerate it. He shoved a chair out of his way and strode across the few feet of carpet that separated them, his body radiating a palpable,violent rage.

The celebratory noise died down as people sensed the impending confrontation.

"What do you think you're doing, Elisa?" August's voice was a low, dangerous growl, each word forced through clenched teeth. His eyes were locked on the ring, a look of furious, betrayed disbelief on his face.

"Do you have any idea what you've just done?Have you forgotten who you are?" he hissed, his words a veiled threat, a reminder of her still-legal status as
his wife.
Elisa met his gaze without flinching. A small, cold smile of victory played on her lips.

"My identity?" she asked, her voice cool and clear."I am Elisa. And now, I am Mr. Thorne's fiancée."

She deliberately lifted her left hand, pretending to tuck a stray strand of hair behind her ear. The movement was slow, calculated, designed to make the massive diamond flash directly in his line of sight.

"As for any other unpleasant, outdated identities,"she continued, her voice dropping so only the three of them could hear, "I'm sure the judge's final order will sweep them into the trash where they belong."

August's face turned a dark, mottled red. A vein pulsed in his temple. He lunged, his hand shooting out to grab her wrist, to rip the ring from her finger.

Before he could make contact, Thorne's hand shot out, clamping around August's forearm like a vice.The strength in his grip was shocking.

"Mr. Chambers," Thorne's voice was quiet, but it carried an absolute, non-negotiable threat. "Show some respect. To my fiancée."
The two men stood frozen, a tableau of raw,
masculine aggression in the center of the ballroom.The air crackled with tension.

The standoff was broken by Alastair, who ambled over with two glasses of champagne, his face a mask of cheerful innocence.

"August, my friend, don't be so dramatic," he said,pressing one of the glasses into August's rigid hand. "I know you're upset that our Elisa stole the show from your... what is she? A medical consultant?"

Alastair loaded the words "medical consultant" with so much sarcasm it was practically dripping. He was tearing Allena's carefully constructed identity to shreds in front of everyone.

Allena, who had been standing nearby in a state of shock, flinched. "Alastair! You go too far!"

Alastair put a hand to his chest in mock horror. "Oh,I am so sorry, Miss Mills. I completely forgot. You were about to propose yourself, weren't you? What a shame." He let out a theatrical sigh. "Well, don't worry.I hear Cartier has an excellent return policy.Just make sure you have the receipt."

A few stifled snorts of laughter broke out from the nearby tables. Allena's face went from pale to crimson. Humiliating tears welled in her eyes.
The public mockery was the final straw for August.He slammed the champagne flute down on a nearby table with a crash.

"Enough," he snarled. "We're leaving."He threw the words at Allena without looking at her, then turned and stalked toward the ballroom doors, his back rigid with defeat.

He didn't wait for her. He practically fled, his retreat a clear admission of his utter humiliation.

Allena shot Elisa a look of pure, venomous hatred,then gathered her ridiculous dress and scurried after him.

Elisa watched them go, the tension finally draining from her shoulders. She let out a long, slow breath.

"Well done, Elisa," Alastair said, raising his glass to her in a toast.

Thorne looked down at her, a rare hint of approval in his dark eyes. "This was only the opening move,"he said quietly. "Are you ready for the war?"
###Chapter 279


With August and Allena's ignominious exit, the party's atmosphere surged back to life. Guests swarmed Elisa and Thorne, offering congratulations, their faces alight with the thrill of having witnessed such a high-stakes corporate drama.

Elisa maintained a flawless, professional smile,accepting handshakes and murmuring polite thank-yous.She played the part of the triumphant fiancée perfectly.

But Thorne, standing close beside her, could feel the fine tremor in the hand she kept hidden in the folds of her dress.

The confrontation with August had taken a toll. The look in his eyes when he'd stared at her-it wasn't just anger. It was something darker, more destructive.

"He looked at me," she murmured to Thorne, her voice so low it was lost in the noise of the room,"like he'd never seen me before. Like I was a complete stranger he wanted to destroy."
Thorne subtly took the champagne flute from her hand and replaced it with a glass of water from a passing waiter's tray. "That is the predictable reaction of a beast when it realizes its prey is no longer in its cage," he said, his voice a calm anchor in the chaos.

He leaned in closer, his lips near her ear,the gesture appearing intimate to any onlookers. "You did what needed to be done, Elisa. Now you need to rest."

Thorne turned to the crowd, offered a polite but firm apology, and citing his fiancée's fatigue, began to steer Elisa out of the ballroom.

They walked down the long, quiet corridor toward the elevators, the thick wool carpet swallowing the sound of their footsteps. The noise of the party faded behind them.

Suddenly, a flash of red burst from around a corner,blocking their path.

It was Allena. She hadn't left with August.She had been lying in wait.

Her makeup was smudged from crying, and her eyes were wild, glittering with a desperate,unhinged malice.
"You bitch!" she shrieked, pointing a trembling finger at Elisa. "Are you happy now?"
"You deliberately seduce Mr. Thorne just to humiliate me! That's all you know how to do, isn't it? Fight dirty, like the trash you are!"

Thorne's expression turned to ice. He took a step forward, ready to have security physically remove her, but Elisa put a hand on his arm, stopping him.

She stepped forward herself, her gaze cold and analytical as she took in Allena's pathetic,crumbling state.

"Humiliate you?" Elisa's voice was devoid of all emotion. "You give yourself too much credit.You served your own face up on a platter.I just swung."

She took another step closer. "Did you really think a birthday cake could force a man like August Chambers to marry you? You don't know him at all.He's a narcissist. He would have let you humiliate yourself and then cut you loose the moment it became inconvenient."

"Shut up! What do you know?" Allena screamed,stung by the truth.

She tried to pull herself together, to salvage one last shred of dignity. "You think that ring means
you've won? August already promised me! We're going to have the biggest wedding New York has ever seen! And you? You'll always be the used,discarded trash he got tired of!"

Her hysterical voice echoed in the empty corridor.

Elisa just looked at her. There was no anger in her eyes, only a profound, detached pity, the kind one might feel for a bug twitching on a pin.

"ls that so?" Elisa's voice was terrifyingly calm."Then I wish you both a lifetime of happiness. A bitch and a dog. A match made in heaven."

She didn't wait for a response. She turned to Thorne. "Let's go. The air in this hallway has become contaminated."

Thorne gave Allena one last, dismissive glance,as if looking at something he'd scraped off the bottom of his shoe. He then placed a guiding hand on Elisa's back and they continued toward the elevators.

Allena stood frozen, watching them go. She was trembling with a rage so intense it made her teeth chatter. Her manicured nails dug into her palms,drawing blood.

She ripped her phone from her clutch, her
movements jerky and violent. She found a number in her contacts and pressed call.

"It's me," she hissed into the phone when the CEO of Apex Therapeutics answered. "I want to move up the timeline on the Aethel recruiment plan. I want every key member of their R&D team. I want you to gut that company. I want to leave that bitch Elisa Wilder with nothing."
###Chapter 280


Elisa and Thorne reached the VIP elevator bank.Thorne pressed the call button, and a soft chime announced the car's arrival.
The polished steel doors slid open.

The elevator was not empty. August was inside,leaning against the mirrored back wall, his hands shoved deep in his tuxedo pockets. His eyes were dark and stormy, and he was staring directly at them.

He hadn't left. He had sent Allena away and come back to wait for her.

Elisa's body tensed for a fraction of a second,but she quickly masked it, her expression returning to one of cool indifference. She met his stare head-on.

Thorne moved instinctively, positioning himself slightly in front of her, a human shield. His gaze on August was cold steel.

"This is a private matter, Thorne. Between me and my wife," August said, his voice dangerously low,each word a command.
Thorne didn't budge. "She is my fiancée now,Chambers. Any issues you have can be directed to my legal team."

Elisa placed a light hand on Thorne's arm. "It's alright, L.," she said quietly. "I can handle this. I'll meet you upstairs."

Thorne studied her face, searching for any sign of fear or hesitation. Seeing only resolve, he gave a curt nod and stepped back, ceding the space.

Elisa walked into the elevator alone. The doors slid shut, closing her in with August, their reflections staring back at them in the polished metal.

The car began its silent ascent. The small,enclosed space felt instantly suffocating, the air thick with unspoken violence.

August pushed himself off the wall. He advanced on her, step by step, until she was backed into a corner. His height and size were overwhelming,a physical manifestation of his oppressive presence.

"What is this game you're playing?" he demanded,his voice a low snarl. "L. Thorne? When did you start sleeping with him?"

A humorless smile touched Elisa's lips. She tilted her chin up, refusing to be intimidated. "That's
none of your business. The contract between us is over."

"Over?" His laugh was a harsh, ugly sound."Until that divorce decree is in your hands, you are mine.You live as my wife, you die as my wife." His possessiveness, raw and absolute,filled the small space.

"That's pathetic," she shot back, her voice cutting."You didn't seem to remember you had a wife when you were letting your mistress try to force a proposal out of you in front of five hundred people."

He flinched, the truth of her words hitting him like a physical blow. A flicker of shame crossed his face before it was consumed by a deeper, darker anger.

"That was a strategic move to keep Allena manageable! But you... you actually put his ring on your finger!"

His hand shot out, grabbing for her left hand, his intention to rip the pink diamond from her finger.
Elisa reacted instantly, slapping his hand away with a sharp, violent motion. "Don't touch me! You make me sick."

A red mark appeared on the back of his hand.He stared at her, his eyes narrowing into dangerous
slits.A cruel, knowing smirk twisted his lips.

"You think he loves you? You think he can protect you?" he sneered. "Don't be naive. The Thatchers have been circling Thorne's family for months,trying to lock down a marriage contract. All of Europe is watching. My people saw Cyprian Thatcher himself having a very private dinner with Thorne's uncle just last night. You're a pawn, Elisa,a temporary piece he's moving on the board to counter me."

He leaned in, his voice dropping to a venomous whisper. "He's using you, Elisa. You're a shield.A temporary distraction. And when the Thatcher family decides to remove you from the board,you'll be crushed. You'll disappear without a trace."

Elisa's heart gave a painful lurch. She kept her face a mask of stone, but his words landed like shards of ice in her gut. "That's my choice to make," she said, her voice steady. "Better to be a shield for a powerful man than to drown in the filth of your family."

Ding.
The elevator arrived at her floor. The doors slid open.

She shoved past him, stepping out into the hallway.
Just as the doors began to close, her phone, inside her clutch, began to vibrate. She glanced at the screen, expecting a message from Alastair. Instead,an unknown New York number was calling. A cold knot of dread tightened in her stomach. Her grandfather was the only reason a New York number would be calling. She swiped to answer,her hand trembling slightly.

"Is this Elisa Wilder?" a harried, unfamiliar voice asked, the background noise a chaotic mix of beeping machines and urgent voices. "This is Dr.Evans from New York-Presbyterian. I'm calling about your grandfather, Phineas Wilder. There's been a critical emergency in his ICU ward,and the Chambers family just issued orders to halt his experimental treatments."

The color drained from Elisa's face. The world tilted on its axis.
###Chapter 281

The doctor's words hit Elisa like a physical blow.
"...critical emergency...Chambers family just issued orders to halt his experimental treatments."

The air in her lungs turned to ice. She couldn't draw a breath. Her fingers, gripping the small clutch,went numb. The plush, patterned carpet of the hotel hallway seemned to tilt beneath her feet.
Grandfather.

The one person in the world who had ever shown her kindness. The reason she survived.

Behind her, the elevator doors finally slid shut with a soft, final thud. They had been delayed by a large hand forcing them back open just moments before.August hadn't stayed inside. The second he saw her freeze at the sight of the unknown caller ID,his instincts had propelled him out into the hallway.The sound of his expensive leather shoes sinking into the thick wool carpet was almost silent, but she felt his presence behind her, a wave of cold fury radiating from him. He had been standing right there, watching her for the entirety of her brief,devastating call.
He saw her rigidback, the phone still pressed to her ear though the line was dead. He saw the way her shoulders were locked, a statue of tension.

He completely misinterpreted it. A cruel, satisfied smirk touched the corner of his mouth. He thought she was terrified of him. Of his warning about the Thatchers.

"You should be scared, Elisa," his voice was a low growl, inches from her ear. "You think Thorne can protect you from a family like the Thatchers? You're a temporary shield. A toy."

Elisa's mind was a screaming void. She couldn't process his words. All she could see was her grandfather's face, frail and smiling, in his small West Virginia home.

"And you'd better remember." August continued,stepping around to stand in front of her, blocking her path, his sheer size an oppressive wall. "Until our divorce is finalized, you are still bound by the Chambers prenuptial agreement. Every clause."

The raw threat finally pierced the fog of her panic.Her survival instincts kicked in. She couldn't let him see her weakness. Not now. He would use it. He would use her grandfather.
With a jerky, mechanical movement, she lowered the phone, locked the screen, and shoved it deep into her clutch. The knuckles of her hand were white.

She lifted her head, her eyes, which a moment ago were wide with terror, now narrowed into chips of ice.

The tension between them was a physical thing, a high-frequency hum in the air.

Just as he raised a hand, pointing a finger at her chest, another voice cut through the silence.
"That's enough,Chambers."

The voice wasn't loud, but it had the sharp, cutting
authority of a blade.
Both Elisa and August turned.

Thorne was walking toward them from the far end of the corridor. He moved with a calm,unhurried grace that was somehow more menacing than August's overt aggression. He didn't stop until he was standing beside Elisa.He ignored August completely.

August's eyes dropped to Thorne's hand on Elisa's waist. The air in his lungs seemed to freeze. A dark,possessive fury, primal and ugly, contorted his features. The vein in his temple began to pulse.

her body finally obeying ner m111u 1010commands.

But the sudden movement, combined with the adrenaline crash and the ten-centimeter heels she wore, was a disastrous combination. The edge of her shoe caught on the polished marble inlay that bordered the carpet.

Her ankle twisted violently.

The abrupt, unnatural torque of her lower body sent a terrifying jolt straight up her spine. A sharp,agonized cry escaped her lips-not just for her ankle, but as her damaged L4 and L5 vertebrae screamed in violent protest. She lost her balance,her body lurching backward, sideways, into the empty space between the two men.

"Careful!" Thorne's hand shot out to grab her,but he was a half-step too far away. His fingers brushed against the sleeve of her dress, missing their mark.

August didn't think. He didn't plan. He reacted.

His body moved with an instinct he thought he had buried long ago. His arm shot out, a powerful,reflexive motion. He wrapped it securely around her waist,pulling her back from the brink.
Elisa slammed into his chest.

The impact was hard, knocking the last of the air from her lungs. More devastatingly, the violent whiplash of the collision tore through her lower back like a jagged blade. A blinding,paralyzing spike of agony radiated from her L4 and L5 discs,causing her vision to white out at the edges. Her entire respiratory system seemed to shut down as the horrific nerve pain hijacked her senses. His arm was a band of steel around her, holding her tight. He looked down, his gray-blue eyes wide,staring at her face, which was just inches from his.A flicker of something-not anger, but raw,unguarded panic-flashed in their depths. He had caught her. He was holding her.
It was a moment of pure, animal instinct, a crack in the carefully constructed walls between them.

And it was in that exact moment of charged,impossible stillness that a new sound echoed down the hallway. The frantic, hurried click-clack of high heels on marble.
Allena, her face a mask of tear-streaked fury,came running around the corner. She had clearly been chasing after August, unwilling to accept her defeat.

She stopped dead, her breath catching in a strangled gasp.

She saw them. August, with his arms wrapped tightly around Elisa, holding her in a possessive,intimate embrace.

"August!"

"What are you DOING?"

Her legs nearly gave out under the spasm. Gasping for air through clenched teeth, she awkwardly stumbled back,desperate to pull away from his touch, away from the suffocating familiarity of his body.
Thorne was there instantly, his strong arm wrapping securely around her ribs, physically taking the brunt of her weight as she swayed.

"I was stopping her from breaking her neck on my hotel's floor," he said, his voice dripping with condescending venom. "It would have ruined the carpet."

Allena wasn't listening. She rushed forward and latched onto August's arm, clinging to him like a drowning woman, her eyes, filled with hate, fixed on Elisa.

Elisa didn't even grant them a glance. Her world had narrowed to a single, desperate point of grief and physical agony. She grabbed Thorne's arm, her fingers digging into the expensive fabric of his suit to keepherself upright.
"L, please," her voice was a raw whisper, threaded with a tremor she couldn't control. "You have to help me. I have to get back to New York. Right now."
Thorne saw the genuine terror and the Staik, uaieiy concealed pain in her eyes. He didn't ask questions. He didn't hesitate.

"I'll handle it," he said, his voice a low, reassuring promise. He wrapped a supportive arm around her shoulders**, bearing nearly half of her body weight,** and began guiding her toward the private elevator at the end of the hall.

"Chambers," he said, his voice soft but carrying the unmistakable weight of a warning. "Back off. She's made her choice."

August's jaw tightened. His hands balled into fists at his sides. But before he could respond, Elisa tugged at Thorne's arm, her patience exhausted.

"L.Now."

She pushed past August without looking at him, heavily leaning against Thorne's side to take the punishing pressure off her lower back. step, she didn't stop. She didn't look back.

He had threatened her. He had tried to break her.

And in the end, he had only pushed her further into the arms of another man.
###Chapter 282


The heavy brass doors of the private elevator slid shut, sealing Elisa and Thorne in a world of polished mahogany and quiet efficiency. Thorne maintained his firm grip around her waist, taking the brunt of her weight as her injured ankle throbbed with a sharp, fiery pain. The suffocating drama of the hallway was gone, replaced by a tense,humming silence.

August, left in the hallway, roughly shook Allena's hand off his arm. His face was a thundercloud of frustration and a confusion he couldn't name. His gaze was fixed on the closed elevator doors,his mind replaying the image of Elisa in Thorne's
protection.

The explanation was smooth. Plausible. It allowed her to breathe a little easier. She didn't have the energy for emotional complications. She had a war to fight, a grandfather to save.
But August's words from the other elevator still echoed in her mind. The Thatchers have been circling...you're a shield.

Thorne's hand, adjusting his cufflink, paused for a fraction of a second. It was the only sign that her question had hit its mark. He finally turned to look at her, his dark eyes unreadable.

He didn't deny it.

"Everyone in my position has complications that need to be managed," he said, his voice a low baritone. It was a careful, political answer. "But that doesn't change my primary objective. Protecting you and Kayden. That is a separate and non-negotiable priority."

The name Kayden was a key, unlocking the tension in her shoulders. For her daughter, she could endure being a pawn in his games. For Kayden, she would make a deal with the devil himself. This was a partnership, a transaction. She could handle that.
The elevator chimed softly, arriving at the
penthouse level. The doors opened onto a private foyer. "Keep your weight off that foot," Thorne instructed quietly, practically carrying her into a sprawling, minimalist suite, all clean lines, dark wood,and floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the glittering lake. It was a world away from the gaudy opulence of the Chambers' properties. It was a fortress.

Elisa sank onto a low, cream-colored sofa, wincing softly as she carefully extended her swollen ankle. The exhaustion hitting her like a physical weight. She pulled the massive pink diamond from her finger. The stone felt heavy, alien. She placed it carefully on the glass coffee table.

"I can't keep this," she said. "It's too much. It's not part of the deal."

Thorne walked to the wet bar and poured a glass of still water, then retrieved an ice pack from the mini-fridge. He brought them to her, his movements economical and precise. He knelt to place the ice pack gently over her injured ankle before placing the glass in her hand.

"You are, for all public purposes, my fiancée," he said, standing back up. "The ring is a necessary part of the uniform.You will wear it."
"But it's not real," she insisted, pushing the ring toward him with her index finger. "It's a performance. I can't give you a real marriage,Thorne. I can't give you any kind of promise like that." She had to draw the line, for her own sanity.

He looked down at her, his tall frame casting a shadow over the sofa. His expression was serious,the kind of intensity she'd only seen him use when discussing billion-dollar mergers.

"My commitment is to Kayden," he said, his voice quiet but firm. "She deserves the illusion of a stable, powerful family unit to protect her from the chaos of yours. That is what this ring represents."

His focus on Kayden disarmed her. It always did. He was using her daughter as his best and most effective weapon against her defenses. She felt a flicker of gratitude, warring with her suspicion.

He seemed to sense her hesitation. He leaned down slightly, resting one hand on the back of the sofa,his face now level with hers.

"However," he said, his voice dropping lower,becoming a soft, magnetic murmur that sent an unexpected shiver down her spine. "Should you ever decide you want something real... a real partnership, a real marriage... my offer stands.I am ready whenever you are."
The words hung in the air between them. It wasn't a demand. It was an open door. An option, sitting there, waiting. It stole the air from her lungs,leaving her speechless.

Just then, a sharp, polite knock sounded at the suite door.

The moment was broken. Reality rushed back in.

Thorne straightened up, his expression once again becoming impassive as he went to the door. It was his executive assistant, a sharp young man in a slim-fit suit, holding a tablet.

"The flight plan is filed, sir. Wheels up in ninety minutes. The car is waiting downstairs."

Elisa gripped the armrest of the sofa, relying entirely on her good leg to push herself upright,suppressing a groan as a jolt of pain shot up her calf. The spell broken. Her mind was clear again.New York. Grandfather. That was all that mattered.

The assistant's eyes flickered from the massive diamond on the coffee table to Elisa, then to his boss. A knowing, slightly amused smile touched his lips. Thorne shot him a look that could freeze fire,and the smile vanished instantly.

"It's just a business arrangement," Elisa said
quickly, feeling a ridiculous need to explain, to dispel the intimacy the assistant had clearly sensed.

The assistant just nodded politely. "Of course, Ms.Wilder." He handed her a sealed envelope with her travel documents.

Elisa knew she couldn't risk being seen leaving with Thorne. August was unhinged. He would have people watching.

"I'll go to the airport alone," she said, her voice firm."It's safer."

Thorne didn't argue, though his gaze flicked downward in concern. "You can barely walk," he pointed out. "I'll have my driver escort you and arrange a wheelchair at the terminal." He understood the tactical advantage of her moving unpredictably. "I'll finish our business here in Geneva.I will see you in New York."

He gave a slight, formal nod and walked out of the suite, leaving her alone with the silence and the glittering pink diamond.

The moment the door clicked shut, she pulled out her phone. She looked at the text from the hospital again, the words "critical" and "declining" burning into her retinas. Her heart seized in her chest, a
cold,tight fist of fear.

She knew what was waiting for her in New York.The full, unrestrained wrath of the Chambers family, and a life hanging in the balance.

Her hand closed around the pink diamond on the table. It was cold and hard, a weapon. She slid it back onto her ring finger. It felt like armor.
Taking a deep breath, Elisa picked up her small travel bag and slung it over her shoulder. Gritting her teeth against the throbbing pain, she leaned heavily on the assistant who had returned to the doorway to aid her. Favoring her good leg,she limped out into the night, ready for the storm.
###Chapter 283


The Gulfstream G650 cut silently through the stratosphere, a silver needle stitching a path across the dark expanse of the Atlantic. She saw the blinking, erratic lines of an ICU monitor.Dr.Evans's frantic voice from the earlier phone call still echoed in her ears.

Across the polished wood table, Alastair Cromwell,the CEO of Aethel Intelligence, swirled the amber liquid in his glass. He had insisted on flying back with her, a silent, supportive presence.
"Are you sure you're okay, Faye?" he asked,his usual flamboyant energy subdued. "That performance with Thorne... it was brilliant, but even for you, that was intense. His eyes when he looked at you... that wasn't acting."

mind, a distraction she couldn't afford.

"Elisa."
